meta.overview: Luke 1 opens the Third Gospel with a literary prologue unique among the four Gospels (vv.1-4), addressed to 'most excellent Theophilus' and establishing Luke's method: careful historical research, eyewitness consultation, and orderly presentation. The chapter then unfolds through two dramatic angelic announcements set in the world of Second Temple Judaism. Gabriel appears to the aged priest Zacharias at the golden altar of incense in the Jerusalem temple (vv.5-25), announcing that his barren wife Elisabeth will bear a son who will go before the Messiah 'in the spirit and power of Elias' (v.17) — a Nazarite from the womb, filled with the Holy Ghost, turning Israel's hearts to the Lord. Zacharias's unbelief earns nine months of enforced silence (v.20). Six months later Gabriel appears to the virgin Mary in Nazareth (vv.26-38), announcing the virginal conception of the Son of the Highest, heir to David's eternal throne, whose kingdom shall have no end (vv.32-33). Mary's humble submission — 'Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word' (v.38) — stands in contrast to Zacharias's hesitation. Mary's visit to Elisabeth (vv.39-56) produces the leap of the unborn John in the womb — prenatal recognition of his Lord — and Elisabeth's Spirit-filled blessing (vv.41-45). Mary responds with the Magnificat (vv.46-55), a Hebrew poetic canticle celebrating God's sovereign mercy, his scattering of the proud, his exaltation of the lowly, and his faithfulness to the Abrahamic covenant. John's birth and circumcision (vv.57-66) occasion Zacharias's restored speech and the wonder of all Judaea. The Benedictus (vv.67-80), sung by the Spirit-filled Zacharias, proclaims the dawning of messianic redemption — salvation from enemies, remission of sins, the 'dayspring from on high' visiting those in darkness — and sets the trajectory of the entire Gospel narrative.
meta.theme: The Sovereign Initiative of God — Divine Announcements of the Forerunner and the Saviour
meta.keyWordMeaning: To proclaim good news, to announce the gospel — from eu (good) + angelos (messenger/message). The verb appears in v.19 when Gabriel declares to Zacharias that he has been sent to 'shew thee these glad tidings' (euangelisasthai). Luke uses this verb more than any other New Testament writer (10x in Luke, 15x in Acts). The entire chapter is a cascade of good news: a barren woman will conceive, a virgin will bear the Son of God, the long-awaited Messiah comes at last. Every announcement is an act of divine euangelismos — God speaking good news into situations of impossibility.
outline: Prologue — Luke's Method and Purpose (vv.1-4): Forasmuch as many have taken in hand to set forth in order a declaration of those things which are most surely believed among us even as they delivered them unto us which from the beginning were eyewitnesses and ministers of the word. It seemed good to me also having had perfect understanding of all things from the very first to write unto thee in order most excellent Theophilus that thou mightest know the certainty of those things wherein thou hast been instructed

Announcement to Zacharias — A Son Named John (vv.5-25): There was in the days of Herod the king of Judaea a certain priest named Zacharias of the course of Abia and his wife was of the daughters of Aaron and her name was Elisabeth. They were both righteous before God walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord blameless. They had no child because Elisabeth was barren and they both were now well stricken in years. While Zacharias executed the priest's office his lot was to burn incense. An angel of the Lord appeared standing on the right side of the altar of incense. He was troubled and fear fell upon him. The angel said — Fear not Zacharias for thy prayer is heard and thy wife Elisabeth shall bear thee a son and thou shalt call his name John. He shall be great in the sight of the Lord filled with the Holy Ghost even from his mother's womb. He shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias to make ready a people prepared for the Lord. Zacharias asked — Whereby shall I know this? Gabriel answered — I am Gabriel that stand in the presence of God. Behold thou shalt be dumb until the day these things are performed. Elisabeth conceived and hid herself five months saying — Thus hath the Lord dealt with me to take away my reproach among men

Announcement to Mary — The Son of the Highest (vv.26-38): In the sixth month the angel Gabriel was sent from God unto a city of Galilee named Nazareth to a virgin espoused to a man whose name was Joseph of the house of David and the virgin's name was Mary. The angel said — Hail thou that art highly favoured the Lord is with thee. She was troubled and cast in her mind what manner of salutation this should be. Fear not Mary for thou hast found favour with God. Behold thou shalt conceive and bring forth a son and shalt call his name JESUS. He shall be great and shall be called the Son of the Highest and the Lord God shall give unto him the throne of his father David and he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever and of his kingdom there shall be no end. Mary said — How shall this be seeing I know not a man? The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee therefore also that holy thing which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God. For with God nothing shall be impossible. Mary said — Behold the handmaid of the Lord be it unto me according to thy word

The Visitation and the Magnificat (vv.39-56): Mary arose and went into the hill country with haste into a city of Juda and entered into the house of Zacharias. When Elisabeth heard the salutation of Mary the babe leaped in her womb and Elisabeth was filled with the Holy Ghost. She spake out with a loud voice — Blessed art thou among women and blessed is the fruit of thy womb. And whence is this to me that the mother of my Lord should come to me? Blessed is she that believed for there shall be a performance of those things told her from the Lord. Mary's Magnificat: My soul doth magnify the Lord and my spirit hath rejoiced in God my Saviour. He hath regarded the low estate of his handmaiden. He that is mighty hath done to me great things and holy is his name. His mercy is on them that fear him from generation to generation. He hath scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts. He hath put down the mighty from their seats and exalted them of low degree. He hath filled the hungry with good things and the rich he hath sent empty away. He hath holpen his servant Israel in remembrance of his mercy as he spake to our fathers to Abraham and to his seed for ever. Mary abode with her about three months and returned to her own house

The Birth and Naming of John (vv.57-66): Elisabeth's full time came and she brought forth a son. Her neighbours and cousins heard how the Lord had shewed great mercy upon her and they rejoiced. On the eighth day they came to circumcise the child and called him Zacharias after his father. His mother said — Not so but he shall be called John. They said — There is none of thy kindred called by this name. They made signs to his father. He asked for a writing table and wrote — His name is John. And they marvelled all. His mouth was opened immediately and his tongue loosed and he spake and praised God. Fear came on all and all these sayings were noised abroad throughout all the hill country of Judaea saying — What manner of child shall this be

The Benedictus of Zacharias (vv.67-80): Zacharias was filled with the Holy Ghost and prophesied. Blessed be the Lord God of Israel for he hath visited and redeemed his people and hath raised up an horn of salvation in the house of his servant David. As he spake by his holy prophets since the world began — salvation from our enemies to perform the mercy promised to our fathers and to remember his holy covenant the oath sworn to Abraham. That we being delivered might serve him in holiness and righteousness all our days. And thou child shalt be called the prophet of the Highest for thou shalt go before the face of the Lord to prepare his ways — to give knowledge of salvation by the remission of sins through the tender mercy of our God whereby the dayspring from on high hath visited us to give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death to guide our feet into the way of peace. The child grew and waxed strong in spirit and was in the deserts till the day of his shewing unto Israel

verses.kjv_text: Forasmuch as many have taken in hand to set forth in order a declaration of those things which are most surely believed among us,
verses.original_text: Ἐπειδήπερ πολλοὶ ἐπεχείρησαν ἀνατάξασθαι διήγησιν περὶ τῶν πεπληροφορημένων ἐν ἡμῖν πραγμάτων
verses.transliteration: Epeidēper polloi epecheirēsan anataxasthai diēgēsin peri tōn peplērophorēmenōn en hēmin pragmatōn
verses.study_note: Luke's prologue (vv.1-4) is the most polished piece of classical Greek prose in the New Testament, modelled on the prologues of Hellenistic historians such as Thucydides and Josephus. 'Many have taken in hand' (polloi epecheirēsan) — Luke acknowledges prior Gospel accounts without disparaging them. 'Most surely believed' (peplērophorēmenōn — perfect passive participle of plērophorō, fully established/fulfilled). Luke is writing about historical events that have been accomplished (fulfilled) and are therefore firmly believed in the Christian community. This is not legend but verified testimony.

verses.kjv_text: Even as they delivered them unto us, which from the beginning were eyewitnesses, and ministers of the word;
verses.original_text: καθὼς παρέδοσαν ἡμῖν οἱ ἀπ᾽ ἀρχῆς αὐτόπται καὶ ὑπηρέται γενόμενοι τοῦ λόγου
verses.transliteration: kathōs paredosan hēmin hoi ap archēs autoptai kai hypēretai genomenoi tou logou
verses.study_note: 'Delivered' (paredosan — handed over, transmitted; the same word for oral tradition, paradosis). 'Eyewitnesses' (autoptai — from autos + optō, those who saw with their own eyes; this word is unique in the NT). 'Ministers of the word' (hypēretai tou logou — servants/assistants of the message). Luke distinguishes between the original eyewitnesses (the apostles and those present from the beginning) and those like himself who received the tradition from them. This places the Gospel narrative on the foundation of eyewitness testimony, not invented myth.

verses.kjv_text: It seemed good to me also, having had perfect understanding of all things from the very first, to write unto thee in order, most excellent Theophilus,
verses.original_text: ἔδοξε κἀμοί παρηκολουθηκότι ἄνωθεν πᾶσιν ἀκριβῶς καθεξῆς σοι γράψαι κράτιστε Θεόφιλε
verses.transliteration: edoxe kamoi parēkolouthēkoti anōthen pasin akribōs kathexēs soi grapsai kratiste Theophile
verses.study_note: 'Having had perfect understanding' (parēkolouthēkoti — perfect active participle of parakoloutheō, to follow alongside carefully, to trace accurately). 'From the very first' (anōthen — from above/from the beginning). 'In order' (kathexēs — sequentially, in orderly succession). 'Most excellent Theophilus' (kratiste Theophile — the title kratistos was used for Roman officials of equestrian rank, cf. Acts 23:26; 24:3; 26:25). Theophilus ('lover of God') was likely a real Roman patron who funded Luke's literary project. Luke's method is that of a careful ancient historian: interviewing sources, tracing events from their beginning, and writing in orderly sequence.

verses.kjv_text: That thou mightest know the certainty of those things, wherein thou hast been instructed.
verses.original_text: ἵνα ἐπιγνῷς περὶ ὧν κατηχήθης λόγων τὴν ἀσφάλειαν
verses.transliteration: hina epignōs peri hōn katēchēthēs logōn tēn asphaleian
verses.study_note: 'That thou mightest know' (hina epignōs — so that you might fully know, recognise with certainty; epiginōskō intensifies ginōskō). 'Certainty' (asphaleian — from a-sphallō, that which cannot be overthrown or tripped; firmness, security, reliability). 'Hast been instructed' (katēchēthēs — taught orally, catechised; the root of our word catechism). Theophilus may have been a recent convert receiving basic Christian instruction; Luke writes to give him secure historical grounding for his faith. The purpose of the Gospel is not novelty but confirmation of what is already believed.

verses.kjv_text: There was in the days of Herod, the king of Judaea, a certain priest named Zacharias, of the course of Abia: and his wife was of the daughters of Aaron, and her name was Elisabeth.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις Ἡρῴδου τοῦ βασιλέως τῆς Ἰουδαίας ἱερεύς τις ὀνόματι Ζαχαρίας ἐξ ἐφημερίας Ἀβιά καὶ ἡ γυνὴ αὐτοῦ ἐκ τῶν θυγατέρων Ἀαρών καὶ τὸ ὄνομα αὐτῆς Ἐλισάβετ
verses.transliteration: Egeneto en tais hēmerais Hērōdou tou basileōs tēs Ioudaias hiereus tis onomati Zacharias ex ephēmerias Abia kai hē gynē autou ek tōn thygateron Aarōn kai to onoma autēs Elisabet
verses.study_note: The narrative shifts abruptly from polished Greek prologue to the world of the Jerusalem priesthood. 'In the days of Herod the king' (Herod the Great, died 4 BC) gives historical grounding. 'Course of Abia' (ephēmerias Abia — the priestly division of Abijah; 1 Chronicles 24:10 lists Abijah as the 8th of 24 priestly divisions). Each division served two weeks per year in the temple. 'Daughters of Aaron' — Elisabeth had double priestly lineage, being both married to a priest and herself of the Aaronic line. 'Zacharias' (Heb. Zechariah — the LORD remembers) and 'Elisabeth' (Heb. Elisheba — my God is an oath, the same name as Aaron's wife in Exodus 6:23) — Luke frames the story in deep OT continuity.

verses.kjv_text: And they were both righteous before God, walking in all the commandments and ordinances of the Lord blameless.
verses.original_text: ἦσαν δὲ δίκαιοι ἀμφότεροι ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ πορευόμενοι ἐν πάσαις ταῖς ἐντολαῖς καὶ δικαιώμασιν τοῦ κυρίου ἄμεμπτοι
verses.transliteration: ēsan de dikaioi amphoteroi enōpion tou theou poreuomenoi en pasais tais entolais kai dikaiōmasin tou kyriou amemptoi
verses.study_note: 'Righteous before God' (dikaioi enōpion tou theou — not just in human estimation but in God's sight; cf. Genesis 7:1; Job 1:1). 'Walking in all the commandments' (entolais — specific commandments) 'and ordinances' (dikaiōmasin — regulations, statutes, righteous requirements). 'Blameless' (amemptoi — without blame or reproach). This is not a claim to sinless perfection but to covenantal faithfulness — they lived conscientiously within the Mosaic covenant. Luke intentionally portrays Zacharias and Elisabeth in the pattern of OT saints like Abraham and Sarah, setting the scene for a miracle that echoes the patriarchal narratives.

verses.kjv_text: And they had no child, because that Elisabeth was barren, and they both were now well stricken in years.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐκ ἦν αὐτοῖς τέκνον καθότι ἦν ἡ Ἐλισάβετ στεῖρα καὶ ἀμφότεροι προβεβηκότες ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις αὐτῶν ἦσαν
verses.transliteration: kai ouk ēn autois teknon kathoti ēn hē Elisabet steira kai amphoteroi probebēkotes en tais hēmerais autōn ēsan
verses.study_note: 'No child' (ouk ēn autois teknon — they had no child). 'Barren' (steira — unable to bear children; the same word used of Sarah in Genesis 11:30 LXX, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, and the Shunammite). 'Well stricken in years' (probebēkotes en tais hēmerais autōn — having advanced in their days; the same idiom as Genesis 18:11 LXX about Abraham and Sarah). Luke deliberately mirrors the patriarchal barrenness narratives. In OT theology, barrenness was a sorrow and social reproach, not a moral failing (v.25). The situation is humanly impossible — the ideal setting for a divine miracle.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that while he executed the priest's office before God in the order of his course,
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ ἱερατεύειν αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ τάξει τῆς ἐφημερίας αὐτοῦ ἔναντι τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō hierateuein auton en tē taxei tēs ephēmerias autou enanti tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Executed the priest's office' (hierateuein — present infinitive of hierateuō, to function as a priest). 'In the order of his course' (tē taxei tēs ephēmerias — in the rotation of his priestly division). The temple functioned with precision: 24 priestly divisions, each serving twice a year for a week at a time. 'Before God' (enanti tou theou — in the presence of God). The narrative situates the angelic announcement within the sacred rhythms of Israel's worship — God breaks into the established order of priestly service with a wholly unexpected message.

verses.kjv_text: According to the custom of the priest's office, his lot was to burn incense when he went into the temple of the Lord.
verses.original_text: κατὰ τὸ ἔθος τῆς ἱερατείας ἔλαχε τοῦ θυμιᾶσαι εἰσελθὼν εἰς τὸν ναὸν τοῦ κυρίου
verses.transliteration: kata to ethos tēs hierateias elache tou thymiasai eiselthōn eis ton naon tou kyriou
verses.study_note: 'His lot' (elache — was chosen by lot; laggchanō, to obtain by lot). The burning of incense in the Holy Place (the Heikhal, not the Holy of Holies) was assigned by lot twice daily, at the morning and evening sacrifice. It was the most coveted priestly duty, offered so rarely that a priest might perform it only once in his lifetime (Mishnah, Tamid 5:2-6:3). 'The temple' (ton naon — the sanctuary building itself, as opposed to hieron, the whole temple complex). This was the moment of supreme priestly privilege — and God chose it to announce the birth of the forerunner.

verses.kjv_text: And the whole multitude of the people were praying without at the time of incense.
verses.original_text: καὶ πᾶν τὸ πλῆθος ἦν τοῦ λαοῦ προσευχόμενον ἔξω τῇ ὥρᾳ τοῦ θυμιάματος
verses.transliteration: kai pan to plēthos ēn tou laou proseuchomenon exō tē hōra tou thymiamatos
verses.study_note: While the priest burned incense on the golden altar, the assembled congregation prayed in the Court of Israel outside (Revelation 8:3-4 interprets the incense smoke as the prayers of saints rising before God). 'The whole multitude' (pan to plēthos tou laou — all the mass of the people) — the daily temple prayers drew large crowds. The hour of incense (approximately 9 AM or 3 PM) was the regular hour of prayer (Acts 3:1). Zacharias offers incense while the people pray — it is in this convergence of priestly ministry and congregational prayer that the divine announcement breaks in.

verses.kjv_text: And there appeared unto him an angel of the Lord standing on the right side of the altar of incense.
verses.original_text: ὤφθη δὲ αὐτῷ ἄγγελος κυρίου ἑστὼς ἐκ δεξιῶν τοῦ θυσιαστηρίου τοῦ θυμιάματος
verses.transliteration: ōphthē de autō angelos kyriou hestōs ek dexiōn tou thysiastēriou tou thymiamatostou
verses.study_note: 'There appeared' (ōphthē — aorist passive of horaō, was seen, appeared; the passive is used for divine/angelic appearances throughout Scripture, e.g., Genesis 12:7; Exodus 3:2). 'Angel of the Lord' (angelos kyriou — the standard OT messenger formula). 'Standing on the right side' (hestōs ek dexiōn — standing at the right side of; the right side is the position of honour and power in ancient thought, cf. Psalm 110:1; Mark 16:5). The golden altar of incense stood directly before the veil of the Holy of Holies (Exodus 30:6). An angel standing there would be between Zacharias and the presence of God. The appearance is sudden and unexpected — no prior warning.

verses.kjv_text: And when Zacharias saw him, he was troubled, and fear fell upon him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐταράχθη Ζαχαρίας ἰδὼν καὶ φόβος ἐπέπεσεν ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai etarachthē Zacharias idōn kai phobos epepesen ep auton
verses.study_note: 'Was troubled' (etarachthē — aorist passive of tarassō, was agitated, thrown into confusion, greatly disturbed; the same word used for the disciples in 24:38 and for Herod in Matthew 2:3). 'Fear fell upon him' (phobos epepesen — fear fell upon him; the idiom implies sudden, overwhelming dread). This is the consistent human response to angelic appearances in Scripture (Daniel 8:17; 10:9; Revelation 1:17). Zacharias is not a weak man — he is a faithful priest at the pinnacle of his ministry — yet the sudden appearance of a heavenly being reduces him to trembling. The fear is appropriate: he stands before a messenger of the holy God.

verses.kjv_text: But the angel said unto him, Fear not, Zacharias: for thy prayer is heard; and thy wife Elisabeth shall bear thee a son, and thou shalt call his name John.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ πρὸς αὐτὸν ὁ ἄγγελος Μὴ φοβοῦ Ζαχαρία διότι εἰσηκούσθη ἡ δέησίς σου καὶ ἡ γυνή σου Ἐλισάβετ γεννήσει υἱόν σοι καὶ καλέσεις τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Ἰωάννην
verses.transliteration: eipen de pros auton ho angelos Mē phobou Zacharia dioti eisēkousthē hē deēsis sou kai hē gynē sou Elisabet gennēsei huion soi kai kaleseis to onoma autou Iōannēn
verses.study_note: 'Fear not' (Mē phobou — the standard angelic salutation at moments of divine encounter; Genesis 15:1; 26:24; Judges 6:23; Daniel 10:12; Luke 1:30; 2:10). 'Thy prayer is heard' (eisēkousthē hē deēsis sou — your petition/prayer has been heard; aorist passive, divine perfect act). Had Zacharias been praying for a son even in his old age, or for the redemption of Israel? Probably both — Jewish piety intertwined personal and national hope. 'John' (Iōannēn — from Heb. Yōḥānān, the LORD is gracious/the LORD has shown favour). The name is given from heaven before conception — a mark of divine predestination for John's mission.

verses.kjv_text: And thou shalt have joy and gladness; and many shall rejoice at his birth.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔσται χαρά σοι καὶ ἀγαλλίασις καὶ πολλοὶ ἐπὶ τῇ γενέσει αὐτοῦ χαρήσονται
verses.transliteration: kai estai chara soi kai agalliasis kai polloi epi tē genesei autou charēsontai
verses.study_note: 'Joy' (chara) 'and gladness' (agalliasis — exultant, leaping joy, the fullest expression of delight). Both words appear together in Psalm 45:7 (LXX). 'Many shall rejoice at his birth' — John's birth is not a private family event but a corporate blessing. The rejoicing at John's birth (v.58 fulfils this exactly) anticipates the universal joy of the Messiah he announces. Luke's Gospel is framed by joy: it begins with rejoicing at the forerunner's birth announcement and ends with the disciples returning to Jerusalem 'with great joy' after the Ascension (24:52).

verses.kjv_text: For he shall be great in the sight of the Lord, and shall drink neither wine nor strong drink; and he shall be filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother's womb.
verses.original_text: ἔσται γὰρ μέγας ἐνώπιον κυρίου καὶ οἶνον καὶ σίκερα οὐ μὴ πίῃ καὶ Πνεύματος Ἁγίου πλησθήσεται ἔτι ἐκ κοιλίας μητρὸς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: estai gar megas enōpion kyriou kai oinon kai sikera ou mē piē kai Pneumatos Hagiou plēsthēsetai eti ek koilias mētros autou
verses.study_note: 'Great in the sight of the Lord' — not human greatness (political power, wealth) but divine esteem; cf. Jesus' word in 7:28: 'Among those that are born of women there is not a greater prophet than John the Baptist.' 'Wine nor strong drink' (oinon kai sikera — wine and shekar; the Nazarite vow of Numbers 6:3; cf. Samson in Judges 13:4-5). John's abstinence was vocational, marking him as set apart for God's purposes. 'Filled with the Holy Ghost, even from his mother's womb' — prenatal Spirit-filling is without parallel in OT or NT. The leap in Elisabeth's womb (v.41) is the first manifestation of this Spirit-filling. John is the only person in Scripture described as Spirit-filled from conception.

verses.kjv_text: And many of the children of Israel shall he turn to the Lord their God.
verses.original_text: καὶ πολλοὺς τῶν υἱῶν Ἰσραὴλ ἐπιστρέψει ἐπὶ κύριον τὸν θεὸν αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: kai pollous tōn huiōn Israēl epistrepssei epi kyrion ton theon autōn
verses.study_note: 'Turn' (epistrepssei — future of epistrephō, to turn around, to return, to convert; the classic OT word for repentance/return). John's ministry of baptism and repentance (3:3-18) will bring large numbers of Israelites back to covenant faithfulness. 'Their God' (ton theon autōn) — the LORD remains the God of Israel; John calls Israel to return to what they already have in principle but have abandoned in practice. Acts 2:38-41 records 3,000 responding at Pentecost partly as a result of John's preparatory ministry; the Gospels record crowds from all Judaea coming to John (Matthew 3:5).

verses.kjv_text: And he shall go before him in the spirit and power of Elias, to turn the hearts of the fathers to the children, and the disobedient to the wisdom of the just; to make ready a people prepared for the Lord.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς προελεύσεται ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ ἐν πνεύματι καὶ δυνάμει Ἠλίου ἐπιστρέψαι καρδίας πατέρων ἐπὶ τέκνα καὶ ἀπειθεῖς ἐν φρονήσει δικαίων ἑτοιμάσαι κυρίῳ λαὸν κατεσκευασμένον
verses.transliteration: kai autos proeleusetai enōpion autou en pneumati kai dynamei Hēliou epistrepsai kardias paterōn epi tekna kai apeitheis en phronēsei dikaiōn hetoimasai kyriō laon kateskeuasmenon
verses.study_note: 'Before him' (enōpion autou — in his presence, ahead of him; the 'him' refers to the Lord God, v.16, identifying Jesus as YHWH). 'Spirit and power of Elias' — this echoes Malachi 4:5-6 directly: 'I will send you Elijah the prophet before the coming of the great and dreadful day of the LORD.' Jesus identifies John as the Elijah-to-come (Matthew 11:14; 17:12). Not a reincarnation but a ministry of similar prophetic power: calling Israel to repentance, confronting wickedness in high places (cf. Elijah and Ahab; John and Herod). 'Hearts of the fathers to the children' — restoring generational covenant faithfulness (Malachi 4:6). 'A people prepared' (laon kateskeuasmenon — a people made ready, fitted out) — John's singular task is preparation; the Messiah himself does the rest.

verses.kjv_text: And Zacharias said unto the angel, Whereby shall I know this? for I am an old man, and my wife well stricken in years.
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἶπεν Ζαχαρίας πρὸς τὸν ἄγγελον Κατὰ τί γνώσομαι τοῦτο ἐγὼ γάρ εἰμι πρεσβύτης καὶ ἡ γυνή μου προβεβηκυῖα ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: Kai eipen Zacharias pros ton angelon Kata ti gnōsomai touto egō gar eimi presbytēs kai hē gynē mou probebēkuia en tais hēmerais autēs
verses.study_note: 'Whereby shall I know this?' (Kata ti gnōsomai touto — by what sign shall I know this?). Zacharias's question echoes Abraham's request for a sign in Genesis 15:8 ('Whereby shall I know that I shall inherit it?') yet differs crucially in tone. Abraham had faith and asked for confirmation; Zacharias exhibits unbelief (the angel's verdict in v.20 is explicit: 'because thou believest not my words'). Mary will ask a different question in v.34 ('How shall this be?') — asking about the mechanism, not doubting the truth. 'An old man' (presbytēs) — the same word used for Philemon 9 (Paul as 'Paul the aged'). Zacharias is not disqualified by age but by doubt; God can and does work through the aged, as with Abraham, Sarah, and the Shunammite.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel answering said unto him, I am Gabriel, that stand in the presence of God; and am sent to speak unto thee, and to shew thee these glad tidings.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς ὁ ἄγγελος εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ἐγώ εἰμι Γαβριὴλ ὁ παρεστηκὼς ἐνώπιον τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ἀπεστάλην λαλῆσαι πρὸς σὲ καὶ εὐαγγελίσασθαί σοι ταῦτα
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis ho angelos eipen autō Egō eimi Gabriēl ho parestēkōs enōpion tou theou kai apestalēn lalēsai pros se kai euangelisasthai soi tauta
verses.study_note: 'I am Gabriel' (Egō eimi Gabriēl — emphatic self-identification). Gabriel (Heb. Gavriel — 'God is my strength' or 'mighty man of God') is one of only two angels named in canonical Scripture (the other being Michael). Gabriel appears in Daniel 8:16 and 9:21, always carrying revelatory messages of eschatological significance. 'That stand in the presence of God' (ho parestēkōs enōpion tou theou — perfect active participle; he permanently stands before God, a position of honour reserved for the highest angelic servants). 'To shew thee these glad tidings' (euangelisasthai soi tauta — to announce good news to you; the first use of the verb euangelizomai in Luke). The irony: a man whose very profession is priestly mediation fails to believe the messenger of God himself.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, thou shalt be dumb, and not able to speak, until the day that these things shall be performed, because thou believest not my words, which shall be fulfilled in their season.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἔσῃ σιωπῶν καὶ μὴ δυνάμενος λαλῆσαι ἄχρι ἧς ἡμέρας γένηται ταῦτα ἀνθ᾽ ὧν οὐκ ἐπίστευσας τοῖς λόγοις μου οἵτινες πληρωθήσονται εἰς τὸν καιρὸν αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: kai idou esē siōpōn kai mē dynamenos lalēsai achri hēs hēmeras genētai tauta anth hōn ouk episteusis tois logois mou hoitines plērōthēsontai eis ton kairon autōn
verses.study_note: 'Thou shalt be dumb' (esē siōpōn — you will be silent, unable to speak; siōpaō denotes enforced silence). The sign Zacharias asked for (v.18) becomes a discipline: nine months of silence for nine months of unbelief. This is not mere punishment but a mercy — the silence protects Zacharias from further doubt and gives him time to ponder. 'Because thou believest not' (anth hōn ouk episteusis — in exchange for which you did not believe; the explicit reason). 'My words, which shall be fulfilled in their season' (plērōthēsontai eis ton kairon autōn — will be fulfilled at their appointed time; kairos = appointed, significant time). God's word does not fail because a man doubts it; it is fulfilled at exactly the right time regardless.

verses.kjv_text: And the people waited for Zacharias, and marvelled that he tarried so long in the temple.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἦν ὁ λαὸς προσδοκῶν τὸν Ζαχαρίαν καὶ ἐθαύμαζον ἐν τῷ χρονίζειν ἐν τῷ ναῷ αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: Kai ēn ho laos prosdokōn ton Zacharian kai ethaumazon en tō chronizein en tō naō auton
verses.study_note: 'Waited for' (prosdokōn — present participle of prosdokaō, to expect, await anxiously). The congregation outside was waiting for the priest to emerge, pronounce the Aaronic blessing (Numbers 6:24-26), and dismiss them. 'Marvelled' (ethaumazon — imperfect, they kept on marvelling; duration implied). 'He tarried so long' (chronizein — to be delayed beyond the expected time). A priest was not supposed to linger in the Holy Place lest the congregation fear he had died in the divine presence (cf. Leviticus 16:13). The extended delay signals that something extraordinary has happened. The congregation's wonder becomes the setting for Zacharias's silent, gesturing exit.

verses.kjv_text: And when he came out, he could not speak unto them: and they perceived that he had seen a vision in the temple: for he beckoned unto them, and remained speechless.
verses.original_text: ἐξελθὼν δὲ οὐκ ἠδύνατο λαλῆσαι αὐτοῖς καὶ ἐπέγνωσαν ὅτι ὀπτασίαν ἑώρακεν ἐν τῷ ναῷ καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν διανεύων αὐτοῖς καὶ διέμενεν κωφός
verses.transliteration: exelthōn de ouk ēdynato lalēsai autois kai epegnōsan hoti optasian heōraken en tō naō kai autos ēn dianeuōn autois kai diemenen kōphos
verses.study_note: 'He could not speak' (ouk ēdynato lalēsai — was not able to speak; the angelic sign already operative). 'They perceived' (epegnōsan — they recognised, discerned clearly). 'A vision' (optasian — a supernatural appearance, a heavenly vision). The congregation drew the right conclusion from the wrong data: they knew something divine had occurred but not what. 'He beckoned unto them' (ēn dianeuōn — kept making signs/gesturing; imperfect, continuing action). 'Remained speechless' (diemenen kōphos — continued deaf-mute; kōphos can mean both deaf and mute, possibly both conditions affected Zacharias, cf. v.62 where they made signs to him). The inability to pronounce the priestly blessing was a double sign: the people received no benediction from Zacharias, but the true blessing of the Messiah was on its way.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, as soon as the days of his ministration were accomplished, he departed to his own house.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ὡς ἐπλήσθησαν αἱ ἡμέραι τῆς λειτουργίας αὐτοῦ ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto hōs eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tēs leitourgias autou apēlthen eis ton oikon autou
verses.study_note: 'Days of his ministration' (hēmerai tēs leitourgias autou — days of his liturgical/priestly service; leitourgia = liturgy, official public service, the source of our word 'liturgy'). Zacharias completed his week of temple duty despite his silence — faithful to duty even in affliction. 'Departed to his own house' — the priestly courses lived in various towns of Judah (cf. Joshua 21; 1 Chronicles 6:54-81). His home was 'in the hill country of Judah' (v.39). He returned to his priestly life in the ordinary world, carrying within him the most extraordinary secret: God had spoken.

verses.kjv_text: And after those days his wife Elisabeth conceived, and hid herself five months, saying,
verses.original_text: Μετὰ δὲ ταύτας τὰς ἡμέρας συνέλαβεν Ἐλισάβετ ἡ γυνὴ αὐτοῦ καὶ περιέκρυβεν ἑαυτὴν μῆνας πέντε λέγουσα
verses.transliteration: Meta de tautas tas hēmeras synelaben Elisabet hē gynē autou kai periekruben heautēn mēnas pente legousa
verses.study_note: 'Elisabeth conceived' (synelaben Elisabet — she conceived; synlambanō = to conceive/take together). The miracle happens as announced. 'Hid herself five months' (periekruben heautēn mēnas pente — she concealed herself for five months). The reason is not given explicitly. Perhaps: (1) to confirm the pregnancy before making it known; (2) to protect herself from premature publicity; (3) to spend the early months in prayer and thanksgiving. At five months she would be visibly pregnant; only then did she speak (v.25). The six-month mark (v.26) is when Gabriel visits Mary — the timing is intentional: Elisabeth's pregnancy becomes the confirming sign for Mary (v.36).

verses.kjv_text: Thus hath the Lord dealt with me in the days wherein he looked on me, to take away my reproach among men.
verses.original_text: ὅτι Οὕτως μοι πεποίηκεν κύριος ἐν ἡμέραις αἷς ἐπεῖδεν ἀφελεῖν τὸ ὄνειδός μου ἐν ἀνθρώποις
verses.transliteration: hoti Houtōs moi pepoiēken kyrios en hēmerais hais epeiden aphelein to oneidos mou en anthrōpois
verses.study_note: 'Thus hath the Lord dealt with me' (Houtōs moi pepoiēken kyrios — thus the Lord has done for me; pepoiēken = perfect, a completed action with lasting result). Elisabeth's words echo Rachel in Genesis 30:23 ('God hath taken away my reproach') and Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 1:11; 2:1-10. 'Looked on me' (epeiden — from epeidon, to look upon with favour; the same root as the Magnificat's 'regarded,' v.48). 'Reproach among men' (oneidos — shame, dishonour). In the ancient world, barrenness was often misunderstood as divine disfavour or personal failing. Elisabeth's private joy before making her pregnancy known reflects humble gratitude rather than triumphalism.

verses.kjv_text: And in the sixth month the angel Gabriel was sent from God unto a city of Galilee, named Nazareth,
verses.original_text: Ἐν δὲ τῷ μηνὶ τῷ ἕκτῳ ἀπεστάλη ὁ ἄγγελος Γαβριὴλ ὑπὸ τοῦ θεοῦ εἰς πόλιν τῆς Γαλιλαίας ᾗ ὄνομα Ναζαρέτ
verses.transliteration: En de tō mēni tō hektō apestalē ho angelos Gabriēl hypo tou theou eis polin tēs Galilaias hē onoma Nazaret
verses.study_note: 'The sixth month' (tō mēni tō hektō) — six months into Elisabeth's pregnancy, connecting the two annunciations precisely. 'Gabriel was sent' (apestalē Gabriēl — was commissioned and dispatched; apostellō, the verb from which 'apostle' derives). Gabriel goes on a divine mission, sent with authority and purpose. 'A city of Galilee, named Nazareth' — Nazareth was an insignificant village in lower Galilee, not mentioned in the OT, Josephus, or the Talmud until it appears in the NT. Archaeological evidence suggests a population of c.200-400 in the first century. God bypasses Jerusalem and the priestly establishment for a Galilean peasant village — a pattern of divine reversal central to Luke's theology.

verses.kjv_text: To a virgin espoused to a man whose name was Joseph, of the house of David; and the virgin's name was Mary.
verses.original_text: πρὸς παρθένον μεμνηστευμένην ἀνδρὶ ᾧ ὄνομα Ἰωσὴφ ἐξ οἴκου Δαυὶδ καὶ τὸ ὄνομα τῆς παρθένου Μαριάμ
verses.transliteration: pros parthenon memnēsteumenēn andri hō onoma Iōsēph ex oikou Dauid kai to onoma tēs parthenou Mariam
verses.study_note: 'Virgin' (parthenon — the standard Greek word for a young woman who has not had sexual intercourse; used twice in this verse). Isaiah 7:14 LXX used parthenos for 'almah' (young woman), establishing the prophetic basis for the virgin birth. 'Espoused' (memnēsteumenēn — betrothed, legally pledged in marriage; Jewish betrothal was a binding legal contract, broken only by divorce). 'Of the house of David' — Joseph's Davidic lineage is emphasized (cf. Matthew 1:1-16 which traces Joseph's genealogy; Luke 3:23-38 likely traces Mary's). 'Mary' (Mariam — Greek form of Heb. Miriam). The name means 'bitter' or possibly 'beloved of God.' She is the most blessed woman in human history (v.42), and also the most humble (v.38).

verses.kjv_text: And the angel came in unto her, and said, Hail, thou that art highly favoured, the Lord is with thee: blessed art thou among women.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰσελθὼν ὁ ἄγγελος πρὸς αὐτὴν εἶπεν Χαῖρε κεχαριτωμένη ὁ κύριος μετὰ σοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai eiselthōn ho angelos pros autēn eipen Chaire kecharitōmenē ho kyrios meta sou
verses.study_note: 'Hail' (Chaire — rejoice, greetings; the standard Greek greeting, but here laden with theological weight: Gabriel is announcing the source of all true joy). 'Highly favoured' (kecharitōmenē — perfect passive participle of charitoō, to bestow grace/favour; only used here and Ephesians 1:6 in the NT). The perfect participle indicates a completed action with lasting effect: Mary has already been endowed with God's grace before the angel arrives. This is not a title or status she has earned, but grace received. The Latin 'gratia plena' (full of grace) of the Vulgate is a reasonable translation but should not imply Mary has grace to dispense to others — she has received it, as all believers do (Ephesians 1:6). 'The Lord is with thee' — the Immanuel promise of the OT applied personally (Genesis 28:15; Joshua 1:5). 'Blessed art thou among women' — the highest rank among women, not above all creation.

verses.kjv_text: And when she saw him, she was troubled at his saying, and cast in her mind what manner of salutation this should be.
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ ἐπὶ τῷ λόγῳ διεταράχθη καὶ διελογίζετο ποταπὸς εἴη ὁ ἀσπασμὸς οὗτος
verses.transliteration: hē de epi tō logō dietarachthē kai dielogizeto potapos eiē ho aspasmos houtos
verses.study_note: 'Was troubled' (dietarachthē — greatly disturbed, thrown into confusion; a compound form of tarassō, intensifying the agitation). Note: Mary is troubled at the 'saying' (logos, the word), not merely at the appearance — she is disturbed by what is said, not just the sight of an angel. 'Cast in her mind' (dielogizeto — was reasoning carefully, pondering, turning the matter over; present imperfect, continuous action). 'What manner of salutation' (potapos eiē ho aspasmos — what kind of greeting this might be). Mary does not respond with fear of the vision but with thoughtful engagement with its content. Her pondering is a sign of her wisdom (cf. 2:19, 51 — she treasured and pondered all these things).

verses.kjv_text: And the angel said unto her, Fear not, Mary: for thou hast found favour with God.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν ὁ ἄγγελος αὐτῇ Μὴ φοβοῦ Μαριάμ εὗρες γὰρ χάριν παρὰ τῷ θεῷ
verses.transliteration: kai eipen ho angelos autē Mē phobou Mariam heures gar charin para tō theō
verses.study_note: 'Fear not, Mary' — the same angelic reassurance given to Zacharias (v.13), to the shepherds (2:10), and throughout the biblical narrative when God encounters humanity. 'Thou hast found favour with God' (heures charin para tō theō — you have found grace/favour before God; aorist: a definite past event). 'Found favour' is the OT idiom for receiving divine grace and election (Genesis 6:8 — Noah; Exodus 33:12-13 — Moses; 1 Samuel 1:18 — Hannah). It means God has looked upon Mary with delight and chosen her for this purpose — not because of merit, but because of sovereign grace. 'With God' (para tō theō — in God's sight, before God) — not merely human approval but divine election.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, thou shalt conceive in thy womb, and bring forth a son, and shalt call his name JESUS.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ συλλήψῃ ἐν γαστρὶ καὶ τέξῃ υἱόν καὶ καλέσεις τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦν
verses.transliteration: kai idou syllēpsē en gastri kai texē huion kai kaleseis to onoma autou Iēsoun
verses.study_note: 'Behold' (idou — the divine attention-getter, look!). 'Thou shalt conceive' (syllēpsē — future middle of syllambanō, to conceive; the same word as v.24 for Elisabeth). 'In thy womb' (en gastri — in the womb/belly; explicit physical language affirming the genuine humanity of the incarnation). 'Bring forth a son' (texē huion — you will give birth to a son). 'JESUS' (Iēsoun — Greek form of Heb. Yēshūa, Joshua, meaning the LORD saves/the LORD is salvation). Matthew 1:21 makes the etymology explicit: 'thou shalt call his name JESUS: for he shall save his people from their sins.' The name defines the mission. This is the first use of 'Jesus' in Luke.

verses.kjv_text: He shall be great, and shall be called the Son of the Highest: and the Lord God shall give unto him the throne of his father David:
verses.original_text: οὗτος ἔσται μέγας καὶ υἱὸς ὑψίστου κληθήσεται καὶ δώσει αὐτῷ κύριος ὁ θεὸς τὸν θρόνον Δαυὶδ τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: houtos estai megas kai huios hypsistou klēthēsetai kai dōsei autō kyrios ho theos ton thronon Dauid tou patros autou
verses.study_note: 'He shall be great' — John was described as 'great in the sight of the Lord' (v.15); Jesus is simply 'great,' the absolute superlative without qualification. 'Son of the Highest' (huios hypsistou — Son of the Most High; hupsistos = the Highest, the Supreme One, a title for God used in LXX and Luke). The title identifies Jesus as the divine Son, not merely a great human figure. 'The throne of his father David' — the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:12-16) promised an eternal throne to David's descendant. Jesus is the ultimate fulfilment: 'his kingdom shall have no end' (v.33). Both natures are implied: Son of the Highest (divine) and heir of David (human). The Messiah must be both.

verses.kjv_text: And he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever; and of his kingdom there shall be no end.
verses.original_text: καὶ βασιλεύσει ἐπὶ τὸν οἶκον Ἰακὼβ εἰς τοὺς αἰῶνας καὶ τῆς βασιλείας αὐτοῦ οὐκ ἔσται τέλος
verses.transliteration: kai basileusei epi ton oikon Iakōb eis tous aiōnas kai tēs basileias autou ouk estai telos
verses.study_note: 'Shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever' (basileusei epi ton oikon Iakōb eis tous aiōnas — will reign over the house of Jacob into the ages). 'House of Jacob' = Israel, but in its full eschatological sense embracing the Israel of God (Galatians 6:16) — all who are Abraham's seed by faith (Galatians 3:29). 'Of his kingdom there shall be no end' (tēs basileias autou ouk estai telos — of his kingdom there will not be an end). Daniel 7:14 prophesied of the Son of Man: 'his dominion is an everlasting dominion, which shall not pass away, and his kingdom that which shall not be destroyed.' Revelation 11:15: 'The kingdoms of this world are become the kingdoms of our Lord, and of his Christ; and he shall reign for ever and ever.'

verses.kjv_text: Then said Mary unto the angel, How shall this be, seeing I know not a man?
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ Μαριὰμ πρὸς τὸν ἄγγελον Πῶς ἔσται τοῦτο ἐπεὶ ἄνδρα οὐ γινώσκω
verses.transliteration: eipen de Mariam pros ton angelon Pōs estai touto epei andra ou ginōskō
verses.study_note: 'How shall this be?' (Pōs estai touto — how will this be?). Mary's question differs fundamentally from Zacharias's (v.18). Zacharias asked for a confirming sign because he doubted; Mary asks about the manner (how), not the reality (whether). She accepts the fact of the announcement and enquires about its mechanism. 'I know not a man' (andra ou ginōskō — I do not know a man; ginōskō used as a euphemism for sexual intercourse, as in Genesis 4:1 LXX). Mary's question presupposes that she intends to remain a virgin, even within the betrothal — otherwise the conception would happen normally through Joseph after marriage. Her question is not doubt but wonder: the logic of a virgin conceiving requires divine explanation.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel answered and said unto her, The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee, and the power of the Highest shall overshadow thee: therefore also that holy thing which shall be born of thee shall be called the Son of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς ὁ ἄγγελος εἶπεν αὐτῇ Πνεῦμα ἅγιον ἐπελεύσεται ἐπὶ σέ καὶ δύναμις ὑψίστου ἐπισκιάσει σοι διὸ καὶ τὸ γεννώμενον ἅγιον κληθήσεται υἱὸς θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis ho angelos eipen autē Pneuma hagion epeleusetai epi se kai dynamis hypsistou episkiasei soi dio kai to gennōmenon hagion klēthēsetai huios theou
verses.study_note: 'The Holy Ghost shall come upon thee' (Pneuma hagion epeleusetai epi se — the Holy Spirit will come upon you; eperchomai = to come upon, a term for divine enabling). 'The power of the Highest shall overshadow thee' (dynamis hypsistou episkiasei soi — the power of the Most High will overshadow you; episkiazō = to cast a shadow over, as the Shekinah cloud overshadowed the tabernacle, Exodus 40:35 LXX). The incarnation is a Trinitarian act: the Father (the Highest) sends, the Spirit performs the creative work. 'That holy thing' (to gennōmenon hagion — the holy one being born) — the holiness of Jesus at conception is affirmed. 'Son of God' — the title flows from the manner of conception: because he has no human father, the Father of this child is God himself. The virginal conception is the grounds for the title Son of God in its unique, personal sense.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, thy cousin Elisabeth, she hath also conceived a son in her old age: and this is the sixth month with her, who was called barren.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ Ἐλισάβετ ἡ συγγενίς σου καὶ αὐτὴ συνείληφεν υἱὸν ἐν γήρει αὐτῆς καὶ οὗτος μὴν ἕκτος ἐστὶν αὐτῇ τῇ καλουμένῃ στείρᾳ
verses.transliteration: kai idou Elisabet hē synggenis sou kai autē syneilēphen huion en gērei autēs kai houtos mēn hektos estin autē tē kaloumenē steira
verses.study_note: 'Thy cousin' (hē synggenis sou — your relative; synggenis = kinswoman, female relative; the precise degree of relationship is not specified). 'She hath also conceived' (kai autē syneilēphen — she also has conceived; perfect, action already complete). Gabriel provides a confirming sign without being asked (contrast Zacharias who asked for a sign and received a discipline). The miracle of Elisabeth's conception confirms the power of God for the greater miracle of the virgin birth. 'Who was called barren' (tē kaloumenē steirō — the one who was called barren) — the past tense implicit: she was called barren, but is no longer. In God's economy, the impossible always yields to the divine word.

verses.kjv_text: For with God nothing shall be impossible.
verses.original_text: ὅτι οὐκ ἀδυνατήσει παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ πᾶν ῥῆμα
verses.transliteration: hoti ouk adynatēsei para tou theou pan rhēma
verses.study_note: 'With God nothing shall be impossible' (ouk adynatēsei para tou theou pan rhēma — every word/thing will not be impossible with God; a litotes: 'nothing impossible' = 'everything possible'). The Greek says literally 'every word will not be impossible with God' — rhēma (word/thing) echoes Genesis 18:14 LXX: 'Shall any thing be too hard for the LORD?' spoken to Sarah about Isaac's miraculous birth. The same divine omnipotence that created the universe from nothing can create new life in a virgin's womb. This verse is the theological anchor for both annunciations: Elisabeth's miraculous conception and Mary's virgin conception rest on the same foundation — the unlimited power of God.

verses.kjv_text: And Mary said, Behold the handmaid of the Lord; be it unto me according to thy word. And the angel departed from her.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ Μαριάμ Ἰδοὺ ἡ δούλη κυρίου γένοιτό μοι κατὰ τὸ ῥῆμά σου καὶ ἀπῆλθεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτῆς ὁ ἄγγελος
verses.transliteration: eipen de Mariam Idou hē doulē kyriou genoito moi kata to rhēma sou kai apēlthen ap autēs ho angelos
verses.study_note: 'Behold the handmaid of the Lord' (Idou hē doulē kyriou — behold the female slave of the Lord; doulē = bond-slave, the most extreme word for complete submission and ownership). Mary does not say 'servant' (diakonos) or 'helper' but 'slave' — she places herself wholly at God's disposal. 'Be it unto me according to thy word' (genoito moi kata to rhēma sou — may it be to me according to your word; optative mood expressing a wish/surrender: 'let it be so!'). This is the supreme act of faith and submission in the NT. Mary consents to shame (pregnant outside of wedlock, from the world's perspective), danger (Joseph could legally divorce her, even have her stoned), and sorrow (the sword through her soul, 2:35) — all for the word of God. 'The angel departed' — Gabriel's mission is complete. Mary stands alone with the most profound mystery in history.

verses.kjv_text: And Mary arose in those days, and went into the hill country with haste, into a city of Juda;
verses.original_text: Ἀναστᾶσα δὲ Μαριὰμ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ταύταις ἐπορεύθη εἰς τὴν ὀρεινὴν μετὰ σπουδῆς εἰς πόλιν Ἰούδα
verses.transliteration: Anastasa de Mariam en tais hēmerais tautais eporeuthē eis tēn oreinēn meta spoudēs eis polin Iouda
verses.study_note: 'Arose' (Anastasa — aorist participle of anistēmi, rose up; the same word used for resurrection). 'With haste' (meta spoudēs — with eagerness, urgency, speed; not fearful haste but the haste of joyful purpose). Mary's immediate action reflects both obedience (the angel had mentioned Elisabeth, and Mary goes to verify and share) and love (a visit of caring fellowship and support). 'The hill country' (tēn oreinēn — the mountainous region) — the hill country of Judah, approximately 100 miles from Nazareth, a three-to-five day journey. Mary makes the journey probably within days of the annunciation. Tradition identifies the city as Ein Kerem, several miles west of Jerusalem.

verses.kjv_text: And entered into the house of Zacharias, and saluted Elisabeth.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον Ζαχαρίου καὶ ἠσπάσατο τὴν Ἐλισάβετ
verses.transliteration: kai eisēlthen eis ton oikon Zachariou kai ēspasato tēn Elisabet
verses.study_note: 'Entered into the house of Zacharias' — the priestly household provides the setting for the meeting of the two mothers who are carrying the forerunner and the Messiah. Luke gives no description of the journey, no dialogue en route — the narrative focuses entirely on the meeting and its Spirit-filled result. 'Saluted Elisabeth' (ēspasato tēn Elisabet — greeted, embraced Elisabeth; aspazomai = to greet warmly, embrace). The Greek greeting could be verbal, physical, or both. It is this greeting (ton aspasmon, v.44) that triggers the Spirit-filled response in both Elisabeth and the unborn John.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, when Elisabeth heard the salutation of Mary, the babe leaped in her womb; and Elisabeth was filled with the Holy Ghost:
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο ὡς ἤκουσεν τὸν ἀσπασμὸν τῆς Μαρίας ἡ Ἐλισάβετ ἐσκίρτησεν τὸ βρέφος ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ αὐτῆς καὶ ἐπλήσθη Πνεύματος Ἁγίου ἡ Ἐλισάβετ
verses.transliteration: kai egeneto hōs ēkousen ton aspasmon tēs Marias hē Elisabet eskirtēsen to brephos en tē koilia autēs kai eplēsthē Pneumatos Hagiou hē Elisabet
verses.study_note: 'The babe leaped' (eskirtēsen to brephos — the baby leaped/jumped; skirtaō = to leap, spring, used in LXX for the leaping of the nations before God in Psalm 114:4, 6, and for the leaping of fetal Jacob in Genesis 25:22 LXX). 'Brephos' — the word used here for John in the womb is the same word used in 2:12 for the baby Jesus in the manger and in 18:15 for infants brought to Jesus — Luke consistently uses brephos for an unborn or newborn child. The leap is the prenatal Spirit-filled recognition of the Lord by his forerunner. 'Elisabeth was filled with the Holy Ghost' — Spirit-filling produces prophetic speech (vv.42-45). The unborn John's response vindicates his prenatal Spirit-filling (v.15).

verses.kjv_text: And she spake out with a loud voice, and said, Blessed art thou among women, and blessed is the fruit of thy womb.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀνεφώνησεν κραυγῇ μεγάλῃ καὶ εἶπεν Εὐλογημένη σὺ ἐν γυναιξίν καὶ εὐλογημένος ὁ καρπὸς τῆς κοιλίας σου
verses.transliteration: kai anephōnēsen kraugē megalē kai eipen Eulogēmenē su en gynaixin kai eulogēmenos ho karpos tēs koilias sou
verses.study_note: 'Spake out with a loud voice' (anephōnēsen kraugē megalē — exclaimed with a great cry/shout; anaphōneō = to cry out, exclaim; the word is used in LXX for the Levitical singers in 1 Chronicles 15:28; 16:4-5 — a liturgical, Spirit-moved proclamation). 'Blessed art thou among women' (Eulogēmenē su en gynaixin — blessed are you among women; eulogēmenē = praised, honoured, the one upon whom God's blessing rests). The same blessing will be spoken in 11:27. 'Blessed is the fruit of thy womb' — Elisabeth blesses both Mary and her child. This double beatitude echoes Deborah's blessing of Jael (Judges 5:24). The blessing of Mary is appropriate and scriptural; but the reason for blessing Mary is always her Son.

verses.kjv_text: And whence is this to me, that the mother of my Lord should come to me?
verses.original_text: καὶ πόθεν μοι τοῦτο ἵνα ἔλθῃ ἡ μήτηρ τοῦ κυρίου μου πρός με
verses.transliteration: kai pothen moi touto hina elthē hē mētēr tou kyriou mou pros me
verses.study_note: 'Whence is this to me' (pothen moi touto — from where does this come to me, how have I deserved this?) — Elisabeth expresses astonished humility at being visited by the mother of her Lord. 'The mother of my Lord' (hē mētēr tou kyriou mou — the mother of my Lord). This is the earliest explicit confession of Jesus' Lordship in the NT, spoken by a woman filled with the Spirit before Jesus was even born. 'Kyrios' (Lord) in this context carries the full weight of the LXX usage for YHWH; Elisabeth is identifying Mary's unborn child as her Lord-God. This provides the biblical basis for the title Theotokos (God-bearer) affirmed at the Council of Ephesus (431 AD) — not that Mary is the mother of the divine nature, but that the one she carries is genuinely God.

verses.kjv_text: For, lo, as soon as the voice of thy salutation sounded in mine ears, the babe leaped in my womb for joy.
verses.original_text: ἰδοὺ γάρ ὡς ἐγένετο ἡ φωνὴ τοῦ ἀσπασμοῦ σου εἰς τὰ ὦτά μου ἐσκίρτησεν τὸ βρέφος ἐν ἀγαλλιάσει ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ μου
verses.transliteration: idou gar hōs egeneto hē phōnē tou aspasmou sou eis ta ōta mou eskirtēsen to brephos en agalliasei en tē koilia mou
verses.study_note: 'The voice of thy salutation' (hē phōnē tou aspasmou sou — the sound of your greeting). The trigger for John's leap was the sound of Mary's voice — her physical presence, not merely a spiritual perception. 'Leaped for joy' (eskirtēsen en agalliasei — leaped in exultation/joyful excitement; agalliasis = the same word used in v.14 for the 'gladness' at John's birth and in v.47 in the Magnificat). The unborn John fulfils the angel's prediction (v.14: 'many shall rejoice at his birth') even before his birth, and fulfils his vocation (v.17: preparing the Lord's way) even in the womb. This is the first recorded response to the presence of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And blessed is she that believed: for there shall be a performance of those things which were told her from the Lord.
verses.original_text: καὶ μακαρία ἡ πιστεύσασα ὅτι ἔσται τελείωσις τοῖς λελαλημένοις αὐτῇ παρὰ κυρίου
verses.transliteration: kai makaria hē pisteuasasa hoti estai teleiōsis tois lelalēmenois autē para kyriou
verses.study_note: 'Blessed' (makaria — happy, fortunate, blessed; makarios is the word of the Beatitudes, Matthew 5:3-11). This is the first beatitude in Luke's Gospel. 'She that believed' (hē pisteuasasa — the one who believed, trusted, had faith; aorist participle). Elisabeth implicitly contrasts Mary's faith with Zacharias's unbelief (v.20). 'There shall be a performance' (estai teleiōsis — there will be a fulfilment/completion; teleiōsis = completion to the end, full accomplishment). 'Those things told her from the Lord' — the angelic announcement. The beatitude of faith: because Mary believed, the word will be fulfilled; and because the word is fulfilled, Mary is blessed. Faith and fulfilment are inseparable in Luke's theology.

verses.kjv_text: And Mary said, My soul doth magnify the Lord,
verses.original_text: Καὶ εἶπεν Μαριάμ Μεγαλύνει ἡ ψυχή μου τὸν κύριον
verses.transliteration: Kai eipen Mariam Megalynei hē psychē mou ton kyrion
verses.study_note: The Magnificat (vv.46-55) is one of the greatest hymns of Scripture, closely modelled on Hannah's prayer in 1 Samuel 2:1-10. Luke preserves it as authentic historical speech. 'My soul doth magnify the Lord' (Megalynei hē psychē mou ton kyrion — my soul makes great the Lord; megalynō = to magnify, make great, declare great; present tense: ongoing, characteristic action). The soul (psychē — the whole inner person, will, mind, emotion) is fully engaged. Mary does not magnify herself — Elisabeth has just praised her — but immediately redirects all praise to God. This selfless theocentric response is the mark of genuine spiritual greatness. God is made great not because praising him increases his intrinsic greatness, but because our praise rightly represents his glory.

verses.kjv_text: And my spirit hath rejoiced in God my Saviour.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἠγαλλίασεν τὸ πνεῦμά μου ἐπὶ τῷ θεῷ τῷ σωτῆρί μου
verses.transliteration: kai ēgalliiasen to pneuma mou epi tō theō tō sōtēri mou
verses.study_note: 'My spirit hath rejoiced' (ēgalliiasen to pneuma mou — my spirit exulted; aorist, the joyous leap of the moment of encounter; agalliaō = leap for joy, exult). 'God my Saviour' (tō theō tō sōtēri mou — in God the One who is my Saviour). Mary calls God her Saviour — she needs salvation just as all humans do. This is the definitive counter to the Roman Catholic doctrine of the Immaculate Conception (that Mary was conceived without original sin): Mary herself confesses God as her Saviour. No sinless person needs a Saviour. The joy of salvation — personal, experiential, specific — is the ground of the Magnificat.

verses.kjv_text: For he hath regarded the low estate of his handmaiden: for, behold, from henceforth all generations shall call me blessed.
verses.original_text: ὅτι ἐπέβλεψεν ἐπὶ τὴν ταπείνωσιν τῆς δούλης αὐτοῦ ἰδοὺ γὰρ ἀπὸ τοῦ νῦν μακαριοῦσίν με πᾶσαι αἱ γενεαί
verses.transliteration: hoti epeblepsen epi tēn tapeinōsin tēs doulēs autou idou gar apo tou nyn makariousin me pasai hai geneai
verses.study_note: 'He hath regarded' (epeblepsen — aorist, he looked upon; epiblepō = to look upon with care, concern, and love; used in LXX for God's compassionate gaze, Psalm 25:16; 102:17). 'Low estate' (tapeinōsin — lowliness, humiliation, humble station; the social and spiritual abasement of a poor village girl). 'His handmaiden' (tēs doulēs autou — his female slave; the same word as v.38). Mary defines herself not by her exaltation but by her previous condition of lowliness — it is precisely her lowliness that God has chosen to honour. 'All generations shall call me blessed' — this is a prophecy still being fulfilled. Elisabeth already calls her blessed (v.42); now Mary prophesies that the whole church through all ages will do the same. To 'call blessed' is not to worship but to recognise and celebrate the blessing God has placed upon her.

verses.kjv_text: For he that is mighty hath done to me great things; and holy is his name.
verses.original_text: ὅτι ἐποίησέν μοι μεγάλα ὁ δυνατός καὶ ἅγιον τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: hoti epoiēsen moi megala ho dynatos kai hagion to onoma autou
verses.study_note: 'He that is mighty' (ho dynatos — the Powerful/Mighty One; dynatos = able, powerful, mighty). 'Hath done to me great things' (epoiēsen moi megala — has done great things for me; aorist, specific divine act). 'Holy is his name' (hagion to onoma autou — holy is his name; the name represents the person and character). This echoes Psalm 111:9: 'Holy and reverend is his name.' The two attributes of God celebrated here — power and holiness — are the foundation of true worship. God's power without holiness would be terror; his holiness without power would be irrelevance. Together they characterise the God who has acted to bring salvation in a specific, historical, verifiable way.

verses.kjv_text: And his mercy is on them that fear him from generation to generation.
verses.original_text: καὶ τὸ ἔλεος αὐτοῦ εἰς γενεὰς καὶ γενεὰς τοῖς φοβουμένοις αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai to eleos autou eis geneas kai geneas tois phoboumenois auton
verses.study_note: 'His mercy' (to eleos autou — his mercy/steadfast love; eleos translates Heb. hesed, the covenantal loving-kindness of God, his loyal faithfulness to the covenant). 'From generation to generation' (eis geneas kai geneas — into generations and generations; the repeated word emphasises endlessness). 'Them that fear him' (tois phoboumenois auton — the ones who fear/reverence him; participle). The fear of God is not servile terror but reverent, worshipful awe — the orientation of the whole person toward God as the ultimate reality. God's mercy (hesed) is continuous across every generation to those who maintain this orientation. The Magnificat moves from personal testimony (vv.46-49) to corporate and universal doxology (vv.50-55).

verses.kjv_text: He hath shewed strength with his arm; he hath scattered the proud in the imagination of their hearts.
verses.original_text: Ἐποίησεν κράτος ἐν βραχίονι αὐτοῦ διεσκόρπισεν ὑπερηφάνους διανοίᾳ καρδίας αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: Epoiēsen kratos en brachioni autou dieskorpisen hyperēphanous dianoia kardias autōn
verses.study_note: 'Shewed strength with his arm' (Epoiēsen kratos en brachioni autou — he showed/worked strength with his arm). The 'arm of the LORD' is the classic OT metaphor for divine power in redemptive action (Exodus 6:6; Psalm 89:13; Isaiah 40:10; 51:5). 'Scattered the proud' (dieskorpisen hyperēphanous — scattered the arrogant; diaskorpizō = to scatter, disperse; hyperēphanous = those who are arrogant, haughty, self-exalting). 'Imagination of their hearts' (dianoia kardias autōn — the thought/reasoning of their hearts). Pride operates in the heart's interior — in delusion, self-deception, and the arrogant independence from God. God scatters not just proud actions but proud thoughts. Proverbs 16:18: 'Pride goeth before destruction, and an haughty spirit before a fall.'

verses.kjv_text: He hath put down the mighty from their seats, and exalted them of low degree.
verses.original_text: καθεῖλεν δυνάστας ἀπὸ θρόνων καὶ ὕψωσεν ταπεινούς
verses.transliteration: katheilen dynastas apo thronōn kai hypsōsen tapeinous
verses.study_note: 'Put down the mighty from their seats' (katheilen dynastas apo thronōn — he pulled down rulers/potentates from thrones; kathairō = to pull down, demolish; dynastas = rulers, powerful ones, dynasts). The aorist tense presents these as accomplished facts in the divine perspective — God's characteristic pattern of action. 'Exalted them of low degree' (hypsōsen tapeinous — lifted up the humble/lowly; tapeinous = those of low station, the opposite of the proud). This is the great reversal — the pattern of the kingdom of God: what human society exalts, God humbles; what human society despises, God honours. Mary herself is the paradigm: a poor peasant girl, chosen above all women. 1 Samuel 2:8; Job 5:11; Psalm 147:6.

verses.kjv_text: He hath filled the hungry with good things; and the rich he hath sent empty away.
verses.original_text: πεινῶντας ἐνέπλησεν ἀγαθῶν καὶ πλουτοῦντας ἐξαπέστειλεν κενούς
verses.transliteration: peinōntas eneplēsen agathōn kai ploutountas exapesteilen kenous
verses.study_note: 'He hath filled the hungry with good things' (peinōntas eneplēsen agathōn — he filled the hungry with good things; peinōntas = present participle, the habitually hungry, those in ongoing need; empimplēmi = to fill completely). 'Rich he hath sent empty away' (ploutountas exapesteilen kenous — he sent away the wealthy empty; kenous = empty, hollow, having nothing). This is not a simple endorsement of poverty or condemnation of wealth, but a statement about spiritual orientation: the hungry (those who acknowledge their need) are filled; the rich (those who trust in their wealth and feel no need of God) are sent away empty. The Beatitudes echo this: 'Blessed are they which do hunger and thirst after righteousness' (Matthew 5:6); cf. Luke 6:20-21, 24-25.

verses.kjv_text: He hath holpen his servant Israel, in remembrance of his mercy;
verses.original_text: ἀντελάβετο Ἰσραὴλ παιδὸς αὐτοῦ μνησθῆναι ἐλέους
verses.transliteration: antelabeto Israēl paidos autou mnēsthēnai eleous
verses.study_note: 'Holpen his servant Israel' (antelabeto Israēl paidos autou — he helped/took hold of Israel his servant; antilambanō = to take hold of in order to help, to assist, to come to the aid of). 'Israel' here is the covenant people. 'His servant' (paidos autou — his child/servant; pais = servant/child, the LXX word for the Servant of the LORD in Isaiah 41:8-9; 42:1; 44:1; 49:3). The coming of Jesus is God's definitive intervention to help his people. 'In remembrance of his mercy' (mnēsthēnai eleous — to remember mercy/steadfast love; the aorist infinitive expressing purpose). God is not obligated; he acts out of remembered covenant love (hesed). The incarnation is an act of divine memory and faithfulness.

verses.kjv_text: As he spake to our fathers, to Abraham, and to his seed for ever.
verses.original_text: καθὼς ἐλάλησεν πρὸς τοὺς πατέρας ἡμῶν τῷ Ἀβραὰμ καὶ τῷ σπέρματι αὐτοῦ εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα
verses.transliteration: kathōs elalēsen pros tous pateras hēmōn tō Abraam kai tō spermati autou eis ton aiōna
verses.study_note: 'As he spake to our fathers' (kathōs elalēsen pros tous pateras hēmōn — just as he spoke to our fathers; the Abrahamic covenant is the specific reference). 'To Abraham, and to his seed for ever' (tō Abraam kai tō spermati autou eis ton aiōna — to Abraham and to his seed forever). Paul unpacks 'seed' in Galatians 3:16: 'Now to Abraham and his seed were the promises made. He saith not, And to seeds, as of many; but as of one, And to thy seed, which is Christ.' The Magnificat ends by anchoring the coming of Jesus in the foundational covenant promise of Genesis 12:1-3; 22:18. Every blessing from God flows through Abraham's seed — ultimately through Christ (Galatians 3:29). Mary's hymn is thus not simply a personal thanksgiving but an eschatological announcement: the Abrahamic promises are now being fulfilled.

verses.kjv_text: And Mary abode with her about three months, and returned to her own house.
verses.original_text: Ἔμεινεν δὲ Μαριὰμ σὺν αὐτῇ ὡσεὶ μῆνας τρεῖς καὶ ὑπέστρεψεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: Emeinen de Mariam syn autē hōsei mēnas treis kai hypestrepsen eis ton oikon autēs
verses.study_note: 'About three months' (hōsei mēnas treis — approximately three months). Mary arrived when Elisabeth was six months pregnant (v.26, 36) and stayed three months — leaving just before or just after John's birth. Luke records the Visitation's end before narrating John's birth (vv.57ff), keeping the two narratives distinct. 'Returned to her own house' (hypestrepsen eis ton oikon autēs — returned home). By the time Mary returned to Nazareth, she would herself have been noticeably pregnant — exactly the situation that confronted Joseph and that Matthew describes (Matthew 1:18-19). Mary's three-month stay was an act of practical love and solidarity: she helped Elisabeth through the final trimester of her pregnancy.

verses.kjv_text: Now Elisabeth's full time came that she should be delivered; and she brought forth a son.
verses.original_text: Τῇ δὲ Ἐλισάβετ ἐπλήσθη ὁ χρόνος τοῦ τεκεῖν αὐτήν καὶ ἐγέννησε�� υἱόν
verses.transliteration: Tē de Elisabet eplēsthē ho chronos tou tekein autēn kai egennēsen huion
verses.study_note: 'Full time came' (eplēsthē ho chronos tou tekein autēn — the time for her to give birth was fulfilled; plēroō = to fill up, complete, fulfil; a Lukan word for divine fulfilment). The word 'fulfilled' (plēroō) is significant: this is not a random biological event but the completion of a divinely orchestrated timeline. 'She brought forth a son' (egennēsen huion — she gave birth to a son; aorist, simple statement of fact). The promised birth happens exactly as announced. Gabriel's word to Zacharias (v.13) is now fulfilled in fact. The miracle is underscored by Luke's silence about any difficulty in the birth — the same God who enabled conception also guided delivery.

verses.kjv_text: And her neighbours and her cousins heard how the Lord had shewed great mercy upon her; and they rejoiced with her.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤκουσαν οἱ περίοικοι καὶ οἱ συγγενεῖς αὐτῆς ὅτι ἐμεγάλυνεν κύριος τὸ ἔλεος αὐτοῦ μετ᾽ αὐτῆς καὶ συνέχαιρον αὐτῇ
verses.transliteration: kai ēkousan hoi perioikoi kai hoi syngeneis autēs hoti emegalynen kyrios to eleos autou met autēs kai synechairon autē
verses.study_note: 'Heard how the Lord had shewed great mercy' (emegalynen kyrios to eleos autou — the Lord made great/magnified his mercy with her; the same verb as 'magnify' in the Magnificat, v.46). The community recognises what Elisabeth had already said privately (v.25): this birth is an act of divine mercy, not mere biological fortune. 'Rejoiced with her' (synechairon autē — rejoiced together with her; the community joins the family's joy). The fulfilment of v.14 ('many shall rejoice at his birth') is immediate: neighbours and relatives gather to celebrate. Joy at John's birth anticipates the greater joy his ministry will announce.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that on the eighth day they came to circumcise the child; and they called him Zacharias, after the name of his father.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῇ ὀγδόῃ ἦλθον περιτεμεῖν τὸ παιδίον καὶ ἐκάλουν αὐτὸ ἐπὶ τῷ ὀνόματι τοῦ πατρὸς αὐτοῦ Ζαχαρίαν
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tē hēmera tē ogdoē ēlthon peritemein to paidion kai ekaloun auto epi tō onomati tou patros autou Zacharian
verses.study_note: 'On the eighth day' (en tē hēmera tē ogdoē) — circumcision on the eighth day was the Mosaic law (Leviticus 12:3; cf. Genesis 17:12). Luke notes this detail to show that John was properly incorporated into the covenant community from birth. 'They called him Zacharias, after the name of his father' — the naming at circumcision was customary by this period (though not required by Torah). Naming after the father or grandfather was a common Jewish practice in the Second Temple period. The attempt to name the child Zacharias reflects human convention; the name had already been given by God through Gabriel (v.13). Naming a child is here an act of claiming and conferring identity — the community tries to claim John for the familiar, but God has ordained the extraordinary.

verses.kjv_text: And his mother answered and said, Not so; but he shall be called John.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεῖσα ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ εἶπεν Οὐχί ἀλλὰ κληθήσεται Ἰωάννης
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheisa hē mētēr autou eipen Ouchi alla klēthēsetai Iōannēs
verses.study_note: 'Not so' (Ouchi — strong negative, certainly not). Elisabeth's firm refusal is remarkable: she contradicts community custom and social pressure without hesitation or apology. Note that Zacharias was present (being deaf-mute, v.22, 62) but it is Elisabeth who first insists on the name — she too has been told, either by Zacharias' communication before his silence or by direct revelation. 'He shall be called John' (klēthēsetai Iōannēs — he will be called John; future passive, expressing divine certainty). Elisabeth does not say 'I want to name him John' but 'he shall be called' — she recognises that the name has been given from above. Faithful obedience to the heavenly mandate outweighs the pressure of human convention.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto her, There is none of thy kindred that is called by this name.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς αὐτὴν ὅτι Οὐδείς ἐστιν ἐν τῇ συγγενείᾳ σου ὃς καλεῖται τῷ ὀνόματι τούτῳ
verses.transliteration: kai eipan pros autēn hoti Oudeis estin en tē syngeneia sou hos kaleitai tō onomati toutō
verses.study_note: 'There is none of thy kindred that is called by this name' — the social objection: 'John' (Yōḥānān) was not a common name in this family's tradition. In Jewish culture, naming after relatives honoured the family line and maintained memory across generations. Breaking this custom required strong justification. The community's objection reveals how radical God's intervention is: he does not follow family convention but creates something new. The name John ('the LORD is gracious') would soon become one of the most beloved names in the world — but at this moment, it was strange and unexpected. Divine naming always breaks human patterns.

verses.kjv_text: And they made signs to his father, how he would have him called.
verses.original_text: ἐνένευον δὲ τῷ πατρὶ αὐτοῦ τὸ τί ἂν θέλοι καλεῖσθαι αὐτό
verses.transliteration: eneneuon de tō patri autou to ti an theloi kaleisthai auto
verses.study_note: 'Made signs to his father' (eneneuon — they were making gestures, nodding/signing to his father; neuō = to nod, make signs; imperfect, they kept making signs). The fact that they made signs confirms that Zacharias was both mute and deaf (kōphos in v.22 covers both). Had he been only mute (unable to speak but able to hear), there would be no need for signs. The community turns to Zacharias as the head of household and the one with patriarchal authority over the naming. They still expected him to override Elisabeth's unusual choice. 'How he would have him called' (to ti an theloi kaleisthai auto — what he might want him to be called). The optative verb (theloi) expresses polite enquiry.

verses.kjv_text: And he asked for a writing table, and wrote, saying, His name is John. And they marvelled all.
verses.original_text: καὶ αἰτήσας πινακίδιον ἔγραψεν λέγων Ἰωάννης ἐστὶν τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐθαύμασαν πάντες
verses.transliteration: kai aitēsas pinakidion egrapsen legōn Iōannēs estin to onoma autou kai ethaumazon pantes
verses.study_note: 'Writing table' (pinakidion — a small writing tablet; diminutive of pinax; a wax-coated wooden tablet on which one wrote with a stylus, then smoothed for reuse). 'Wrote, saying, His name is John' — not 'I want him to be called John' or 'his mother suggests John' but the definitive assertion: 'His name IS John' (Iōannēs estin to onoma autou — John is his name; present tense, simple statement of established fact). Zacharias echoes Elisabeth's certainty. The independent confirmation of the name from both parents — without communication between them — is itself a sign: God has spoken to both. 'They marvelled all' (ethaumazon pantes — they all kept marvelling; imperfect, continuing amazement).

verses.kjv_text: And his mouth was opened immediately, and his tongue loosed, and he spake, and praised God.
verses.original_text: ἀνεῴχθη δὲ τὸ στόμα αὐτοῦ παραχρῆμα καὶ ἡ γλῶσσα αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐλάλει εὐλογῶν τὸν θεόν
verses.transliteration: aneōchthē de to stoma autou parachrēma kai hē glōssa autou kai elalei eulogōn ton theon
verses.study_note: 'His mouth was opened immediately' (aneōchthē to stoma autou parachrēma — his mouth was opened at once; aorist passive, divine action; parachrēma = at that very moment, instantly). The moment Zacharias confirms the divinely given name, his silence ends. The sign-discipline (v.20) is lifted at the moment of full obedience. The loose of his tongue is the reverse of the binding: Gabriel silenced him for unbelief; God opens his mouth upon his act of faith. 'Praised God' (eulogōn ton theon — blessing/praising God; present participle, his first words after nine months of silence are doxology). The Benedictus (vv.67-79) is the extended form of this praise.

verses.kjv_text: And fear came on all that dwelt round about them: and all these sayings were noised abroad throughout all the hill country of Judaea.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο ἐπὶ πάντας φόβος τοὺς περιοικοῦντας αὐτούς καὶ ἐν ὅλῃ τῇ ὀρεινῇ τῆς Ἰουδαίας διελαλεῖτο πάντα τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα
verses.transliteration: kai egeneto epi pantas phobos tous perioikountas autous kai en holē tē oreinē tēs Ioudaias dielaleito panta ta rhēmata tauta
verses.study_note: 'Fear came on all' (egeneto epi pantas phobos — fear came upon all). The reverential awe (phobos) of a community that has witnessed unmistakable divine activity: barrenness overcome, priestly muteness inflicted and released, an angelic visitation confirmed by the healed man's testimony. This is the same fear that marks every divine encounter in Luke (1:12; 2:9; 5:26; 7:16; 8:37; 9:34). 'Noised abroad' (dielaleitō — was talked about, spread by word of mouth; the imperfect suggests ongoing spreading of the report). 'All the hill country of Judaea' — the news of John's miraculous birth spread throughout the region, preparing the soil for his future ministry of repentance.

verses.kjv_text: And all they that heard them laid them up in their hearts, saying, What manner of child shall this be! And the hand of the Lord was with him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔθεντο πάντες οἱ ἀκούσαντες ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ αὐτῶν λέγοντες Τί ἄρα τὸ παιδίον τοῦτο ἔσται καὶ γὰρ χεὶρ κυρίου ἦν μετ᾽ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai ethento pantes hoi akousantes en tē kardia autōn legontes Ti ara to paidion touto estai kai gar cheir kyriou ēn met autou
verses.study_note: 'Laid them up in their hearts' (ethento en tē kardia autōn — placed them in their heart; the heart as the seat of memory and reflection). This is the same response Luke attributes to Mary in 2:19, 51 — treasuring and pondering. 'What manner of child shall this be?' (Ti ara to paidion touto estai — what then will this child be?; ara = 'then,' expressing wonder and inference from all the signs). The question is asked with awe, not scepticism. 'The hand of the Lord was with him' (cheir kyriou ēn met autou — the Lord's hand was with him; an OT phrase for divine empowering, 2 Kings 3:15; Ezra 7:9; Acts 11:21). The narrator confirms what the people wondered: this child is already under divine protection and purpose.

verses.kjv_text: And his father Zacharias was filled with the Holy Ghost, and prophesied, saying,
verses.original_text: Καὶ Ζαχαρίας ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ ἐπλήσθη Πνεύματος Ἁγίου καὶ ἐπροφήτευσεν λέγων
verses.transliteration: Kai Zacharias ho patēr autou eplēsthē Pneumatos Hagiou kai eprophēteusen legōn
verses.study_note: 'Was filled with the Holy Ghost' (eplēsthē Pneumatos Hagiou — was filled with the Holy Spirit; aorist passive, divine action). This is the third Spirit-filling in Luke 1 (after Elisabeth in v.41 and the prenatal John in v.15). 'Prophesied' (eprophēteusen — spoke forth as a prophet; aorist).
