The Benedictus is not merely a thanksgiving hymn but a prophetic declaration: Zacharias, now silent for nine months, opens his mouth under prophetic inspiration to declare what God is doing and will do. The nine months of enforced silence have produced depth of meditation; when the Spirit releases his tongue, the accumulated reflection pours out as prophecy. He who was silenced for unbelief now speaks as a prophet — the most complete turnaround in Luke 1.

verses.kjv_text: Blessed be the Lord God of Israel; for he hath visited and redeemed his people,
verses.original_text: Εὐλογητὸς κύριος ὁ θεὸς τοῦ Ἰσραήλ ὅτι ἐπεσκέψατο καὶ ἐποίησεν λύτρωσιν τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: Eulogētos kyrios ho theos tou Israēl hoti epeskepsato kai epoiēsen lytrōsin tō laō autou
verses.study_note: 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel' (Eulogētos kyrios ho theos tou Israēl — Blessed is the Lord the God of Israel). The Benedictus begins with the Jewish berakah formula ('Blessed be...'). 'Hath visited' (epeskepsato — aorist, he has visited/looked upon; episkeptomai = to visit, inspect, come to care for; used in LXX for God's redemptive coming in Exodus 4:31; Ruth 1:6; Psalm 80:14). The aorist is prophetic perfect: the thing is so certain it is spoken as accomplished. 'Redeemed' (epoiēsen lytrōsin — has performed redemption; lytrōsis = ransoming, setting free by payment; from lytron, ransom price). God's redemption is forensic (legal), costly, and definitively accomplished. This is the great announcement of the Benedictus: the God of Israel has at last come to ransom his people.

verses.kjv_text: And hath raised up an horn of salvation for us in the house of his servant David;
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤγειρεν κέρας σωτηρίας ἡμῖν ἐν οἴκῳ Δαυὶδ παιδὸς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai ēgeiren keras sōtērias hēmin en oikō Dauid paidos autou
verses.study_note: 'Horn of salvation' (keras sōtērias — horn of salvation). The 'horn' in OT imagery is a symbol of strength, power, and dignity (cf. Psalm 18:2; 89:17; 132:17; 1 Samuel 2:1, 10; Hannah's song which prefigures the Benedictus). A raised horn indicated victory and exaltation. 'Raised up' (ēgeiren — has raised up; the same verb used for resurrection). 'In the house of his servant David' (en oikō Dauid paidos autou — in the house of David his servant; pais = servant; cf. Isaiah 37:35). Jesus is the horn of salvation raised up in David's house — fulfilling the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 7:12-16). The 'house of David' in the first century was obscure and politically negligible; God raises his horn precisely here.

verses.kjv_text: As he spake by the mouth of his holy prophets, which have been since the world began:
verses.original_text: καθὼς ἐλάλησεν διὰ στόματος τῶν ἁγίων ἀπ᾽ αἰῶνος προφητῶν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kathōs elalēsen dia stomatos tōn hagiōn ap aiōnos prophētōn autou
verses.study_note: 'As he spake by the mouth of his holy prophets' (kathōs elalēsen dia stomatos tōn hagiōn ap aiōnos prophētōn autou — just as he spoke through the mouth of his holy prophets from of old). 'From the world began' (ap aiōnos — from the age, from ancient times; the KJV rendering 'since the world began' captures the meaning). The coming of Jesus is not a divine improvisation or a late addition to God's plan, but the fulfilment of a purpose spoken through the prophets from the beginning of Israel's history. 'Through the mouth of his holy prophets' — the prophets were the instrument; God was the author. 2 Peter 1:21: 'Holy men of God spake as they were moved by the Holy Ghost.'

verses.kjv_text: That we should be saved from our enemies, and from the hand of all that hate us;
verses.original_text: σωτηρίαν ἐξ ἐχθρῶν ἡμῶν καὶ ἐκ χειρὸς πάντων τῶν μισούντων ἡμᾶς
verses.transliteration: sōtērian ex echthrōn hēmōn kai ek cheiros pantōn tōn misountōn hēmas
verses.study_note: 'Saved from our enemies' (sōtērian ex echthrōn hēmōn — salvation from our enemies). Zacharias describes the messianic salvation in OT political categories — freedom from national enemies. This was the dominant expectation of first-century Judaism. While this is not wrong (the Messiah will ultimately establish universal peace and defeat all enemies), the deeper fulfilment transcends political liberation: the true enemies from which Jesus saves are sin, death, and Satan. The OT imagery of national deliverance is a type and shadow of the greater spiritual deliverance. 'The hand of all that hate us' — the 'hand' is the OT metaphor for power and control. Jesus breaks the power of whatever enslaves his people.

verses.kjv_text: To perform the mercy promised to our fathers, and to remember his holy covenant;
verses.original_text: ποιῆσαι ἔλεος μετὰ τῶν πατέρων ἡμῶν καὶ μνησθῆναι διαθήκης ἁγίας αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: poiēsai eleos meta tōn paterōn hēmōn kai mnēsthēnai diathēkēs hagias autou
verses.study_note: 'To perform the mercy promised to our fathers' (poiēsai eleos meta tōn paterōn hēmōn — to show mercy with our fathers; poiēsai eleos meta = to act with covenantal love/mercy toward). 'Remember his holy covenant' (mnēsthēnai diathēkēs hagias autou — to remember his holy covenant). God remembers not as one who forgets, but in the biblical sense of acting in accordance with what was promised. His covenant memory produces redemptive action. The 'holy covenant' is primarily the Abrahamic covenant (v.73 specifies), but includes all the patriarchal promises and the subsequent Mosaic, Davidic, and New Covenant commitments. The coming of Jesus is God's definitive act of covenant faithfulness.

verses.kjv_text: The oath which he sware to our father Abraham,
verses.original_text: ὅρκον ὃν ὤμοσεν πρὸς Ἀβραὰμ τὸν πατέρα ἡμῶν τοῦ δοῦναι ἡμῖν
verses.transliteration: horkon hon ōmosen pros Abraam ton patera hēmōn tou dounai hēmin
verses.study_note: 'The oath which he sware to our father Abraham' — the specific covenant referred to is the oath of Genesis 22:16-18: 'By myself have I sworn, saith the LORD...I will multiply thy seed as the stars of the heaven...and in thy seed shall all the nations of the earth be blessed.' God swore an oath — the strongest possible divine commitment, anchored in his own character (Hebrews 6:13-18: 'when God made promise to Abraham, because he could swear by no greater, he sware by himself'). 'To our father Abraham' — not just Israel's ancestor but the father of faith, the one in whom all nations find blessing (Galatians 3:6-9). The oath is the unbreakable foundation of all messianic fulfilment.

verses.kjv_text: That he would grant unto us, that we being delivered out of the hand of our enemies might serve him without fear,
verses.original_text: ἀφόβως ἐκ χειρὸς τῶν ἐχθρῶν ἡμῶν ῥυσθέντας λατρεύειν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: aphobōs ek cheiros tōn echthrōn hēmōn rhysthentes latreuein autō
verses.study_note: 'Being delivered out of the hand of our enemies' (ek cheiros tōn echthrōn hēmōn rhysthentes — having been rescued from the hand of our enemies; aorist participle, prior deliverance). 'Might serve him without fear' (aphobōs...latreuein autō — to serve/worship him fearlessly; latreuō = to render religious service, worship, serve as in priestly ministry; this is the goal of redemption: not just freedom from enemies but freedom to worship God). 'Without fear' (aphobōs — fearlessly, without dread). The purpose of the Exodus was always worship: 'Let my people go, that they may serve me' (Exodus 8:1). The purpose of the new exodus in Christ is the same — freedom from enslaving powers so that redeemed humanity may offer God the worship he deserves.

verses.kjv_text: In holiness and righteousness before him, all the days of our life.
verses.original_text: ἐν ὁσιότητι καὶ δικαιοσύνῃ ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ πάσας τὰς ἡμέρας ἡμῶν
verses.transliteration: en hosiotēti kai dikaiosynē enōpion autou pasas tas hēmeras hēmōn
verses.study_note: 'In holiness and righteousness' (en hosiotēti kai dikaiosynē — in holiness and righteousness). Two complementary qualities: holiness (hosiotēs = piety toward God, devotion to the divine) and righteousness (dikaiosynē = right conduct, justice in human relationships). Together they summarise the double love commandment — love for God (holiness) and love for neighbour (righteousness). 'Before him' (enōpion autou — in his presence; all of life lived coram Deo, before the face of God). 'All the days of our life' (pasas tas hēmeras hēmōn — every day of our lives; no vacation from holiness). The redeemed life is not an occasional religious observance but a comprehensive daily orientation toward God in all its dimensions.

verses.kjv_text: And thou, child, shalt be called the prophet of the Highest: for thou shalt go before the face of the Lord to prepare his ways;
verses.original_text: Καὶ σύ παιδίον προφήτης ὑψίστου κληθήσῃ προπορεύσῃ γὰρ ἐνώπιον κυρίου ἑτοιμάσαι ὁδοὺς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: Kai su paidion prophētēs hypsistou klēthēsē proporeusei gar enōpion kyriou hetoimasai hodous autou
verses.study_note: 'Thou, child' — Zacharias now addresses the newborn John directly. The shift from third-person praise to second-person address is deeply moving. 'Prophet of the Highest' (prophētēs hypsistou — prophet of the Most High; contrast with Jesus who is 'Son of the Highest,' v.32). John is not the Son but the herald. 'Go before the face of the Lord' (proporeusei enōpion kyriou — you will go before the Lord; proporeuomai = to go before, precede). The word fulfils Malachi 3:1: 'Behold, I send my messenger, and he shall prepare the way before my face.' 'To prepare his ways' (hetoimasai hodous autou — to prepare his paths/roads; hodous = roads, ways). John's ministry is road-building — removing obstacles, filling valleys, levelling mountains — so that the Lord's coming finds a prepared people.

verses.kjv_text: To give knowledge of salvation unto his people by the remission of their sins,
verses.original_text: τοῦ δοῦναι γνῶσιν σωτηρίας τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ ἐν ἀφέσει ἁμαρτιῶν αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: tou dounai gnōsin sōtērias tō laō autou en aphesei hamartiōn autōn
verses.study_note: 'Give knowledge of salvation' (dounai gnōsin sōtērias — to give knowledge of salvation). John does not save — he announces salvation and calls people to receive it through repentance. 'Knowledge of salvation' (gnōsin sōtērias — experiential knowledge of saving grace; gnōsis here is not mere information but the relational, transformative knowing of the Hebrew yada). 'By the remission of their sins' (en aphesei hamartiōn autōn — in/by the forgiveness/release of their sins; aphesis = release, freedom, forgiveness; used in 3:3 for John's 'baptism of repentance for the remission of sins' and in 24:47 for the preaching of the apostles). The foundation of salvation is forgiveness. John announces the mechanism; Jesus provides it.

verses.kjv_text: Through the tender mercy of our God; whereby the dayspring from on high hath visited us,
verses.original_text: διὰ σπλάγχνα ἐλέους θεοῦ ἡμῶν ἐν οἷς ἐπισκέψεται ἡμᾶς ἀνατολὴ ἐξ ὕψους
verses.transliteration: dia splanchna eleous theou hēmōn en hois episkepsetai hēmas anatolē ex hypsous
verses.study_note: 'Tender mercy' (splanchna eleous — the bowels/entrails of mercy; splanchna = the inner organs, the seat of deep emotion in Hellenistic thought, equivalent to 'heart' in English; the phrase means 'deep, gut-level mercy'). 'Our God' — not an abstract deity but Israel's covenant God, personally and relationally ours. 'The dayspring from on high' (anatolē ex hypsous — the rising/dawn from on high; anatolē = rising of the sun, east, dawn; can also mean 'branch' as in LXX of Zechariah 3:8; 6:12 where the 'Branch' is the messianic title). The double meaning is intentional: Jesus is both the rising sun (Malachi 4:2: 'the Sun of righteousness shall arise with healing in his wings') and the Davidic Branch. 'Hath visited us' (episkepsetai hēmas — will visit us; some MSS have future, some perfect; the future is the more difficult reading). The dawn of God's presence has come at last.

verses.kjv_text: To give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death, to guide our feet into the way of peace.
verses.original_text: ἐπιφᾶναι τοῖς ἐν σκότει καὶ σκιᾷ θανάτου καθημένοις τοῦ κατευθῦναι τοὺς πόδας ἡμῶν εἰς ὁδὸν εἰρήνης
verses.transliteration: epiphanai tois en skotei kai skia thanatou kathēmenois tou kateuthunai tous podas hēmōn eis hodon eirēnēs
verses.study_note: 'Give light to them that sit in darkness and in the shadow of death' (epiphanai tois en skotei kai skia thanatou kathēmenois — to appear/shine upon those who sit in darkness and in the shadow of death). This quotes Isaiah 9:2 (cited also in Matthew 4:16): 'The people that walked in darkness have seen a great light: they that dwell in the land of the shadow of death, upon them hath the light shined.' 'The shadow of death' (skia thanatou — tsalmāwet in Hebrew; a compound of tsel [shadow] and māwet [death]). Those in darkness are not enemies to be avoided but objects of divine compassion — the dayspring shines precisely upon those in deepest darkness. 'Guide our feet into the way of peace' (kateuthunai tous podas hēmōn eis hodon eirēnēs — to guide/direct our feet into the way of peace). The Messiah brings shalom — the comprehensive well-being of restored relationship with God and with one another. Isaiah 9:6: 'his name shall be called...The Prince of Peace.'

verses.kjv_text: And the child grew, and waxed strong in spirit, and was in the deserts till the day of his shewing unto Israel.
verses.original_text: Τὸ δὲ παιδίον ηὔξανεν καὶ ἐκραταιοῦτο πνεύματι καὶ ἦν ἐν ταῖς ἐρήμοις ἕως ἡμέρας ἀναδείξεως αὐτοῦ πρὸς τὸν Ἰσραήλ
verses.transliteration: To de paidion ēuxanen kai ekrataiouto pneumati kai ēn en tais erēmois heōs hēmeras anadeixeōs autou pros ton Israēl
verses.study_note: 'The child grew' (ēuxanen — kept on growing; imperfect, continuous growth). 'Waxed strong in spirit' (ekrataiouto pneumati — grew powerful in spirit; krataiō = to become strong, be empowered; the same word used of Jesus in 2:40). John's development is both physical (grew) and spiritual (strong in spirit). 'Was in the deserts' (ēn en tais erēmois — was in the wilderness regions; plural of erēmos = desert, uninhabited place; not one spot but the wilderness of Judea generally). John's desert formation echoes Moses (Midian), Elijah (wilderness), and Israel (Sinai wilderness). The wilderness is the school of God — solitude, silence, and dependence produce the prophet. 'The day of his shewing unto Israel' (hēmera anadeixeōs autou pros ton Israēl — the day of his public presentation/manifestation to Israel; anadeixis = public appointment, official presentation). This day comes in 3:1-18 when John begins his public ministry. The chapter ends on a note of expectation: everything is in preparation; the moment of revelation approaches.

meta.overview: Luke 2 narrates the birth of Jesus (vv.1-7) in the historical framework of a Roman census under Caesar Augustus, grounding the incarnation in verifiable history. In Bethlehem — the city of David — Mary 'brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes, and laid him in a manger; because there was no room for them in the inn' (v.7). The contrast between the majesty of the eternal Son and the poverty of a manger is the defining paradox of the Incarnation. Angels appear to shepherds in the fields (vv.8-14), announcing 'good tidings of great joy' and directing them to the sign of the swaddled infant. The Gloria in Excelsis (v.14) — 'Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men' — is history's most celebrated angelic doxology. The shepherds go, see, and become the first evangelists (vv.15-20). Eight days later the circumcision and naming of Jesus (v.21) formally incorporate him into the covenant community. At forty days the Presentation in the Temple (vv.22-38) fulfils Mosaic law (Leviticus 12; Exodus 13:2, 12). There Simeon, filled with the Spirit, recognises the infant Jesus as the promised consolation of Israel and the Light of the Gentiles, and warns Mary of the sword to pierce her soul (vv.28-35). Anna, an aged prophetess, also recognises and proclaims him (vv.36-38). The family returns to Nazareth where Jesus grows in wisdom, stature, and favour (v.40). At age twelve the Passover pilgrimage to Jerusalem ends with the boy Jesus found in the temple, confounding the teachers and declaring his first recorded words: 'I must be about my Father's business' (v.49) — an implicit assertion of divine sonship. The chapter closes with the summary of his silent years: 'And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man' (v.52).
meta.theme: The Incarnate Lord — Humility, Worship, and the Silent Years of Growth
meta.keyWordMeaning: Saviour — one who saves, delivers, preserves; from sōzō (to save). The angel announces in v.11: 'unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord.' Sōtēr was a title used in the Graeco-Roman world for beneficent gods and powerful emperors (Augustus was acclaimed sōtēr). Luke deliberately applies the title to a newborn infant in a manger — inverting the imperial claim. The true Saviour does not come with armies and political power but with swaddling clothes and a feeding trough. In the Roman world, Caesar brought 'salvation' through conquest; in Bethlehem, God brings salvation through vulnerable humanity. Sōtēr occurs in Luke at v.11, and will define the entire ministry of Jesus: he seeks and saves the lost (19:10).
outline: The Birth of Jesus in Bethlehem (vv.1-7): Caesar Augustus issues a decree for a census of all the world. Cyrenius is governor of Syria. Joseph goes from Nazareth of Galilee to Bethlehem the city of David with Mary his espoused wife being great with child. While they are there the days are accomplished that she should be delivered. She brings forth her firstborn son and wraps him in swaddling clothes and lays him in a manger because there was no room for them in the inn

The Shepherds and the Angels (vv.8-20): Shepherds are abiding in the field keeping watch over their flock by night. The angel of the Lord comes upon them and the glory of the Lord shines round about them and they are sore afraid. Fear not — for behold I bring you good tidings of great joy which shall be to all people. For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour which is Christ the Lord. And this shall be a sign unto you — ye shall find the babe wrapped in swaddling clothes lying in a manger. Suddenly there is with the angel a multitude of the heavenly host praising God saying — Glory to God in the highest and on earth peace good will toward men. The shepherds go and find Mary and Joseph and the babe lying in a manger. They make known abroad the saying told them. Mary keeps all these things and ponders them in her heart. The shepherds return glorifying and praising God

The Circumcision and Presentation (vv.21-38): At eight days he is circumcised and named Jesus. After the days of purification they bring him to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord and to offer the prescribed sacrifice. Simeon was just and devout waiting for the consolation of Israel and the Holy Ghost was upon him. It was revealed to him that he should not see death before he had seen the Lord's Christ. He comes by the Spirit into the temple. He takes the child in his arms and blesses God — Lord now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace for mine eyes have seen thy salvation a light to lighten the Gentiles and the glory of thy people Israel. Joseph and Mary marvel. Simeon blesses them and says to Mary — a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also. Anna a prophetess of great age served God with fastings and prayers and gave thanks and spoke of him to all them that looked for redemption in Jerusalem
The Return to Nazareth and the Silent Years (vv.39-40): They return to Galilee to Nazareth their own city. The child grows and waxes strong filled with wisdom and the grace of God is upon him

The Boy Jesus in the Temple (vv.41-52): His parents go to Jerusalem every year at the feast of the Passover. When he is twelve years old they go up and after fulfilling the days as they return the child Jesus tarries in Jerusalem. Not knowing this they seek him among their kinsfolk and acquaintance. After three days they find him in the temple sitting in the midst of the doctors both hearing them and asking them questions. All that heard him are astonished at his understanding and answers. His mother says — Son why hast thou thus dealt with us? He says — How is it that ye sought me? Wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business? They do not understand the saying. He goes down to Nazareth and is subject unto them. His mother keeps all these sayings in her heart. Jesus increases in wisdom and stature and in favour with God and man

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass in those days, that there went out a decree from Caesar Augustus, that all the world should be taxed.
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ἐκείναις ἐξῆλθεν δόγμα παρὰ Καίσαρος Αὐγούστου ἀπογράφεσθαι πᾶσαν τὴν οἰκουμένην
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tais hēmerais ekeinais exēlthen dogma para Kaisaros Augoustou apographesthai pasan tēn oikoumenēn
verses.study_note: Luke grounds the birth of Jesus in the broadest political context: Caesar Augustus (born Gaius Octavius, 63 BC-14 AD; nephew and heir of Julius Caesar, Rome's first emperor). 'Decree' (dogma — an official imperial edict; the source of our word 'dogma'). 'All the world' (pasan tēn oikoumenēn — the entire inhabited world; the Roman ecumene, the known civilised world). The census is the human instrument; behind it is divine providence directing history so that the Messiah is born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2). The greatest empire in the world becomes an unwitting servant of the purposes of God. Augustus, who called himself 'saviour' (sōtēr) and 'son of god' (divi filius), issues a decree that sends the true Saviour and true Son of God to his appointed birthplace.

verses.kjv_text: (And this taxing was first made when Cyrenius was governor of Syria.)
verses.original_text: αὕτη ἀπογραφὴ πρώτη ἐγένετο ἡγεμονεύοντος τῆς Συρίας Κυρηνίου
verses.transliteration: hautē apographē prōtē egeneto hēgemoneuontos tēs Syrias Kyrēniou
verses.study_note: This verse has been the subject of extensive historical debate. Quirinius ('Cyrenius') is attested as governor of Syria in 6-7 AD, which seems to conflict with Herod the Great's death in 4 BC (during which the birth occurred). Proposed solutions: (1) The Greek prōtē (first) may mean 'before' — 'this registration took place before Quirinius was governor' (a recognised use of prōtos + genitive in Greek); (2) Quirinius may have held an earlier, unofficial administrative role in Syria before his formal governorship; (3) some manuscript traditions differ. Josephus mentions a census under Quirinius but in a later context. The historical question remains open; Luke's verifiable accuracy in other details (Acts) supports his reliability here.

verses.kjv_text: And all went to be taxed, every one into his own city.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπορεύοντο πάντες ἀπογράφεσθαι ἕκαστος εἰς τὴν ἑαυτοῦ πόλιν
verses.transliteration: kai eporeuonto pantes apographesthai hekastos eis tēn heautou polin
verses.study_note: The census required registration in one's ancestral city ('his own city' = the city of family origin). For Joseph, this meant Bethlehem, the city of David. Roman censuses in Egypt required people to return to their place of origin (attested in papyri from 104 AD and 48 AD). The journey from Nazareth to Bethlehem was approximately 90-100 miles through the hill country. The logistical difficulty for a woman 'great with child' (v.5) makes the journey an act of faith and obedience — both to Roman law and to the providential direction of God toward the prophetically mandated birthplace.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph also went up from Galilee, out of the city of Nazareth, into Judaea, unto the city of David, which is called Bethlehem; (because he was of the house and lineage of David:)
verses.original_text: Ἀνέβη δὲ καὶ Ἰωσὴφ ἀπὸ τῆς Γαλιλαίας ἐκ πόλεως Ναζαρὲθ εἰς τὴν Ἰουδαίαν εἰς πόλιν Δαυὶδ ἥτις καλεῖται Βηθλέεμ διὰ τὸ εἶναι αὐτὸν ἐξ οἴκου καὶ πατριᾶς Δαυίδ
verses.transliteration: Anebē de kai Iōsēph apo tēs Galilaias ek poleōs Nazareth eis tēn Ioudaian eis polin Dauid hētis kaleitai Bēthleem dia to einai auton ex oikou kai patrias Dauid
verses.study_note: 'Went up' (Anebē — ascended; travelling to Jerusalem and the surrounding highlands was always 'going up' geographically). 'City of David...called Bethlehem' — Bethlehem (Heb. Beit Lechem, house of bread) was David's birthplace (1 Samuel 16:1-4; 17:12). Micah 5:2 specifically prophesied: 'But thou, Bethlehem Ephratah, though thou be little among the thousands of Judah, yet out of thee shall he come forth unto me that is to be ruler in Israel.' 'House and lineage of David' (ex oikou kai patrias Dauid — from the house and family/clan of David). Joseph's descent from David is the legal ground for Jesus's Davidic lineage (Matthew 1:1-17). The parenthetical explanation is Luke's aside to Theophilus explaining why Joseph went to this particular city.

verses.kjv_text: To be taxed with Mary his espoused wife, being great with child.
verses.original_text: ἀπογράψασθαι σὺν Μαριὰμ τῇ μεμνηστευμένῃ αὐτῷ οὔσῃ ἐγκύῳ
verses.transliteration: apograpsasthai syn Mariam tē memnēsteumenē autō ousē enkuō
verses.study_note: 'His espoused wife' (tē memnēsteumenē autō — the one betrothed to him; still using the betrothal term, though Matthew 1:24 indicates Joseph had formally taken Mary as his wife by this point). 'Being great with child' (ousē enkuō — being pregnant; enkuos = pregnant, heavy with child). Mary makes the difficult journey in the last stage of pregnancy. Why did she accompany Joseph? Perhaps: (1) she was also required to register; (2) Joseph would not leave her alone in Nazareth where she could face social danger; (3) providential necessity to fulfil the Bethlehem prophecy. The journey, though difficult, is under divine care — the child who is carried is the One who carries all things.

verses.kjv_text: And so it was, that, while they were there, the days were accomplished that she should be delivered.
verses.original_text: ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ εἶναι αὐτοὺς ἐκεῖ ἐπλήσθησαν αἱ ἡμέραι τοῦ τεκεῖν αὐτήν
verses.transliteration: egeneto de en tō einai autous ekei eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou tekein autēn
verses.study_note: 'The days were accomplished' (eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou tekein autēn — the days for her to give birth were fulfilled; plēroō = to fulfil, complete; the same word Luke consistently uses for the fulfilment of divine purposes). The birth happens at the appointed time and in the appointed place. Galatians 4:4: 'But when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law.' The Greek word plēroō (fulfilled) applied to Mary's days of pregnancy echoes the same word applied to the fulfilment of Scripture — Luke is signalling that this birth is the completion of a long divine countdown begun with the Abrahamic promise.

verses.kjv_text: And she brought forth her firstborn son, and wrapped him in swaddling clothes, and laid him in a manger; because there was no room for them in the inn.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔτεκεν τὸν υἱὸν αὐτῆς τὸν πρωτότοκον καὶ ἐσπαργάνωσεν αὐτὸν καὶ ἀνέκλινεν αὐτὸν ἐν φάτνῃ διότι οὐκ ἦν αὐτοῖς τόπος ἐν τῷ καταλύματι
verses.transliteration: kai eteken ton huion autēs ton prōtotokon kai espargānōsen auton kai aneklinen auton en phatnē dioti ouk ēn autois topos en tō katalymati
verses.study_note: 'Firstborn' (prōtotokon — firstborn; prōtotokos carries legal and theological weight: the firstborn belongs to God, Exodus 13:2; the firstborn inherits the double portion; Jesus as the Father's firstborn, Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:6). 'Swaddling clothes' (espargānōsen — wrapped in strips of cloth; sparganon = a swaddling band used to wrap newborns to keep limbs straight). This practice is referenced in Wisdom 7:4-5 where Solomon says he was swaddled like any other child. The swaddling is the sign given to the shepherds (v.12): not royal robes or a magnificent cradle, but the same cloth used for any poor Jewish baby. 'Manger' (phatnē — a feeding trough for animals; used twice more as the sign in v.12, 16). 'No room in the inn' (katalyma — a guest room, lodging place; could be a caravanserai, a travellers' inn, or a household guest room). The Creator of the universe, born among animals, cradled in a feeding trough: the ultimate act of divine condescension. 2 Corinthians 8:9: 'though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor.'

verses.kjv_text: And there were in the same country shepherds abiding in the field, keeping watch over their flock by night.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ποιμένες ἦσαν ἐν τῇ χώρᾳ τῇ αὐτῇ ἀγραυλοῦντες καὶ φυλάσσοντες φυλακὰς τῆς νυκτὸς ἐπὶ τὴν ποίμνην αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: Kai poimenes ēsan en tē chōra tē autē agraulountes kai phylassontes phylakas tēs nyktos epi tēn poimnēn autōn
verses.study_note: 'Shepherds' (poimenes — shepherds; Jesus will call himself the Good Shepherd, poimēn, in John 10:11). Shepherds were socially marginal in first-century Judaism — their occupation prevented them from observing strict purity laws, their testimony was not accepted in court. Yet they are the first recipients of the angelic announcement. This is Luke's characteristic reversal: the mighty Caesar issues a decree; God chooses shepherds as the first evangelists. 'Abiding in the field' (agraulountes — camping/living outdoors; a term for those sleeping in the open countryside). 'Keeping watch by night' (phylassontes phylakas tēs nyktos — keeping the night watches). The time of year is debated; the Mishnah mentions lambs for temple use being pastured near Bethlehem year-round.

verses.kjv_text: And, lo, the angel of the Lord came upon them, and the glory of the Lord shone round about them: and they were sore afraid.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἄγγελος κυρίου ἐπέστη αὐτοῖς καὶ δόξα κυρίου περιέλαμψεν αὐτούς καὶ ἐφοβήθησαν φόβον μέγαν
verses.transliteration: kai angelos kyriou epestē autois kai doxa kyriou perielamsen autous kai ephobēthēsan phobon megan
verses.study_note: 'Came upon them' (epestē — stood over them, appeared suddenly; ephistēmi = to come upon suddenly, as of angelic appearance). 'The glory of the Lord shone round about them' (doxa kyriou perielamsen autous — the glory of the Lord shone around them; the Shekinah glory, the visible manifestation of God's presence in radiant light; the same glory that filled the tabernacle, Exodus 40:34-35, and the temple, 1 Kings 8:10-11). 'Sore afraid' (ephobēthēsan phobon megan — they feared a great fear; the cognate accusative intensifying the terror). The shepherds' response is the human reaction to unexpected divine glory in a fallen world — the glory that was lost in Eden breaks in again, and its first effect is overwhelming dread.

verses.kjv_text: And the angel said unto them, Fear not: for, behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy, which shall be to all people.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς ὁ ἄγγελος Μὴ φοβεῖσθε ἰδοὺ γὰρ εὐαγγελίζομαι ὑμῖν χαρὰν μεγάλην ἥτις ἔσται παντὶ τῷ λαῷ
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autois ho angelos Mē phobeisthe idou gar euangelizomai hymin charan megalēn hētis estai panti tō laō
verses.study_note: 'Fear not' — the third 'Fear not' in Luke 1-2 (after 1:13 and 1:30), always preceding a divine announcement. 'I bring you good tidings' (euangelizomai — I am proclaiming the gospel/good news; euangelizomai in its most decisive moment). 'Good tidings of great joy' (charan megalēn — great joy; chara = the uniquely Christian experience of joy rooted in the grace of God). 'Which shall be to all people' (hētis estai panti tō laō — which will be for all the people; laos used here probably of Israel primarily, but expanding to all nations in 3:6: 'all flesh shall see the salvation of God'). The angel's message is not a private communication to these shepherds but a universal announcement that uses these shepherds as its first human recipients.

verses.kjv_text: For unto you is born this day in the city of David a Saviour, which is Christ the Lord.
verses.original_text: ὅτι ἐτέχθη ὑμῖν σήμερον σωτὴρ ὅς ἐστιν Χριστὸς κύριος ἐν πόλει Δαυίδ
verses.transliteration: hoti etechthē hymin sēmeron sōtēr hos estin Christos kyrios en polei Dauid
verses.study_note: The most compact and comprehensive christological confession in the NT: three titles in one verse. 'Saviour' (sōtēr — see chapter overview). 'Christ' (Christos — the Anointed One; the Greek translation of Heb. Māshiaḥ, Messiah). 'The Lord' (kyrios — the supreme title; in the LXX, kyrios translates YHWH, the divine Name). The combination 'Christ the Lord' (Christos Kyrios) is unique in the NT — Jesus is both the long-awaited Messiah and the LORD himself. 'Born this day' (etechthē sēmeron — was born today; aorist passive: the birth is a divine act, not merely a biological event). 'In the city of David' — Bethlehem, fulfilling Micah 5:2. 'Unto you' (hymin — for you, to you; the gift is personal and communal: for these shepherds and for all Israel and all humanity).

verses.kjv_text: And this shall be a sign unto you; Ye shall find the babe wrapped in swaddling clothes, lying in a manger.
verses.original_text: καὶ τοῦτο ὑμῖν τὸ σημεῖον εὑρήσετε βρέφος ἐσπαργανωμένον καὶ κεῖμενον ἐν φάτνῃ
verses.transliteration: kai touto hymin to sēmeion heurēsete brephos espargānōmenon kai keimenon en phatnē
verses.study_note: 'A sign unto you' (to sēmeion hymin — the sign for you; sēmeion = a confirming, distinguishing mark). The sign is not spectacular power or royal grandeur but humble specificity: a baby wrapped in swaddling cloths in a manger. 'Brephos' — the word for the unborn John (1:41, 44) and Mary's baby in the manger: the same word for both, affirming the continuity of personal identity from conception through birth. The manger (phatnē) is the identifying detail: in Bethlehem that night there was only one baby in a feeding trough. The sign deliberately inverts royal expectations. Isaiah 7:14 promised 'Immanuel'; the sign is not a palace nursery but a stable's manger — and this is exactly right, for this is the God who exalts the humble.

verses.kjv_text: And suddenly there was with the angel a multitude of the heavenly host praising God, and saying,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξαίφνης ἐγένετο σὺν τῷ ἀγγέλῳ πλῆθος στρατιᾶς οὐρανίου αἰνούντων τὸν θεὸν καὶ λεγόντων
verses.transliteration: kai exaiphnēs egeneto syn tō angelō plēthos stratias ouraniou ainountōn ton theon kai legontōn
verses.study_note: 'Suddenly' (exaiphnēs — suddenly, unexpectedly; the speed and surprise of the divine breaking in). 'A multitude of the heavenly host' (plēthos stratias ouraniou — a great number of the heavenly army; stratia = army, host; the word gives us our term 'stratagem'). The 'heavenly host' (tseva hashamayim in Hebrew) is the vast angelic army of God (1 Kings 22:19; Psalm 148:2; Revelation 5:11-12). At creation God spoke alone; at the incarnation heaven's armies appear. One angel announced; a multitude celebrates. 'Praising God' (ainountōn ton theon — continuously praising God; present participle). The heavenly worship that fills eternity breaks through into time and space over the fields of Bethlehem.

verses.kjv_text: Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men.
verses.original_text: Δόξα ἐν ὑψίστοις θεῷ καὶ ἐπὶ γῆς εἰρήνη ἐν ἀνθρώποις εὐδοκίας
verses.transliteration: Doxa en hypsistois theō kai epi gēs eirēnē en anthrōpois eudokias
verses.study_note: The Gloria in Excelsis — the angels' doxology forms a couplet: (1) God receives glory in the highest realms; (2) earth receives peace. 'Glory to God in the highest' (Doxa en hypsistois theō — glory in the highest places to God; God is glorified in his own heavenly domain at the moment of the incarnation). 'On earth peace' (epi gēs eirēnē — on earth, peace; the shalom of the messianic age, Isaiah 9:6-7). 'Good will toward men' (en anthrōpois eudokias — among men of goodwill/good pleasure; eudokia = divine good pleasure, favour; the genitive is best read as 'men upon whom his favour rests' — those chosen by grace). The peace the angels announce is not a political truce but the peace of reconciliation: God and humanity at peace through the work of the Prince of Peace. This peace is not universal yet in experience but is established in principle in the incarnation.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, as the angels were gone away from them into heaven, the shepherds said one to another, Let us now go even unto Bethlehem, and see this thing which is come to pass, which the Lord hath made known unto us.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ὡς ἀπῆλθον ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν εἰς τὸν οὐρανὸν οἱ ἄγγελοι καὶ οἱ ποιμένες ἐλάλουν πρὸς ἀλλήλους Διέλθωμεν δὴ ἕως Βηθλέεμ καὶ ἴδωμεν τὸ ῥῆμα τοῦτο τὸ γεγονὸς ὃ ὁ κύριος ἐγνώρισεν ἡμῖν
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto hōs apēlthen ap autōn eis ton ouranon hoi angeloi kai hoi poimenes elaloun pros allēlous Dielthōmen dē heōs Bēthleem kai idōmen to rhēma touto to gegonosein ho ho kyrios egnōrisen hēmin
verses.study_note: 'Let us now go' (Dielthōmen dē — let us go through, let us pass over; dē = the particle of urgency and decisiveness: 'let us go at once'). 'This thing which is come to pass' (to rhēma touto to gegonosein — this word/event which has come to pass; rhēma = word, event, thing; a Hebrew idiom where 'word' and 'event' overlap: the angel's word has become an event). 'Which the Lord hath made known' (ho ho kyrios egnōrisen hēmin — which the Lord has made known to us). The shepherds' immediate response is obedience and investigation — they do not debate the vision but act on it. Their response is the model of faith: hearing the word, they go to verify the reality. The angel's announcement becomes the summons to seek.

verses.kjv_text: And they came with haste, and found Mary, and Joseph, and the babe lying in a manger.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦλθαν σπεύσαντες καὶ ἀνεῦρον τήν τε Μαριὰμ καὶ τὸν Ἰωσὴφ καὶ τὸ βρέφος κείμενον ἐν τῇ φάτνῃ
verses.transliteration: kai ēlthan speusantes kai aneuron tēn te Mariam kai ton Iōsēph kai to brephos keimenon en tē phatnē
verses.study_note: 'With haste' (speusantes — having hurried; the same word as Mary's 'with haste' in 1:39; urgency marks the response to divine announcement). 'Found' (aneuron — discovered, found after searching; ana + heuriskō = found by going and searching). They searched Bethlehem's stable(s) and found the sign exactly as described: Mary, Joseph, and the baby in a manger. The finding is the verification of faith: they went on the word of the angel, and the word proved true. Note the order: 'Mary, and Joseph, and the babe' — Luke preserves Mary's prominence. The manger is named for the third time (vv.7, 12, 16) — Luke's triple emphasis on the poverty and humility of the setting.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had seen it, they made known abroad the saying which was told them concerning this child.
verses.original_text: ἰδόντες δὲ ἐγνώρισαν περὶ τοῦ ῥήματος τοῦ λαληθέντος αὐτοῖς περὶ τοῦ παιδίου τούτου
verses.transliteration: idontes de egnōrisan peri tou rhēmatos tou lalēthentos autois peri tou paidiou toutou
verses.study_note: 'When they had seen' (idontes — having seen; aorist: they saw and then acted). 'They made known abroad' (egnōrisan — they made known, spread the word; from gnōrizō = to make known, publish). The shepherds become the first preachers of the incarnation. They were received the message, verified it by sight, and immediately proclaimed it. This is the pattern of genuine witness: personal encounter produces public proclamation. The shepherds did not need theological training or official authorization — they had seen the Lord, and they could not remain silent. Their evangelism is informal, local, and immediate — and it is exactly the kind Jesus commissions throughout Luke.

verses.kjv_text: And all they that heard it wondered at those things which were told them by the shepherds.
verses.original_text: καὶ πάντες οἱ ἀκούσαντες ἐθαύμασαν περὶ τῶν λαληθέντων ὑπὸ τῶν ποιμένων πρὸς αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: kai pantes hoi akousantes ethaumazon peri tōn lalēthentos hypo tōn poimenōn pros autous
verses.study_note: 'All they that heard it wondered' (pantes hoi akousantes ethaumazon — all those who heard kept on marvelling; imperfect, continuous amazement). The response of the hearers is wonder — the natural human reaction to the intersection of the divine with the ordinary. Wonder is the beginning of worship. The shepherds' audience cannot dismiss the story: it is specific (a baby in a manger in Bethlehem), verifiable (go and see), and miraculous (angels appeared and spoke). The marvelling does not yet become faith for most, but the seed is planted. Luke records the response without judgment: wonder is at least the beginning of a conversation with the divine.

verses.kjv_text: But Mary kept all these things, and pondered them in her heart.
verses.original_text: ἡ δὲ Μαριὰμ πάντα συνετήρει τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα συμβάλλουσα ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: hē de Mariam panta synetērei ta rhēmata tauta symballousa en tē kardia autēs
verses.study_note: 'Mary kept' (synetērei — was keeping, preserving carefully; sun + tēreō = to keep together, to guard closely; imperfect, continuous action). 'Pondered' (symballousa — throwing together, comparing; symballō = to cast together, to turn over in the mind, to consider; present participle: an ongoing, active mental engagement). 'In her heart' (en tē kardia autēs — in her heart, in her inner life). Mary does not merely store information; she actively processes it, compares each event with others, and meditates on the meaning. This is one of two such notes in Luke (cf. 2:51). Mary is presented as the primary source for Luke's infancy narrative — it is her pondering and preserving that makes the account possible. The eyewitness tradition (1:2) runs through Mary.

verses.kjv_text: And the shepherds returned, glorifying and praising God for all the things that they had heard and seen, as it was told unto them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὑπέστρεψαν οἱ ποιμένες δοξάζοντες καὶ αἰνοῦντες τὸν θεὸν ἐπὶ πᾶσιν οἷς ἤκουσαν καὶ εἶδον καθὼς ἐλαλήθη πρὸς αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: kai hypestrepsan hoi poimenes doxazontes kai ainountes ton theon epi pasin hois ēkousan kai eidon kathōs elalēthē pros autous
verses.study_note: 'Glorifying and praising God' (doxazontes kai ainountes ton theon — glorifying and praising God; the same two verbs used of the heavenly host, vv.13-14). The shepherds return to their flocks but return transformed — worship is now their mode of being. 'For all the things that they had heard and seen' (epi pasin hois ēkousan kai eidon — upon all which they had heard and seen). Faith is grounded in both hearing (the word) and seeing (the reality). 'As it was told unto them' (kathōs elalēthē pros autous — just as it was spoken to them). The correspondence between the angelic word and the earthly reality confirms the integrity of the revelation. Their doxology echoes the angelic 'Glory to God' — what was sung in heaven is now echoed on earth.

verses.kjv_text: And when eight days were accomplished for the circumcising of the child, his name was called JESUS, which was so named of the angel before he was conceived in the womb.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὅτε ἐπλήσθησαν ἡμέραι ὀκτὼ τοῦ περιτεμεῖν αὐτόν καὶ ἐκλήθη τὸ ὄνομα αὐτοῦ Ἰησοῦς τὸ κληθὲν ὑπὸ τοῦ ἀγγέλου πρὸ τοῦ συλληφθῆναι αὐτὸν ἐν τῇ κοιλίᾳ
verses.transliteration: Kai hote eplēsthēsan hēmerai oktō tou peritemein auton kai eklēthē to onoma autou Iēsous to klēthen hypo tou angelou pro tou syllēphthēnai auton en tē koilia
verses.study_note: 'Eight days' — circumcision was mandated on the eighth day by Mosaic law (Leviticus 12:3; cf. Genesis 17:12). Jesus is fully incorporated into the covenant community of Israel from infancy — he was 'made under the law' (Galatians 4:4) from the very beginning. 'His name was called JESUS' — the naming at circumcision combined the covenantal sign with the formal giving of the name. 'Which was so named of the angel before he was conceived' — Luke refers to both angelic announcements (Gabriel to Mary in 1:31 and to Joseph in Matthew 1:21). The name precedes conception — Jesus's identity and mission were determined before his earthly existence. The naming is an act of submission to divine authority: God named him before he was born.

verses.kjv_text: And when the days of her purification according to the law of Moses were accomplished, they brought him to Jerusalem, to present him to the Lord;
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὅτε ἐπλήσθησαν αἱ ἡμέραι τοῦ καθαρισμοῦ αὐτῶν κατὰ τὸν νόμον Μωϋσέως ἀνήγαγον αὐτὸν εἰς Ἱεροσόλυμα παραστῆσαι τῷ κυρίῳ
verses.transliteration: Kai hote eplēsthēsan hai hēmerai tou katharismou autōn kata ton nomon Mōuseōs anēgagon auton eis Hierosolyma parastēsai tō kyriō
verses.study_note: 'Days of her purification' (hēmerai tou katharismou — the days of the purification; Leviticus 12:1-8 prescribed 40 days of purification after the birth of a son: 7 days of impurity + 33 more days before the purification offering). 'According to the law of Moses' — Luke emphasises repeatedly in this section (vv.22, 23, 24, 27, 39) that the holy family scrupulously obeyed the Mosaic law. Jesus came not to abolish but to fulfil (Matthew 5:17). 'To present him to the Lord' (parastēsai tō kyriō — to stand him before the Lord; the presentation in the temple echoes Hannah's presentation of Samuel in 1 Samuel 1:24-28). The One who is Lord of the temple is presented in the temple by his parents as a baby — the ultimate act of divine condescension.

verses.kjv_text: (As it is written in the law of the Lord, Every male that openeth the womb shall be called holy to the Lord;)
verses.original_text: καθὼς γέγραπται ἐν νόμῳ κυρίου ὅτι Πᾶν ἄρσεν διανοῖγον μήτραν ἅγιον τῷ κυρίῳ κληθήσεται
verses.transliteration: kathōs gegraptai en nomō kyriou hoti Pan arsen dianoigon mētran hagion tō kyriō klēthēsetai
verses.study_note: 'As it is written' (kathōs gegraptai — just as it stands written; perfect passive: permanently written, always applicable). Luke quotes Exodus 13:2, 12-15 concerning the redemption of the firstborn. Every firstborn male was considered God's property (going back to the Passover night when the firstborn of Egypt died but the firstborn of Israel were spared by the blood of the lamb). 'Holy to the Lord' (hagion tō kyriō — consecrated to the Lord). In this verse's ultimate irony: Jesus, who is himself holy by nature, is presented and declared holy to the Lord — as if the One who is the source of all holiness must be consecrated to himself. He submits to the law he himself gave.

verses.kjv_text: And to offer a sacrifice according to that which is said in the law of the Lord, A pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons.
verses.original_text: καὶ τοῦ δοῦναι θυσίαν κατὰ τὸ εἰρημένον ἐν τῷ νόμῳ κυρίου ζεῦγος τρυγόνων ἢ δύο νοσσοὺς περιστερῶν
verses.transliteration: kai tou dounai thysian kata to eirēmenon en tō nomō kyriou zeugos trygonōn ē dyo nossous peristerōn
verses.study_note: 'A pair of turtledoves, or two young pigeons' — this is the purification offering prescribed for those who could not afford a lamb (Leviticus 12:8: 'And if she be not able to bring a lamb, then she shall bring two turtles, or two young pigeons'). The substitution of birds for a lamb is the provision for the poor. Joseph and Mary were poor — they offered the minimum permissible sacrifice. The One who would himself become the Lamb of God was offered by parents who brought the poverty-offering. He who is the Lamb requires the birds that substitute for a lamb — the poor man's offering for the gift of the Son of God. 2 Corinthians 8:9: 'though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor.'

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, there was a man in Jerusalem, whose name was Simeon; and the same man was just and devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel: and the Holy Ghost was upon him.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄνθρωπος ἦν ἐν Ἱερουσαλὴμ ᾧ ὄνομα Συμεών καὶ ὁ ἄνθρωπος οὗτος δίκαιος καὶ εὐλαβής προσδεχόμενος παράκλησιν τοῦ Ἰσραήλ καὶ Πνεῦμα ἦν Ἅγιον ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: Kai idou anthrōpos ēn en Hierousalēm hō onoma Symeōn kai ho anthrōpos houtos dikaios kai eulabēs prosdechomenos paraklēsin tou Israēl kai Pneuma ēn Hagion ep auton
verses.study_note: 'Simeon' — not identified with any other biblical Simeon. 'Just and devout' (dikaios kai eulabēs — righteous and godly/careful; eulabēs = one who takes hold of God carefully, devout, circumspect in religious matters; the root is the same as our word 'circumspection'). 'Waiting for the consolation of Israel' (prosdechomenos paraklēsin tou Israēl — awaiting the consolation/comfort of Israel; paraklēsis = encouragement, comfort, consolation). The 'consolation of Israel' is a Messianic title based on Isaiah 40:1: 'Comfort ye, comfort ye my people, saith your God.' Simeon embodies the faithful remnant of Israel: righteous, devout, and looking with patient hope for God's promised intervention. 'The Holy Ghost was upon him' — the Spirit of prophecy rested on Simeon, giving him revelatory access to divine purposes.

verses.kjv_text: And it was revealed unto him by the Holy Ghost, that he should not see death, before he had seen the Lord's Christ.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦν αὐτῷ κεχρηματισμένον ὑπὸ τοῦ Πνεύματος τοῦ Ἁγίου μὴ ἰδεῖν θάνατον πρὶν ἢ ἴδῃ τὸν Χριστὸν κυρίου
verses.transliteration: kai ēn autō kechrēmatismenon hypo tou Pneumatos tou Hagiou mē idein thanaton prin ē idē ton Christon kyriou
verses.study_note: 'It was revealed' (kechrēmatismenon — having been divinely instructed/warned; chrēmatizō = to receive a divine communication, oracle, or warning; the same word used in Matthew 2:12 for the Magi's dream-warning and Acts 10:22 for Cornelius's angelic message). This is a specific, personal promise from the Holy Spirit to Simeon: he will not die before seeing the Lord's Anointed. The promise is extraordinary: a mortal man given divine assurance that death would wait. 'Lord's Christ' (ton Christon kyriou — the Anointed of the Lord; the divine genitive: the Messiah whom the LORD himself has appointed and sent). Simeon is the old man of Israel's expectation — faithful, Spirit-endowed, held in life by a promise until he can embrace what he has waited for.

verses.kjv_text: And he came by the Spirit into the temple: and when the parents brought in the child Jesus, to do for him after the custom of the law,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦλθεν ἐν τῷ Πνεύματι εἰς τὸ ἱερόν καὶ ἐν τῷ εἰσαγαγεῖν τοὺς γονεῖς τὸ παιδίον Ἰησοῦν τοῦ ποιῆσαι αὐτοὺς κατὰ τὸ εἰθισμένον τοῦ νόμου περὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai ēlthen en tō Pneumati eis to hieron kai en tō eisagagein tous goneis to paidion Iēsoun tou poiēsai autous kata to eithismenon tou nomou peri autou
verses.study_note: 'He came by the Spirit' (ēlthen en tō Pneumati — he came in/by the Spirit; the Spirit directed his movement to the temple at precisely the right moment). The timing is providential: of all the days and hours when people came to the temple, the Spirit guides Simeon to arrive at the exact moment when the infant Jesus is being presented. 'Into the temple' (eis to hieron — the temple complex, hieron; not naon, the sanctuary building). 'The parents' (tous goneis — the parents; Luke uses the legal term for Joseph and Mary as Jesus's parents by law). 'Custom of the law' (kata to eithismenon tou nomou — according to the established custom of the law). Everything is done in covenantal order; into this ordered piety, the Spirit brings the moment of recognition.

verses.kjv_text: Then took he him up in his arms, and blessed God, and said,
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς ἐδέξατο αὐτὸ εἰς τὰς ἀγκάλας αὐτοῦ καὶ εὐλόγησεν τὸν θεὸν καὶ εἶπεν
verses.transliteration: kai autos edexato auto eis tas ankalas autou kai eulogēsen ton theon kai eipen
verses.study_note: 'Took him up in his arms' (edexato auto eis tas ankalas autou — received him into his arms; ankalai = the arms as a cradle; the arms bent to carry). Simeon takes the infant in his aged arms — the old man of Israel's hope holding the fulfilment of Israel's hope. The image is one of the most tender in Scripture: the long-faithful, long-waiting servant finally embracing what he has longed for. He does not hold the child and examine him; he holds the child and blesses God. The proper response to the presence of Jesus is always worship. 'Blessed God' (eulogēsen ton theon — blessed/praised God). Simeon's response mirrors Zacharias's Benedictus (1:68): 'Blessed be the Lord God of Israel.'

verses.kjv_text: Lord, now lettest thou thy servant depart in peace, according to thy word:
verses.original_text: Νῦν ἀπολύεις τὸν δοῦλόν σου δέσποτα κατὰ τὸ ῥῆμά σου ἐν εἰρήνῃ
verses.transliteration: Nyn apolyeis ton doulon sou despota kata to rhēma sou en eirēnē
verses.study_note: The Nunc Dimittis ('Now dismiss') — Simeon's canticle (vv.29-32) is the third great hymn of Luke 1-2 (after the Magnificat and Benedictus). 'Now lettest thou...depart in peace' (Nyn apolyeis...en eirēnē — now you release in peace; apolyō = to dismiss, release, let go; the word used for releasing a prisoner or a servant from duty). Simeon is ready to die — not with resignation but with deep satisfaction. The promise is fulfilled; the mission is complete. 'According to thy word' (kata to rhēma sou — in accordance with your word; the promise of v.26). The peace Simeon experiences is shalom — completeness, rightness, the cessation of the long tension of unfulfilled hope. He who waited for the 'consolation of Israel' (paraklēsis) has found it in this infant.

verses.kjv_text: For mine eyes have seen thy salvation,
verses.original_text: ὅτι εἶδον οἱ ὀφθαλμοί μου τὸ σωτήριόν σου
verses.transliteration: hoti eidon hoi ophthalmoi mou to sōtērion sou
verses.study_note: 'Mine eyes have seen thy salvation' (eidon hoi ophthalmoi mou to sōtērion sou — my eyes have seen your salvation). Simeon holds the infant and looks at him: 'your salvation' (to sōtērion sou — the saving action of God personified; neuter form, 'the salvation'; used in the LXX of Psalm 98:2-3 which promises that 'the LORD hath made known his salvation...all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God'). Simeon does not say 'I have seen a saviour' but 'I have seen thy salvation' — in this baby, God's own saving act is present in person. This is not prophecy about what will happen but recognition of what is already here: salvation has arrived in the form of a forty-day-old infant.

verses.kjv_text: Which thou hast prepared before the face of all people;
verses.original_text: ὃ ἡτοίμασας κατὰ πρόσωπον πάντων τῶν λαῶν
verses.transliteration: ho hētoimasas kata prosōpon pantōn tōn laōn
verses.study_note: 'Which thou hast prepared' (hētoimasas — you have prepared; aorist: the preparation is complete). 'Before the face of all people' (kata prosōpon pantōn tōn laōn — before the face of all the peoples; laōn = peoples, plural). The salvation Simeon holds is not for Israel alone but 'before all peoples' — visible, public, available to all nations. This anticipates the Great Commission (24:47) and the mission of Acts. Simeon's vision breaks out of any narrow nationalism: the infant in his arms is the hope of every people, tribe, tongue, and nation. Isaiah 52:10: 'The LORD hath made bare his holy arm in the eyes of all the nations; and all the ends of the earth shall see the salvation of our God.'

verses.kjv_text: A light to lighten the Gentiles, and the glory of thy people Israel.
verses.original_text: φῶς εἰς ἀποκάλυψιν ἐθνῶν καὶ δόξαν λαοῦ σου Ἰσραήλ
verses.transliteration: phōs eis apokalypsin ethnōn kai doxan laou sou Israēl
verses.study_note: 'A light to lighten the Gentiles' (phōs eis apokalypsin ethnōn — a light for the revelation/unveiling of the Gentiles/nations). Quoting Isaiah 42:6 and 49:6: 'I will also give thee for a light to the Gentiles, that thou mayest be my salvation unto the end of the earth.' 'Apokalypsin' = revelation, unveiling (our word 'apocalypse'): Jesus brings to the Gentiles what was hidden from them — the knowledge of the true God and his salvation. 'The glory of thy people Israel' (doxan laou sou Israēl — the glory of your people Israel). Israel's glory is not national power but the fact that it is through Israel's Messiah, born of Israel, that salvation comes to all peoples. The sequence is significant: Luke places the Gentiles first (light to the nations) and then Israel (their glory) — anticipating the mission direction of Acts.

verses.kjv_text: And Joseph and his mother marvelled at those things which were spoken of him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦν ὁ πατὴρ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἡ μήτηρ θαυμάζοντες ἐπὶ τοῖς λαλουμένοις περὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai ēn ho patēr autou kai hē mētēr thaumazontes epi tois laloumenois peri autou
verses.study_note: 'His father' (ho patēr autou — his father; Luke uses the legal term for Joseph as Jesus's father by law and by the community's understanding, without contradicting the virginal conception). 'Marvelled' (thaumazontes — continuing to be astonished; imperfect). Though Mary has received two angelic announcements, the birth, the shepherds' visit, and the Magi's visit (in Matthew), she still marvels at each new revelation. This is not unbelief but the inexhaustible wonder of encountering the depth of God's purposes. Each new word about Jesus reveals dimensions that were not fully grasped before. The parents are always discovering new facets of who their son is.

verses.kjv_text: And Simeon blessed them, and said unto Mary his mother, Behold, this child is set for the fall and rising again of many in Israel; and for a sign which shall be spoken against;
verses.original_text: καὶ εὐλόγησεν αὐτοὺς Συμεὼν καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς Μαριὰμ τὴν μητέρα αὐτοῦ Ἰδοὺ οὗτος κεῖται εἰς πτῶσιν καὶ ἀνάστασιν πολλῶν ἐν τῷ Ἰσραὴλ καὶ εἰς σημεῖον ἀντιλεγόμενον
verses.transliteration: kai eulogēsen autous Symeōn kai eipen pros Mariam tēn mētēra autou Idou houtos keitai eis ptōsin kai anastasin pollōn en tō Israēl kai eis sēmeion antilegomenon
verses.study_note: 'Set for the fall and rising again' (keitai eis ptōsin kai anastasin — is set/appointed for the falling and the rising of many). Jesus is the great divider of humanity: some fall before him in judgment (ptōsis); others rise by him in salvation (anastasis). Isaiah 8:14-15 predicted the Messiah as 'a stone of stumbling and rock of offence' to both houses of Israel. The same stone that causes some to fall is the corner stone upon which others build (Psalm 118:22; Isaiah 28:16; cf. 1 Peter 2:6-8). 'A sign which shall be spoken against' (sēmeion antilegomenon — a sign that is contradicted, disputed, opposed). Jesus will be the most controversial figure in human history — the one everyone must reckon with, and many will reject (cf. John 1:11).

verses.kjv_text: (Yea, a sword shall pierce through thy own soul also,) that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed.
verses.original_text: καὶ σοῦ δὲ αὐτῆς τὴν ψυχὴν διελεύσεται ῥομφαία ὅπως ἂν ἀποκαλυφθῶσιν ἐκ πολλῶν καρδιῶν διαλογισμοί
verses.transliteration: kai sou de autēs tēn psychēn dieleussetai rhomphaia hopōs an apokaluphthōsin ek pollōn kardiōn dialogismoi
verses.study_note: 'A sword shall pierce through thy own soul also' (sou de autēs tēn psychēn dieleussetai rhomphaia — a sword will also pass through your own soul; rhomphaia = a large, broad Thracian sword; different from machaira, a shorter sword). Mary's joy will be mingled with anguish — the anguish reaches its climax at the cross (John 19:25-27). Yet even in the cross, the sword serves a purpose: 'that the thoughts of many hearts may be revealed' (hopōs an apokaluphthōsin dialogismoi — so that the deliberations/intentions of many hearts might be exposed). Jesus does not merely bring salvation and judgment — his presence forces every human heart to reveal itself. The cross, which pierces Mary's soul, reveals what every person truly thinks and feels about God, sin, and salvation. No one can stand before the crucified Christ and remain unchanged.

verses.kjv_text: And there was one Anna, a prophetess, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Aser: she was of a great age, and had lived with an husband seven years from her virginity;
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἦν Ἅννα προφῆτις θυγάτηρ Φανουήλ ἐκ φυλῆς Ἀσήρ αὕτη προβεβηκυῖα ἐν ἡμέραις πολλαῖς ζήσασα μετὰ ἀνδρὸς ἔτη ἑπτὰ ἀπὸ τῆς παρθενίας αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: Kai ēn Hanna prophētis thygatēr Phanouel ek phylēs Asēr hautē probebēkuia en hēmerais pollais zēsasa meta andros etē hepta apo tēs parthenias autēs
verses.study_note: 'Anna' (Hanna — grace; the same name as Hannah, Samuel's mother, whose prayer and praise she echoes). 'Prophetess' (prophētis — a female prophet; the OT had prophetesses: Miriam, Deborah, Huldah, Isaiah's wife). 'Of the tribe of Aser' (Asher) — one of the ten northern tribes dispersed since 722 BC. The mention of Asher suggests that Israelite identity was maintained across tribal lines even in the Second Temple period. 'Seven years from her virginity' — married young, widowed after seven years of marriage. By Jewish tradition she was 84 years old (v.37), having spent most of her life in widowhood. She represents Israel's faithful remnant across the centuries — those who waited, fasted, prayed, and did not lose hope.

verses.kjv_text: And she was a widow of about fourscore and four years, which departed not from the temple, but served God with fastings and prayers night and day.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτὴ χήρα ὡς ἐτῶν ὀγδοηκοντατεσσάρων ἣ οὐκ ἀφίστατο τοῦ ἱεροῦ νηστείαις καὶ δεήσεσιν λατρεύουσα νύκτα καὶ τὴν ἡμέραν
verses.transliteration: kai autē chēra hōs etōn ogdoēkontatesarōn hē ouk aphistato tou hierou nēsteiais kai deēsesin latreuousa nykta kai tēn hēmeran
verses.study_note: 'A widow of about fourscore and four years' (chēra hōs etōn ogdoēkontatesarōn — a widow of about 84 years). This could mean 84 years old or 84 years widowed (if the latter and she was married at ~14, she would be about 105). Most scholars take it as her age: 84. 'Departed not from the temple' — she had made the temple precincts her permanent dwelling and ministry station. 'Fastings and prayers night and day' (nēsteiais kai deēsesin latreuousa nykta kai tēn hēmeran — serving/worshipping with fastings and supplications night and day). Her life is a continuous liturgy of prayer and fasting. She embodies 1 Timothy 5:5: 'She that is a widow indeed...trusteth in God, and continueth in supplications and prayers night and day.' Anna is the model of intercessory devotion.

verses.kjv_text: And she coming in that instant gave thanks likewise unto the Lord, and spake of him to all them that looked for redemption in Jerusalem.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτῇ τῇ ὥρᾳ ἐπιστᾶσα ἀνθωμολογεῖτο τῷ θεῷ καὶ ἐλάλει περὶ αὐτοῦ πᾶσιν τοῖς προσδεχομένοις λύτρωσιν Ἱερουσαλήμ
verses.transliteration: kai autē tē hōra epistasa anthōmologeito tō theō kai elalei peri autou pasin tois prosdechomenois lytrōsin Hierousalēm
verses.study_note: 'In that instant' (autē tē hōra — at that very hour; the same Spirit that directed Simeon also times Anna's arrival precisely). 'Gave thanks' (anthōmologeito — was giving thanks, confessing/praising; antho + homologeō = to confess back, to return acknowledgment/praise; the word implies a response of thanksgiving to something received). 'Spake of him to all them that looked for redemption in Jerusalem' (elalei peri autou — she kept speaking about him; imperfect, ongoing). Anna becomes an evangelist — the first woman recorded proclaiming the Messiah. Her audience was not the general public but the faithful remnant in Jerusalem who were 'looking for redemption' (lytrōsin — ransoming, release by payment). She spoke of the infant Jesus to people who already had the frame of reference to understand — the fulfilment of their shared hope.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had performed all things according to the law of the Lord, they returned into Galilee, to their own city Nazareth.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὡς ἐτέλεσαν πάντα τὰ κατὰ τὸν νόμον κυρίου ἐπέστρεψαν εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν εἰς πόλιν ἑαυτῶν Ναζαρέτ
verses.transliteration: Kai hōs etelasan panta ta kata ton nomon kyriou epestrepsan eis tēn Galilaian eis polin heautōn Nazaret
verses.study_note: 'Performed all things according to the law of the Lord' (etelasan panta ta kata ton nomon kyriou — they completed everything according to the Lord's law; teleō = to finish, complete, fulfil; every legal requirement observed). Luke compresses the timeline here (cf. Matthew 2 which records the Magi's visit and the flight to Egypt, events that likely preceded or followed the return to Nazareth). Luke's narrative focuses on the temple events (circumcision, presentation, Simeon, Anna) and then resumes the Nazareth thread. 'Their own city Nazareth' (polin heautōn Nazaret — their own city Nazareth). From Bethlehem to Jerusalem to Nazareth — from the manger to the temple to the village workshop. The greatest story in history begins in obscurity and returns to obscurity.

verses.kjv_text: And the child grew, and waxed strong in spirit, filled with wisdom: and the grace of God was upon him.
verses.original_text: Τὸ δὲ παιδίον ηὔξανεν καὶ ἐκραταιοῦτο πληρούμενον σοφίᾳ καὶ χάρις θεοῦ ἦν ἐπ᾽ αὐτό
verses.transliteration: To de paidion ēuxanen kai ekrataiouto plēroumenon sophia kai charis theou ēn ep auto
verses.study_note: 'The child grew' (ēuxanen — kept growing; imperfect, continuous). 'Waxed strong' (ekrataiouto — grew strong/powerful; imperfect, continuous). 'Filled with wisdom' (plēroumenon sophia — being filled with wisdom; present participle, ongoing process). 'The grace of God was upon him' (charis theou ēn ep auto — the favour/grace of God was upon him). This summary of the hidden years (from the presentation at 40 days to the temple visit at 12) shows that Jesus's development was fully human — genuine growth in body, mind, and spiritual awareness — while being simultaneously under divine favour. Orthodoxy affirms the two natures: the eternal Son, in his humanity, genuinely grew and learned. This is not a contradiction but the mystery of the incarnation: the Logos took on all the genuine features of human development.

verses.kjv_text: Now his parents went to Jerusalem every year at the feast of the passover.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐπορεύοντο οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ κατ᾽ ἔτος εἰς Ἱερουσαλὴμ τῇ ἑορτῇ τοῦ πάσχα
verses.transliteration: Kai eporeuonto hoi goneis autou kat etos eis Hierousalēm tē heortē tou pascha
verses.study_note: 'Every year' (kat etos — year by year; a regular annual practice). 'The feast of the passover' (tē heortē tou pascha — the feast of Passover). Deuteronomy 16:1-8, 16 required all adult males to attend the three pilgrim festivals (Passover, Pentecost, Tabernacles) in Jerusalem. The journey from Nazareth to Jerusalem (approximately 80 miles) was typically made with a large group of pilgrims for safety and fellowship. 'His parents' continued their pattern of faithful covenant observance — the same pattern they have maintained throughout the infancy narrative (circumcision, presentation, annual Passover). Jesus grows up in a family of deliberate, consistent, joyful religious practice.

verses.kjv_text: And when he was twelve years old, they went up to Jerusalem after the custom of the feast.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὅτε ἐγένετο ἐτῶν δώδεκα ἀναβαινόντων αὐτῶν κατὰ τὸ ἔθος τῆς ἑορτῆς
verses.transliteration: Kai hote egeneto etōn dōdeka anabainontōn autōn kata to ethos tēs heortēs
verses.study_note: 'Twelve years old' (etōn dōdeka — twelve years old). At thirteen a Jewish boy became bar mitzvah ('son of the commandment') — responsible for observing the law for himself. The Passover at twelve may have been a preparatory pilgrimage before bar mitzvah the following year. Jewish tradition also associated the age of twelve with the beginning of wisdom's development. At twelve years old, Jesus's unique consciousness of his divine identity and mission begins to express itself publicly for the first time. 'After the custom of the feast' (kata to ethos tēs heortēs — according to the custom of the feast; again emphasising the family's faithful observance).

verses.kjv_text: And when they had fulfilled the days, as they returned, the child Jesus tarried behind in Jerusalem; and Joseph and his mother knew not of it.
verses.original_text: καὶ τελειωσάντων τὰς ἡμέρας ἐν τῷ ὑποστρέφειν αὐτοὺς ὑπέμεινεν Ἰησοῦς ὁ παῖς ἐν Ἱερουσαλήμ καὶ οὐκ ἔγνωσαν οἱ γονεῖς αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai teleiōsantōn tas hēmeras en tō hypostrephein autous hypemeinen Iēsous ho pais en Hierousalēm kai ouk egnōsan hoi goneis autou
verses.study_note: 'When they had fulfilled the days' (teleiōsantōn tas hēmeras — having completed the days of the feast; seven days for Passover plus Unleavened Bread). 'The child Jesus tarried behind' (hypemeinen Iēsous ho pais — Jesus the boy remained behind; hypomenō = to remain, stay; ho pais = the boy, the child, the young person). The word pais implies an older child than brephos (infant) or paidion (small child). He stays by deliberate choice. 'Joseph and his mother knew not' — in large pilgrim caravans (perhaps hundreds of people) a twelve-year-old boy might easily be thought to be in another part of the group for the first day of the journey. The parents' unawareness is entirely plausible and not negligent.

verses.kjv_text: But they, supposing him to have been in the company, went a day's journey; and they sought him among their kinsfolk and acquaintance.
verses.original_text: νομίσαντες δὲ αὐτὸν εἶναι ἐν τῇ συνοδίᾳ ἦλθον ἡμέρας ὁδὸν καὶ ἀνεζήτουν αὐτὸν ἐν τοῖς συγγενέσιν καὶ τοῖς γνωστοῖς
verses.transliteration: nomisantes de auton einai en tē synodia ēlthan hēmeras hodon kai anezētoun auton en tois synggenesin kai tois gnōstois
verses.study_note: 'Supposing him to have been in the company' (nomisantes auton einai en tē synodia — assuming him to be in the company/caravan; synodía = a company travelling together, a pilgrim caravan). One day's journey out of Jerusalem (perhaps 20-30 miles toward Galilee) before the alarm is raised. 'Sought him among their kinsfolk and acquaintance' (anezētoun auton en tois synggenesin kai tois gnōstois — they kept searching for him among relatives and known ones; imperfect, continuing search). The first search is in the caravan — the natural assumption being that he is with others from Nazareth. Only when he is not found there does the full alarm set in.

verses.kjv_text: And when they found him not, they turned back again to Jerusalem, seeking him.
verses.original_text: καὶ μὴ εὑρόντες ὑπέστρεψαν εἰς Ἱερουσαλὴμ ἀναζητοῦντες αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai mē heurontes hypestrepsan eis Hierousalēm anazētountes auton
verses.study_note: Not finding him in the caravan (mē heurontes — not having found him), they reverse course and return the full day's journey back to Jerusalem. 'Seeking him' (anazētountes auton — searching for him; the intensive ana-prefix: searching diligently, thoroughly). The three-day total (one day out, one day back, one day searching Jerusalem = three days, v.46) before finding him parallels the three days of Christ's death and resurrection. Some fathers saw a typological anticipation here. More certainly, the three days of anxious searching become the three-day interval before the reunion — and the lesson given (vv.48-49) transforms the reunion into a revelation.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that after three days they found him in the temple, sitting in the midst of the doctors, both hearing them and asking them questions.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο μετὰ ἡμέρας τρεῖς εὗρον αὐτὸν ἐν τῷ ἱερῷ καθεζόμενον ἐν μέσῳ τῶν διδασκάλων καὶ ἀκούοντα αὐτῶν καὶ ἐπερωτῶντα αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: kai egeneto meta hēmeras treis heuron auton en tō hierō kathezomenon en mesō tōn didaskalōn kai akouonta autōn kai eperōtōnta autous
verses.study_note: 'In the temple, sitting in the midst of the doctors' (en tō hierō kathezomenon en mesō tōn didaskalōn — in the temple complex, seated in the middle of the teachers; didaskalōn = teachers, rabbis). The posture (seated) was the normal position for both teacher and student in rabbinic discussion. Jesus is not lecturing the teachers but engaging in dialogue — 'both hearing them and asking them questions.' The questions of a twelve-year-old could range from profound to naive; what astonishes the teachers is not merely the questions but the quality of understanding behind them (v.47). He sits among Israel's greatest scholars in the very house that belongs to his Father, utterly at home.

verses.kjv_text: And all that heard him were astonished at his understanding and answers.
verses.original_text: ἐξίσταντο δὲ πάντες οἱ ἀκούοντες αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τῇ συνέσει καὶ ταῖς ἀποκρίσεσιν αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: existanto de pantes hoi akouontes autou epi tē synesei kai tais apokrisesin autou
verses.study_note: 'Astonished' (existanto — they were beside themselves, astounded; existēmi = to displace, to be out of one's mind with amazement; imperfect, they kept on being astonished). 'Understanding' (synesei — comprehension, insight; the ability to put things together and see their meaning; cf. 'synapse'). 'Answers' (apokrisesin — answers, responses). The rabbis' astonishment is not at a child who knows a lot of facts, but at a child who demonstrates genuine comprehension and gives penetrating answers. The same teachers who sit astonished at this twelve-year-old will, twenty years later, demand to know 'by what authority' he does these things (20:2) — having forgotten, or never understanding, the answer to that question.

verses.kjv_text: And when they saw him, they were amazed: and his mother said unto him, Son, why hast thou thus dealt with us? behold, thy father and I have sought thee sorrowing.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδόντες αὐτὸν ἐξεπλάγησαν καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτὸν ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ Τέκνον τί ἐποίησας ἡμῖν οὕτως ἰδοὺ ὁ πατήρ σου κἀγὼ ὀδυνώμενοι ἐζητοῦμέν σε
verses.transliteration: kai idontes auton exeplāgēsan kai eipen pros auton hē mētēr autou Teknon ti epoiēsas hēmin houtōs idou ho patēr sou kagō odynōmenoi ezētomen se
verses.study_note: 'Were amazed' (exeplāgēsan — were struck with astonishment; ekplēssō = to strike out, knock out, astonish violently; even the parents who know who he is are astonished at finding him here). 'Son' (Teknon — child, dear child; a term of warm affection). 'Thy father and I' (ho patēr sou kagō — your father and I; Mary uses 'father' for Joseph without qualification). 'Sought thee sorrowing' (odynōmenoi — being in pain, distressed; the word implies deep anguish, the same word in 16:24-25 for the torment of the rich man in Hades). Three days of not knowing where Jesus is has been agony for Mary. Her question is not rebuke but wounded maternal love: 'Why have you done this to us?' The pain of a mother who has been searching for her lost son. And then comes the answer that redefines 'father.'

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, How is it that ye sought me? wist ye not that I must be about my Father's business?
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Τί ὅτι ἐζητεῖτέ με οὐκ ᾔδειτε ὅτι ἐν τοῖς τοῦ πατρός μου δεῖ εἶναί με
verses.transliteration: kai eipen pros autous Ti hoti ezēteite me ouk ēdeite hoti en tois tou patros mou dei einai me
verses.study_note: The first recorded words of Jesus in Scripture — and they are a claim of divine sonship. 'Wist ye not' (ouk ēdeite — did you not know?; the question implies gentle surprise that they would seek him anywhere else). 'My Father's business' (en tois tou patros mou — in the things of my Father; or 'in my Father's house,' depending on how one supplies the implied noun; the Greek is literally 'in the of my Father,' leaving the noun unstated). Both meanings are intended: the temple is his Father's house (cf. John 2:16: 'make not my Father's house an house of merchandise') and he must be about his Father's affairs/business. The word 'must' (dei — it is necessary, it is divinely required; a Lukan word for divine necessity, 4:43; 9:22; 13:33; 19:5; 24:7, 26, 44) signals that this is not preference but divine compulsion. The temple is not Joseph's place but his Father's — and he calls no earthly man 'father' in this sense.

verses.kjv_text: And they understood not the saying which he spake unto them.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτοὶ οὐ συνῆκαν τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐλάλησεν αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: kai autoi ou synēkan to rhēma ho elalēsen autois
verses.study_note: 'They understood not' (ou synēkan — they did not understand; synhiēmi = to put together, comprehend; the same root as the 'understanding' the teachers marvelled at in v.47). The paradox: the Jewish teachers marvel at his understanding; his own parents do not understand his saying. This is not a moral failing — Mary is the most spiritually perceptive human in the narrative. The mystery of Jesus's identity is so profound that full understanding awaits the resurrection. Even the most intimate human relationships cannot fully contain or comprehend the divine. 'The saying which he spake' — his claim to God as his Father, with all its implications, remains beyond their present capacity to grasp.

verses.kjv_text: And he went down with them, and came to Nazareth, and was subject unto them: but his mother kept all these sayings in her heart.
verses.original_text: καὶ κατέβη μετ᾽ αὐτῶν καὶ ἦλθεν εἰς Ναζαρέτ καὶ ἦν ὑποτασσόμενος αὐτοῖς καὶ ἡ μήτηρ αὐτοῦ διετήρει πάντα τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: kai katebē met autōn kai ēlthen eis Nazaret kai ēn hypotassomenos autois kai hē mētēr autou dietērei panta ta rhēmata tauta en tē kardia autēs
verses.study_note: 'Was subject unto them' (ēn hypotassomenos autois — was being subject/submissive to them; hypotassō = to arrange under, to subordinate; present participle: a continuing disposition of submission). The One who has just asserted his divine sonship and his necessity to be in his Father's house immediately and willingly returns to Nazareth and submits to his human parents. This is the pattern of the Incarnation: divine authority exercised through human submission. For approximately eighteen more years (from twelve to thirty) Jesus submits to Joseph and Mary, works in the carpenter's shop, lives the ordinary hidden life. Philippians 2:8: 'he humbled himself, and became obedient.' 'His mother kept all these sayings' (dietērei — kept carefully, preserved; the intensive form of the same verb in v.19).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus increased in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man.
verses.original_text: Καὶ Ἰησοῦς προέκοπτεν σοφίᾳ καὶ ἡλικίᾳ καὶ χάριτι παρὰ θεῷ καὶ ἀνθρώποις
verses.transliteration: Kai Iēsous proekopten sophia kai hēlikia kai chariti para theō kai anthrōpois
verses.study_note: 'Increased' (proekopten — was progressing, advancing; prokoptō = to cut forward, to forge ahead, to make progress; imperfect: continuous advance; the same word used in Galatians 1:14 for Paul's advancement in Judaism). 'Wisdom' (sophia — understanding of divine truth applied to life). 'Stature' (hēlikia — physical age/height; both interpretations are possible: he grew in years and in physical stature). 'Favour with God and man' (chariti para theō kai anthrōpois — in favour/grace before God and men). This fourfold growth — wisdom (intellectual), stature (physical), favour with God (spiritual), favour with man (social) — describes complete human development.
