Jesus's development was genuine: he was not a God playing at being human, but the Son of God fully entering human growth. The final verse of Luke's infancy narrative leaves Jesus in Nazareth, growing quietly toward the day his public ministry begins. The greatest life in history is mostly hidden — thirty years of ordinariness before three years of ministry that changed the world.

meta.overview: Luke 3 opens the public ministry narrative with a precise historical synchronism (vv.1-2) placing John the Baptist in the fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar — approximately 26-29 AD — and locating him in the wilderness of Judea. The chapter presents John's ministry of preparatory preaching (vv.3-17): he calls Israel to repentance (metanoia) and to a baptism of repentance for the remission of sins (v.3), applying the prophecy of Isaiah 40:3-5. His ethical preaching addresses three groups — the crowds (v.10-11), the tax collectors (v.12-13), and soldiers (v.14) — with specific, practical demands for justice and generosity. His messianic preaching (vv.15-17) contrasts his own water baptism with the coming One's baptism 'with the Holy Ghost and with fire.' John's ministry ends when Herod Antipas imprisons him for his rebuke of Herod's unlawful marriage to Herodias (v.20). Jesus's baptism (vv.21-22) reveals the Trinity: the Son is baptised, the Holy Spirit descends bodily as a dove, and the Father speaks: 'Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased.' The genealogy of Jesus (vv.23-38) traces his lineage through Joseph to David, Abraham, and ultimately to Adam — 'the son of God' — encompassing both the covenant promise and the universal human family. Luke's genealogy goes back to Adam (unlike Matthew's which begins with Abraham), signalling that Jesus is the Saviour of all humanity, not only of Israel.
meta.theme: The Voice in the Wilderness — Repentance, Baptism, and the Revelation of the Son
meta.keyWordMeaning: Repentance — from meta (after/change) + noeō (to perceive, think); literally a change of mind, a turning of the whole person. John's fundamental demand is repentance: 'bring forth fruits worthy of repentance' (v.8). Metanoia in the biblical sense is not merely emotional regret (metameleia) but a decisive reorientation of the mind, will, and life toward God. It involves a turning from sin (the negative) and a turning toward God (the positive), expressed in concrete changed behaviour toward others (vv.10-14). John's baptism is a 'baptism of repentance for the remission of sins' (v.3) — the washing symbolises and confirms the inner turning. The call to metanoia will continue throughout Luke's Gospel and Acts, where repentance and forgiveness are preached in Jesus's name to all nations (24:47; Acts 2:38; 3:19; 17:30; 26:20).
outline: The Historical Setting of John's Ministry (vv.1-2): In the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar Pontius Pilate being governor of Judaea Herod being tetrarch of Galilee his brother Philip tetrarch of Ituraea and Lysanias tetrarch of Abilene Annas and Caiaphas being high priests the word of God came unto John the son of Zacharias in the wilderness

John's Preaching of Repentance (vv.3-14): John came into all the country about Jordan preaching the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins. As it is written in Isaiah — The voice of one crying in the wilderness prepare ye the way of the Lord. He said to the multitudes — O generation of vipers who hath warned you to flee the wrath to come? Bring forth fruits worthy of repentance. Begin not to say We have Abraham for our father — God is able to raise up children to Abraham from these stones. The axe is laid to the root of the trees. The people asked — What shall we do then? He answered — He that hath two coats let him give to him that hath none. Tax collectors asked — What shall we do? He said — Exact no more than that which is appointed you. Soldiers asked — What shall we do? He said — Do violence to no man neither accuse any falsely and be content with your wages

John's Messianic Preaching and Imprisonment (vv.15-20): The people mused whether John were the Christ. John answered — I indeed baptise you with water but one mightier than I cometh the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose. He shall baptise you with the Holy Ghost and with fire. His fan is in his hand and he will throughly purge his floor and will gather the wheat into his garner but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable. Herod the tetrarch being reproved by him for Herodias his brother Philip's wife added this above all that he shut up John in prison

The Baptism of Jesus (vv.21-22): Jesus being baptised and praying the heaven was opened and the Holy Ghost descended in a bodily shape like a dove upon him and a voice came from heaven saying Thou art my beloved Son in thee I am well pleased

The Genealogy of Jesus (vv.23-38): Jesus began to be about thirty years of age being the son of Joseph who was the son of Heli through a lineage traced back through David through Abraham back to Adam the son of God

verses.kjv_text: Now in the fifteenth year of the reign of Tiberius Caesar, Pontius Pilate being governor of Judaea, and Herod being tetrarch of Galilee, and his brother Philip tetrarch of Ituraea and of the region of Trachonitis, and Lysanias the tetrarch of Abilene,
verses.original_text: Ἐν ἔτει δὲ πεντεκαιδεκάτῳ τῆς ἡγεμονίας Τιβερίου Καίσαρος ἡγεμονεύοντος Ποντίου Πιλάτου τῆς Ἰουδαίας καὶ τετραρχοῦντος τῆς Γαλιλαίας Ἡρῴδου Φιλίππου δὲ τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ αὐτοῦ τετραρχοῦντος τῆς Ἰτουραίας καὶ Τραχωνίτιδος χώρας καὶ Λυσανίου τῆς Ἀβιληνῆς τετραρχοῦντος
verses.transliteration: En etei de pentekaidekatō tēs hēgemonias Tiberiou Kaisaros hēgemoneuontos Pontiou Pilatou tēs Ioudaias kai tetrarchountos tēs Galilaias Hērōdou Philippou de tou adelphou autou tetrarchountos tēs Itouraias kai Trachōnitidos chōras kai Lysaniou tēs Abilēnēs tetrarchountos
verses.study_note: Luke's historical synchronism (vv.1-2) is the most precise dating in the NT. 'Fifteenth year of Tiberius Caesar' — Tiberius became emperor upon Augustus's death in 14 AD; his fifteenth year = 28-29 AD (some calculate 26-27 AD if counting from his co-regency with Augustus from 11-12 AD). The six officials named provide multiple overlapping chronological anchors. Pontius Pilate was governor (prefect) of Judaea 26-36 AD. Herod Antipas was tetrarch of Galilee and Peraea (4 BC-39 AD). Philip was tetrarch of Ituraea, Trachonitis, and Gaulanitis (4 BC-34 AD). Lysanias of Abilene: confirmed by a Damascene inscription. The elaborate historical framework demonstrates Luke's commitment to historical verification — this is not myth or legend but datable history.

verses.kjv_text: Annas and Caiaphas being the high priests, the word of God came unto John the son of Zacharias in the wilderness.
verses.original_text: ἐπὶ ἀρχιερέως Ἅννα καὶ Καϊάφα ἐγένετο ῥῆμα θεοῦ ἐπὶ Ἰωάννην τὸν Ζαχαρίου υἱὸν ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ
verses.transliteration: epi archiereōs Hanna kai Kaiapa egeneto rhēma theou epi Iōannēn ton Zachariou huion en tē erēmō
verses.study_note: 'Annas and Caiaphas being the high priests' — technically only one high priest served at a time; Caiaphas (son-in-law of Annas) held the office 18-36 AD. Annas had held it 6-15 AD but retained enormous power and was still called 'high priest' by courtesy. 'The word of God came' (egeneto rhēma theou — the word of God came/happened; the classic OT formula for the prophetic word: 'the word of the LORD came to...' — Jeremiah 1:1-2; Ezekiel 1:3; Hosea 1:1; cf. Jonah 1:1). John is the last of the OT-style prophets (7:26-27; 16:16). The word comes not to Caesar, not to Pilate, not to Herod, not to the high priest — but to the son of Zacharias in the wilderness. God bypasses the entire political and religious establishment.

verses.kjv_text: And he came into all the country about Jordan, preaching the baptism of repentance for the remission of sins;
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦλθεν εἰς πᾶσαν τὴν περίχωρον τοῦ Ἰορδάνου κηρύσσων βάπτισμα μετανοίας εἰς ἄφεσιν ἁμαρτιῶν
verses.transliteration: kai ēlthen eis pasan tēn perichōron tou Iordanou kēryssōn baptisma metanoias eis aphesin hamartiōn
verses.study_note: 'Preaching' (kēryssōn — proclaiming as a herald; kēryssō = to cry out, announce publicly as a herald; the root of our word 'kerygma,' the preached message). 'Baptism of repentance' (baptisma metanoias — a baptism of/for repentance). John's baptism was unique: not a Jewish proselyte bath (taken by Gentiles converting to Judaism) but a baptism of repentance offered to Jews themselves — the shocking implication being that Jews were as spiritually unclean as Gentiles and needed washing. 'For the remission of sins' (eis aphesin hamartiōn — for the sending away/forgiveness of sins; aphesis = release, sending away). The baptism signified and accompanied the repentance; the remission was God's act in response to genuine repentance and faith.

verses.kjv_text: As it is written in the book of the words of Esaias the prophet, saying, The voice of one crying in the wilderness, Prepare ye the way of the Lord, make his paths straight.
verses.original_text: ὡς γέγραπται ἐν βίβλῳ λόγων Ἠσαΐου τοῦ προφήτου λέγοντος Φωνὴ βοῶντος ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ Ἑτοιμάσατε τὴν ὁδὸν κυρίου εὐθείας ποιεῖτε τὰς τρίβους αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: hōs gegraptai en biblō logōn Hēsaiou tou prophētou legontos Phōnē boōntos en tē erēmō Hetoimasate tēn hodon kyriou eutheias poieite tas tribous autou
verses.study_note: Quoting Isaiah 40:3-5. Luke's citation is the longest of the four Gospels (extending through v.6), emphasising the universal scope of the salvation. 'Voice of one crying' (Phōnē boōntos — voice of one shouting/crying; John is not even given the dignity of a name in the prophecy — he is simply 'a voice'). 'The way of the Lord' (tēn hodon kyriou — the road of the Lord; in Isaiah, 'the LORD' = YHWH; in Luke, John prepares the way for Jesus = YHWH come in flesh). John understands his entire ministry through this passage. 'Make his paths straight' (eutheias poieite — make straight; the image is of royal road-builders levelling terrain before a king's visit).

verses.kjv_text: Every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be brought low; and the crooked shall be made straight, and the rough ways shall be made smooth;
verses.original_text: πᾶσα φάραγξ πληρωθήσεται καὶ πᾶν ὄρος καὶ βουνὸς ταπεινωθήσεται καὶ ἔσται τὰ σκολιὰ εἰς εὐθεῖαν καὶ αἱ τραχεῖαι εἰς ὁδοὺς λείας
verses.transliteration: pasa pharanx plērōthēsetai kai pan oros kai bounos tapeinōthēsetai kai estai ta skolia eis eutheian kai hai tracheia eis hodous leias
verses.study_note: The imagery of Isaiah 40:4 describes the preparation of a royal road through difficult terrain. Valleys (pharanx = ravines) are filled, mountains (oros) and hills (bounos) are levelled, crooked paths (skolia = twisted, bent, morally corrupt — cf. 'scoliosis') are straightened, rough ground (tracheia = rugged, rough) is smoothed. Spiritually, the preparation of the way of the Lord requires the levelling of human pride (mountains of self-exaltation brought low) and the lifting of the humble (valleys filled). John's preaching of repentance and justice accomplishes this spiritual levelling — making hearts ready to receive the King.

verses.kjv_text: And all flesh shall see the salvation of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὄψεται πᾶσα σὰρξ τὸ σωτήριον τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai opsetai pasa sarx to sōtērion tou theou
verses.study_note: 'All flesh shall see the salvation of God' (pasa sarx — all flesh/humanity; sōtērion = the saving act, the deliverance). This is Luke's addition beyond what Mark and Matthew quote from Isaiah 40 — he extends the citation to include v.5 of Isaiah, which universalises the salvation. 'All flesh' (pasa sarx) = all humanity without ethnic, national, or social distinction. This is the programmatic statement of Luke-Acts: salvation is not for Jews only but for all peoples. The phrase anticipates Luke 2:10 ('all people'), 2:32 ('all flesh'), and the missions to Samaritans and Gentiles in Acts. John's ministry at the Jordan begins a movement whose scope is genuinely universal.

verses.kjv_text: Then said he to the multitude that came forth to be baptized of him, O generation of vipers, who hath warned you to flee from the wrath to come?
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν οὖν τοῖς ἐκπορευομένοις ὄχλοις βαπτισθῆναι ὑπ᾽ αὐτοῦ Γεννήματα ἐχιδνῶν τίς ὑπέδειξεν ὑμῖν φυγεῖν ἀπὸ τῆς μελλούσης ὀργῆς
verses.transliteration: Elegen oun tois ekporeuomenois ochlois baptisthēnai hyp autou Gennēmata echidnōn tis hypedeixeen hymin phygein apo tēs mellousēs orgēs
verses.study_note: 'Generation of vipers' (Gennēmata echidnōn — offspring of vipers; echidna = a viper, adder; the image of snake-like cunning and deadly venom). The address sounds harsh but is a pastoral alarm: these are people coming for baptism — presumably with some interest in repentance — yet whose lives have not matched the confession. John does not soften the message for a friendly crowd. 'Who hath warned you to flee?' (tis hypedeixen hymin phygein — who showed you to flee; hypodeiknymi = to show secretly, to point out). The question implies: your actions (coming for baptism without genuine repentance) do not match any real understanding of the coming wrath. 'Wrath to come' (orgē mellousa — the coming anger/wrath of God; the eschatological judgment, Romans 1:18; 2:5; 1 Thessalonians 1:10).

verses.kjv_text: Bring forth therefore fruits worthy of repentance, and begin not to say within yourselves, We have Abraham to our father: for I say unto you, That God is able of these stones to raise up children unto Abraham.
verses.original_text: ποιήσατε οὖν καρποὺς ἀξίους τῆς μετανοίας καὶ μὴ ἄρξησθε λέγειν ἐν ἑαυτοῖς Πατέρα ἔχομεν τὸν Ἀβραάμ λέγω γὰρ ὑμῖν ὅτι δύναται ὁ θεὸς ἐκ τῶν λίθων τούτων ἐγεῖραι τέκνα τῷ Ἀβραάμ
verses.transliteration: poiēsate oun karpous axious tēs metanoias kai mē arxēsthe legein en heautois Patera echomen ton Abraam legō gar hymin hoti dynatai ho theos ek tōn lithōn toutōn egeirai tekna tō Abraam
verses.study_note: 'Fruits worthy of repentance' (karpous axious tēs metanoias — fruits proportionate to/appropriate for repentance; axios = worthy, proportionate, of equal weight). Repentance is measured by its fruits — changed behaviour, not religious ceremony. 'We have Abraham to our father' — the false security of ethnic and religious privilege. Second Temple Judaism placed great weight on Abrahamic descent as a guarantee of divine favour. John demolishes this: Abrahamic descent is spiritually irrelevant apart from Abrahamic faith (cf. Romans 4; Galatians 3:7: 'they which are of faith, the same are the children of Abraham'). 'Of these stones raise up children' (ek tōn lithōn toutōn egeirai tekna — from these stones raise up children; lithōn/children is a possible Hebrew wordplay: ʾabanim/banim). The point: God's purposes do not depend on biological lineage.

verses.kjv_text: And now also the axe is laid unto the root of the trees: every tree therefore which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down, and cast into the fire.
verses.original_text: ἤδη δὲ καὶ ἡ ἀξίνη πρὸς τὴν ῥίζαν τῶν δένδρων κεῖται πᾶν οὖν δένδρον μὴ ποιοῦν καρπὸν καλὸν ἐκκόπτεται καὶ εἰς πῦρ βάλλεται
verses.transliteration: ēdē de kai hē axinē pros tēn rhizan tōn dendrōn keitai pan oun dendron mē poioun karpon kalon ekkoptetai kai eis pyr balletai
verses.study_note: 'The axe is laid unto the root' (hē axinē pros tēn rhizan keitai — the axe is already placed at the root). The urgency of 'already' (ēdē) and 'laid' (present tense — lying there now, ready to fall). John's preaching creates eschatological urgency: the time of judgment is not distant but imminent. The axe at the root is more serious than pruning — it threatens the tree's existence. 'Every tree...which bringeth not forth good fruit is hewn down' — the criterion is fruitfulness, not lineage, not ceremony. The parallel with v.8 is intentional: the opposite of 'fruits worthy of repentance' is being 'cast into the fire' (eis pyr balletai). Jesus will use the same tree/fruit imagery (6:43-44; Matthew 7:17-19).

verses.kjv_text: And the people asked him, saying, What shall we do then?
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐπηρώτων αὐτὸν οἱ ὄχλοι λέγοντες Τί οὖν ποιήσωμεν
verses.transliteration: Kai epērōtōn auton hoi ochloi legontes Ti oun poiēsōmen
verses.study_note: 'What shall we do then?' (Ti oun poiēsōmen — what then shall we do?). The question of genuine awakened conscience — the same question will arise at Pentecost (Acts 2:37: 'Men and brethren, what shall we do?'). The crowds take John's preaching seriously enough to ask for practical application. John's answers (vv.11-14) are remarkably concrete and ethical: share clothing, charge fair taxes, do no violence, be content with wages. The kingdom of God is not first an otherworldly spiritual matter but a this-worldly reorientation of social relationships. John insists on social ethics as evidence of genuine repentance.

verses.kjv_text: He answereth and saith unto them, He that hath two coats, let him impart to him that hath none; and he that hath meat, let him do likewise.
verses.original_text: ἀποκριθεὶς δὲ λέγει αὐτοῖς Ὁ ἔχων δύο χιτῶνας μεταδότω τῷ μὴ ἔχοντι καὶ ὁ ἔχων βρώματα ὁμοίως ποιείτω
verses.transliteration: apokritheis de legei autois Ho echōn dyo chitōnas metadotō tō mē echonti kai ho echōn brōmata homoiōs poieitō
verses.study_note: 'Two coats' (dyo chitōnas — two inner garments/tunics; chiton = the inner garment worn next to the skin, the one most easily given). The basic principle is radical generosity: if you have two of what another has none of, share. The commandment is not directed at the wealthy (who might have many tunics) but at the average person who might have a spare. 'Meat' (brōmata — food, things eaten). The practical ethics of repentance: justice and generosity in daily life. This is the social dimension of the kingdom. Luke emphasises economic ethics more than any other Gospel (cf. 12:13-21; 16:19-31; 19:1-10). Genuine repentance reshapes economic relationships.

verses.kjv_text: Then came also publicans to be baptized, and said unto him, Master, what shall we do?
verses.original_text: ἦλθον δὲ καὶ τελῶναι βαπτισθῆναι καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς αὐτόν Διδάσκαλε τί ποιήσωμεν
verses.transliteration: ēlthan de kai telōnai baptisthēnai kai eipan pros auton Didaskale ti poiēsōmen
verses.study_note: 'Publicans' (telōnai — tax collectors; telōnēs = one who collects indirect taxes/tolls; plural of telōnēs). Tax collectors in Roman Judea were Jews who had contracted with Rome to collect taxes, often enriching themselves by charging above the official rate. They were despised as collaborators with the Roman occupation and as notorious extortioners. That tax collectors came for John's baptism indicates the reach of his ministry across social barriers — even the most despised came. Their address — 'Teacher' (Didaskale) — is respectful. Their question 'what shall we do?' is the same as the crowds': they want practical, specific ethical guidance, not vague spiritual encouragement.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Exact no more than that which is appointed you.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Μηδὲν πλέον παρὰ τὸ διατεταγμένον ὑμῖν πράσσετε
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen pros autous Mēden pleon para to diatetagmenon hymin prassete
verses.study_note: 'Exact no more than that which is appointed you' (Mēden pleon para to diatetagmenon hymin prassete — practise/do nothing more beyond what has been assigned to you). John does not tell tax collectors to quit their profession — he calls them to reform it. 'The appointed amount' (to diatetagmenon — what has been officially ordered/prescribed; from diatassō, to appoint, arrange). The fraud lay in charging excess; John commands fairness and honesty within the occupation. This is the pattern of kingdom ethics: not necessarily a change of profession but a transformation of how the profession is conducted. Later in Luke, Zacchaeus (19:8) will voluntarily go beyond this minimum in restoring what he had taken unjustly.

verses.kjv_text: And the soldiers likewise demanded of him, saying, And what shall we do? And he said unto them, Do violence to no man, neither accuse any falsely; and be content with your wages.
verses.original_text: ἐπηρώτων δὲ αὐτὸν καὶ στρατευόμενοι λέγοντες Τί ποιήσωμεν καὶ ἡμεῖς καὶ εἶπεν αὐτοῖς Μηδένα διασείσητε μηδὲ συκοφαντήσητε καὶ ἀρκεῖσθε τοῖς ὀψωνίοις ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: epērōtōn de auton kai strateumenoi legontes Ti poiēsōmen kai hēmeis kai eipen autois Mēdena diaseisēte mēde sykophantēsete kai arkeisthe tois opsōniois hymōn
verses.study_note: 'Soldiers' (strateumenoi — soldiers on service; these may have been Jewish temple police or soldiers in Herod's army, or possibly Roman auxiliary troops). 'Do violence to no man' (Mēdena diaseisēte — shake/extort no one; diaseiō = to shake violently, to extort by intimidation). Soldiers commonly used physical intimidation for personal gain. 'Neither accuse any falsely' (mēde sykophantēsete — do not slander/blackmail; sykophantō = to accuse falsely for extortion; the root of our word 'sycophant'). 'Be content with your wages' (arkeisthe tois opsōniois — be satisfied with your rations/pay; opsōnion = military ration/pay). Three prohibitions targeting the specific temptations of military life: intimidation, false accusations, and covetousness. John's ethics are concrete, occupational, and demand immediate change.

verses.kjv_text: And as the people were in expectation, and all men mused in their hearts of John, whether he were the Christ, or not;
verses.original_text: Προσδοκῶντος δὲ τοῦ λαοῦ καὶ διαλογιζομένων πάντων ἐν ταῖς καρδίαις αὐτῶν περὶ τοῦ Ἰωάννου μήποτε αὐτὸς εἴη ὁ Χριστός
verses.transliteration: Prosdokōntos de tou laou kai dialogizomenōn pantōn en tais kardiais autōn peri tou Iōannou mēpote autos eiē ho Christos
verses.study_note: 'In expectation' (prosdokōntos tou laou — the people being in expectation; prosdokaō = to await eagerly; imperfect, ongoing state of expectation). The messianic expectation was intense in first-century Judea — amplified by John's extraordinary ministry, his call to repentance, his uncompromising preaching. 'Mused in their hearts of John, whether he were the Christ' (dialogizomenōn en tais kardiais — reasoning in their hearts; the interior debate). The question — could John be the Messiah? — was natural given his prophetic power and the lack of a recognised Messiah for centuries. John's response (vv.16-17) is the definitive self-denial: he is not the Christ but the Christ's herald.

verses.kjv_text: John answered, saying unto them all, I indeed baptize you with water; but one mightier than I cometh, the latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose: he shall baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire:
verses.original_text: ἀπεκρίνατο λέγων πᾶσιν ὁ Ἰωάννης Ἐγὼ μὲν ὕδατι βαπτίζω ὑμᾶς ἔρχεται δὲ ὁ ἰσχυρότερός μου οὗ οὐκ εἰμὶ ἱκανὸς λῦσαι τὸν ἱμάντα τῶν ὑποδημάτων αὐτοῦ αὐτὸς ὑμᾶς βαπτίσει ἐν Πνεύματι Ἁγίῳ καὶ πυρί
verses.transliteration: apekrithēnato legōn pasin ho Iōannēs Egō men hydati baptizō hymas erchetai de ho ischyroteros mou hou ouk eimi hikanos lysai ton himanta tōn hypodēmatōn autou autos hymas baptisei en Pneumati Hagiō kai pyri
verses.study_note: 'One mightier than I' (ho ischyroteros mou — the stronger/mightier one than I; ischuros = strong, powerful). 'The latchet of whose shoes I am not worthy to unloose' — removing sandals was a task for the lowest slave; John says he is not even worthy to perform this service for the coming One. Rabbis received such service from their disciples; but John places himself below slave-level in relation to the Messiah. 'Baptize you with the Holy Ghost and with fire' (baptisei en Pneumati Hagiō kai pyri). Two aspects: the giving of the Spirit (Pentecost, Acts 2) and the fire of judgment (v.17). The one baptism has a dual effect depending on the recipient's response: for believers, the Spirit's sanctifying fire; for the unrepentant, the fire of judgment.

verses.kjv_text: Whose fan is in his hand, and he will throughly purge his floor, and will gather the wheat into his garner; but the chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable.
verses.original_text: οὗ τὸ πτύον ἐν τῇ χειρὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ διακαθαριεῖ τὴν ἅλωνα αὐτοῦ καὶ συνάξει τὸν σῖτον εἰς τὴν ἀποθήκην αὐτοῦ τὸ δὲ ἄχυρον κατακαύσει πυρὶ ἀσβέστῳ
verses.transliteration: hou to ptyon en tē cheiri autou kai diakatharisei tēn halōna autou kai synaxei ton siton eis tēn apothēkēn autou to de achyron katakausei pyri asbestō
verses.study_note: 'His fan' (ptyon — a winnowing fork/shovel; used to toss threshed grain into the wind, separating the heavy wheat kernels from the light chaff). 'Throughly purge his floor' (diakatharisei tēn halōna — will completely clean the threshing floor; dia-katharizō = thoroughly purify). 'Gather the wheat into his garner' (synaxei ton siton eis tēn apothēkēn — gather the grain into the storehouse). 'The chaff he will burn with fire unquenchable' (achyron katakausei pyri asbestō — burn the chaff with unquenchable/inextinguishable fire; asbestos = unquenchable; the root of our 'asbestos'). The Messiah comes with ultimate authority to separate the genuine from the false, the repentant from the unrepentant. The judgment is complete, definitive, and permanent. The unquenchable fire is the most solemn warning in John's preaching.

verses.kjv_text: And many other things in his exhortation preached he unto the people.
verses.original_text: Πολλὰ μὲν οὖν καὶ ἕτερα παρακαλῶν εὐηγγελίζετο τὸν λαόν
verses.transliteration: Polla men oun kai hetera parakalōn euēngeliizeto ton laon
verses.study_note: 'Many other things in his exhortation' (Polla men oun kai hetera parakalōn — many other things indeed also, exhorting). 'Preached' (euēngeliizeto — was proclaiming the good news; euangelizomai). Luke's summary note acknowledges that he has given a representative selection of John's preaching, not a comprehensive record. The use of euangelizomai ('preached the gospel') for John's ministry of judgment and repentance shows that even the hard truths of coming wrath and the need for ethical transformation are 'good news' — for the one who hears and heeds them. John's ministry is not merely negative (repent from sin) but positive (the kingdom is near).

verses.kjv_text: But Herod the tetrarch, being reproved by him for Herodias his brother Philip's wife, and for all the evils which Herod had done,
verses.original_text: Ὁ δὲ Ἡρῴδης ὁ τετράρχης ἐλεγχόμενος ὑπ᾽ αὐτοῦ περὶ Ἡρῳδιάδος τῆς γυναικὸς τοῦ ἀδελφοῦ αὐτοῦ καὶ περὶ πάντων ὧν ἐποίησεν πονηρῶν ὁ Ἡρῴδης
verses.transliteration: Ho de Hērōdēs ho tetrarchēs elegchomenos hyp autou peri Hērōdiadēs tēs gynaikos tou adelphou autou kai peri pantōn hōn epoiēsen ponērōn ho Hērōdēs
verses.study_note: 'Being reproved by him' (elegchomenos — being rebuked, exposed, cross-examined; elegchō = to convict, reprove, rebuke; the word for the Spirit's conviction in John 16:8). John rebuked Herod Antipas for his marriage to Herodias, who had been the wife of his half-brother Philip. The relationship was doubly illegal: it violated the Levitical prohibition on marrying a brother's wife (Leviticus 18:16; 20:21) and it required Herodias to divorce Philip while he was still living. 'For all the evils which Herod had done' — John's prophetic courage encompassed a comprehensive rebuke of Herod's whole reign, not merely the marriage issue. The prophet who speaks truth to crowds must also speak truth to power. This courage will cost John his head (Matthew 14:1-12).

verses.kjv_text: Added yet this above all, that he shut up John in prison.
verses.original_text: προσέθηκεν καὶ τοῦτο ἐπὶ πᾶσιν καὶ κατέκλεισεν τὸν Ἰωάννην ἐν φυλακῇ
verses.transliteration: prosethēken kai touto epi pasin kai katekleisen ton Iōannēn en phylakē
verses.study_note: 'He shut up John in prison' (katekleisen ton Iōannēn en phylakē — he locked up/imprisoned John in prison; katakleios = to lock up completely; phylakē = prison, guard-post). Josephus (Antiquities 18.5.2) corroborates John's imprisonment at Machaerus, a fortress east of the Dead Sea. 'Added yet this above all' — Herod's imprisonment of John was the crowning evil added to all his other wickedness: silencing a prophet of God is the supreme act of human defiance against divine authority. Luke inserts John's imprisonment here before narrating Jesus's baptism — a literary and theological move showing the end of the forerunner's mission before the beginning of the One he heralded.

verses.kjv_text: Now when all the people were baptized, it came to pass, that Jesus also being baptized, and praying, the heaven was opened,
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ βαπτισθῆναι ἅπαντα τὸν λαὸν καὶ Ἰησοῦ βαπτισθέντος καὶ προσευχομένου ἀνεῳχθῆναι τὸν οὐρανόν
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō baptisthēnai hapanta ton laon kai Iēsou baptisthentos kai proseuchomenou aneōchthēnai ton ouranon
verses.study_note: 'All the people were baptized' — John's baptism was a mass movement. Luke then notes Jesus's baptism not as a separate, exclusive event but as part of this larger national repentance — he joins the line of penitents. 'Jesus also being baptized' — Jesus had no sin to repent of; his baptism was an act of identification with sinners, fulfilling all righteousness (Matthew 3:15) and foreshadowing his bearing of humanity's sin at the cross. Luke uniquely adds 'and praying' (kai proseuchomenou — and while praying; present participle). Prayer is a key Lukan motif at every decisive moment of Jesus's life (5:16; 6:12; 9:18, 28-29; 11:1; 22:41-44; 23:34, 46). The heavens open in response to prayer.

verses.kjv_text: And the Holy Ghost descended in a bodily shape like a dove upon him, and a voice came from heaven, which said, Thou art my beloved Son; in thee I am well pleased.
verses.original_text: καὶ καταβῆναι τὸ Πνεῦμα τὸ Ἅγιον σωματικῷ εἴδει ὡς περιστερὰν ἐπ᾽ αὐτόν καὶ φωνὴν ἐξ οὐρανοῦ γενέσθαι Σὺ εἶ ὁ υἱός μου ὁ ἀγαπητός ἐν σοὶ εὐδόκησα
verses.transliteration: kai katabēnai to Pneuma to Hagion sōmatikō eidei hōs peristeran ep auton kai phōnēn ex ouranou genesthai Sy ei ho huios mou ho agapētos en soi eudokēsa
verses.study_note: The Trinitarian theophany at the baptism: the Son is present (Jesus), the Holy Spirit descends, the Father speaks. 'In a bodily shape like a dove' (sōmatikō eidei hōs peristeran — in bodily form as a dove; Luke uniquely emphasises the bodily visibility, not just likeness). The dove recalls the Noah narrative (Genesis 8:8-12 — the dove as sign of a new beginning, of peace after judgment) and perhaps the Spirit hovering over the waters in Genesis 1:2. 'Thou art my beloved Son' (Sy ei ho huios mou ho agapētos — You are my Son, the beloved/only). 'Beloved' (agapētos) often means 'only' in the LXX (Genesis 22:2: 'thine only son Isaac, whom thou lovest,' LXX = ton agapēton). 'In thee I am well pleased' (en soi eudokēsa — in you I have taken delight; eudokeō = to be pleased, to take delight; aorist: a decisive, prior delight). The Father's delight is not earned by the baptism but declared upon the obedient Son.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus himself began to be about thirty years of age, being (as was supposed) the son of Joseph, which was the son of Heli,
verses.original_text: Καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν Ἰησοῦς ὡσεὶ ἐτῶν τριάκοντα ἀρχόμενος ὢν υἱός ὡς ἐνομίζετο Ἰωσὴφ τοῦ Ἡλὶ
verses.transliteration: Kai autos ēn Iēsous hōsei etōn triakonta archomenos ōn huios hōs enomizeto Iōsēph tou Hēli
verses.study_note: 'About thirty years of age' (hōsei etōn triakonta — approximately thirty years old). Thirty was the age at which Levites began full temple service (Numbers 4:3, 35, 43) and at which David became king (2 Samuel 5:4). 'Being (as was supposed) the son of Joseph' (hōs enomizeto Iōsēph — as was legally recognised, as was customary assumption, being the son of Joseph). Luke protects the virginal conception: 'as was supposed' (enomizeto = as was customarily believed, thought by legal convention). 'The son of Heli' — the genealogy traces through Joseph, but Heli (rather than Jacob, Matthew 1:16) may indicate that Luke gives Mary's genealogy through her father, with Joseph listed as son-in-law (a known Jewish usage for a man married into a family line). The genealogy proceeds backward through Heli to David to Abraham to Adam.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Matthat, which was the son of Levi, which was the son of Melchi, which was the son of Janna, which was the son of Joseph,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ματθὰτ τοῦ Λευὶ τοῦ Μελχὶ τοῦ Ἰανναὶ τοῦ Ἰωσὴφ
verses.transliteration: tou Matthat tou Leui tou Melchi tou Iannaī tou Iōsēph
verses.study_note: The genealogy proceeds in ascending order (toward the ancestors) with each name in the genitive case, tou ('of') linking them. These names in vv.24-27 cover the period from Heli (Mary's father) back to the post-exilic period. Unlike Matthew's genealogy (which is structured in three groups of fourteen), Luke's is a continuous ascending chain. Luke lists 77 names from Adam to Jesus (some manuscripts vary), which may be a deliberate structuring: 77 = 7 x 11, or possibly 11 x 7, symbolising completeness. The names between Heli and Zerubbabel are largely unknown from other sources and likely drawn from family records.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Mattathias, which was the son of Amos, which was the son of Naum, which was the son of Esli, which was the son of Nagge,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ματταθίου τοῦ Ἀμὼς τοῦ Ναοὺμ τοῦ Ἐσλὶ τοῦ Ναγγαὶ
verses.transliteration: tou Mattathiou tou Amōs tou Naoum tou Esli tou Naggai
verses.study_note: These names continue the post-exilic lineage, entirely unattested elsewhere in Scripture or in Josephus. The very obscurity of these names testifies to the authenticity of the genealogical records: no one constructing a legendary genealogy would invent a long list of unknown names. These were real people who lived, had children, and died — the ordinary human thread through which the extraordinary Saviour came. The genealogy affirms the full humanity of Jesus: he has real ancestors, a real family history, real roots in the story of Israel and of humanity.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Maath, which was the son of Mattathias, which was the son of Semei, which was the son of Joseph, which was the son of Juda,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Μαὰθ τοῦ Ματταθίου τοῦ Σεμεΐ τοῦ Ἰωσὴφ τοῦ Ἰούδα
verses.transliteration: tou Maath tou Mattathiou tou Semei tou Iōsēph tou Iouda
verses.study_note: The names Mattathias (twice), Joseph, and Juda (Judah) appear in this section and may reflect the common Jewish practice of naming children after famous ancestors or patriarchs. The repeated names in genealogical records can cause confusion but also testify to authentic family practice. Several of these names echo the Maccabean period (Mattathias was the father of Judas Maccabeus), suggesting these families maintained memory of the heroic leaders of Israel's recent past.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Joanna, which was the son of Rhesa, which was the son of Zorobabel, which was the son of Salathiel, which was the son of Neri,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ἰωανὰν τοῦ Ῥησὰ τοῦ Ζοροβάβελ τοῦ Σαλαθιὴλ τοῦ Νηρὶ
verses.transliteration: tou Iōannan tou Rhēsa tou Zorobabel tou Salathiēl tou Nēri
verses.study_note: 'Zorobabel' (Zerubbabel) and 'Salathiel' (Shealtiel) appear in both genealogies (Matthew 1:12 and Luke 3:27), providing a convergence point in the post-exilic period. Zerubbabel was the governor who led the return from Babylon and oversaw the rebuilding of the temple (Ezra 2:2; 3:2; Haggai 1:1; 2:23: 'I will make thee as a signet: for I have chosen thee, saith the LORD of hosts'). From this point back the two genealogies diverge until they converge again at David. Neri (Luke) vs. Jeconiah (Matthew): different routes within the Davidic line, one through Solomon (Matthew/royal line), one through Nathan (Luke/collateral line).

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Melchi, which was the son of Addi, which was the son of Cosam, which was the son of Elmodam, which was the son of Er,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Μελχὶ τοῦ Ἀδδὶ τοῦ Κωσὰμ τοῦ Ἐλμωδὰμ τοῦ Ἤρ
verses.transliteration: tou Melchi tou Addi tou Kōsam tou Elmodaem tou Ēr
verses.study_note: These names belong to the period from the exile to approximately the 8th-10th century BC — mostly unknown outside this genealogy. The genealogy through Nathan (David's son by Bathsheba, 2 Samuel 5:14; 1 Chronicles 3:5) rather than Solomon distinguishes Luke from Matthew. This line has the advantage of not passing through Jeconiah (Coniah), upon whom Jeremiah pronounced a curse (Jeremiah 22:30: 'no man of his seed shall prosper, sitting upon the throne of David'). Jesus is legally Davidic through Joseph (Matthew's royal-Solomonic line) and biologically Davidic through Mary (Luke's Nathan line, thus bypassing the curse on Jeconiah).

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Jose, which was the son of Eliezer, which was the son of Jorim, which was the son of Matthat, which was the son of Levi,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ἰωσὴ τοῦ Ἐλιέζερ τοῦ Ἰωρὶμ τοῦ Ματθὰτ τοῦ Λευὶ
verses.transliteration: tou Iōsē tou Elieza tou Iōrim tou Matthat tou Leui
verses.study_note: These names continue the pre-exilic Davidic line through the Nathan branch. The name Eliezer recalls Abraham's faithful servant (Genesis 15:2; 24:2), a name of hope and service. Levi and Joseph (Jose) are patriarchal names frequently reused in Jewish families. The fact that Levi (a priestly name) appears in a Davidic genealogy reflects the social reality of intermarriage between priestly and royal families in ancient Israel — something confirmed in the NT with Elisabeth's Aaronic descent and Mary's relationship to her (1:36).

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Simeon, which was the son of Juda, which was the son of Joseph, which was the son of Jonan, which was the son of Eliakim,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Συμεὼν τοῦ Ἰούδα τοῦ Ἰωσὴφ τοῦ Ἰωνὰν τοῦ Ἐλιακείμ
verses.transliteration: tou Symeōn tou Iouda tou Iōsēph tou Iōnan tou Eliakeim
verses.study_note: Simeon, Judah (Juda), Joseph, and Jonan are all patriarchal names — the naming practices of this period reflect deep reverence for the tribal ancestors. Eliakim ('God raises up') is a significant OT name (2 Kings 18:18; Isaiah 22:20) suggesting the aspirations parents placed in naming their children. Each of these individuals was, in human terms, an ordinary person living an ordinary life — yet through their faithfulness in marriage, family, and faith, they maintained the lineage through which the world's Saviour would come. The genealogy is also a theology of divine providence working through the ordinary.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Melea, which was the son of Menan, which was the son of Mattatha, which was the son of Nathan, which was the son of David,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Μελεὰ τοῦ Μεννὰ τοῦ Ματταθὰ τοῦ Ναθὰμ τοῦ Δαυίδ
verses.transliteration: tou Meleā tou Menna tou Mattatha tou Nathan tou Dauid
verses.study_note: 'Nathan...David' — the genealogy reaches David through his son Nathan (not Solomon). Nathan (not to be confused with the prophet Nathan) is listed in 2 Samuel 5:14 and 1 Chronicles 3:5 as a son of David by Bathsheba. The line from David through Nathan rather than Solomon is the key distinctive of Luke's genealogy compared to Matthew's. By going through Nathan, Mary's line (as many scholars hold this to be) avoids the Jeconiah curse of Jeremiah 22:30 and provides the biological Davidic descent required for the Messiah — while Joseph's line (Matthew) provides the legal royal inheritance through Solomon. Together, both genealogies are needed for a complete picture of Jesus's Davidic qualification.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Jesse, which was the son of Obed, which was the son of Booz, which was the son of Salmon, which was the son of Naasson,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ἰεσσαὶ τοῦ Ἰωβὴδ τοῦ Βοὸζ τοῦ Σαλμὼν τοῦ Ναασσὼν
verses.transliteration: tou Iessai tou Iōbēd tou Booz tou Salmōn tou Naassōn
verses.study_note: 'Jesse...Obed...Boaz...Salmon...Nahshon' — the ancestors of David familiar from the OT and confirmed in Ruth's genealogy (Ruth 4:18-22). Jesse was David's father (1 Samuel 16:1; Isaiah 11:1: 'a rod out of the stem of Jesse'). Obed was the son of Ruth and Boaz. Boaz was the kinsman-redeemer of Ruth — a picture of Christ the great Redeemer, who marries his Gentile bride and raises up an heir in Israel. Salmon married Rahab (Matthew 1:5) — another Gentile woman in the Messianic line. Nahshon was the leader of the tribe of Judah during the Exodus (Numbers 1:7; 2:3). The lineage of Jesus includes women of faith from outside Israel: a pattern of grace throughout.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Aminadab, which was the son of Aram, which was the son of Esrom, which was the son of Phares, which was the son of Juda,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ἀμιναδὰβ τοῦ Ἀράμ τοῦ Ἑσρὼμ τοῦ Φάρες τοῦ Ἰούδα
verses.transliteration: tou Aminadab tou Aram tou Hesrōm tou Phares tou Iouda
verses.study_note: 'Aminadab...Aram...Hezron...Perez...Judah' — the patriarchal line through Judah (the tribe from which Messiah was to come, Genesis 49:10: 'The sceptre shall not depart from Judah...until Shiloh come'). Perez (Phares) was the son of Judah by Tamar — another irregularity in the lineage (Genesis 38), yet Perez is in the line. The inclusion of Tamar's son (from a morally complex situation) in the Messianic genealogy demonstrates that divine grace works through and in spite of human failures and irregularities. Hezron and Aminadab appear in the genealogy of Exodus 6:23 and Numbers 1:7 — these are real historical figures in Israel's early national period.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Jacob, which was the son of Isaac, which was the son of Abraham, which was the son of Thara, which was the son of Nachor,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ἰακὼβ τοῦ Ἰσαὰκ τοῦ Ἀβραὰμ τοῦ Θάρα τοῦ Ναχὼρ
verses.transliteration: tou Iakōb tou Isaak tou Abraam tou Thara tou Nachōr
verses.study_note: 'Jacob...Isaac...Abraham' — the great patriarchal triad, the foundational figures of Israel's covenant history. The genealogy has now reached the explicit point of covenant: God's promise to Abraham (Genesis 12:1-3; 15; 17; 22:15-18) is the backbone of all salvation history. The Benedictus (1:73) celebrated the oath sworn to Abraham; now we see that Jesus is biologically descended from Abraham — the promised seed fulfilled (Galatians 3:16). Terah (Thara) was Abraham's father, called out of Ur of the Chaldees (Genesis 11:26-32; Acts 7:2-4). Nahor was Abraham's brother. The genealogy goes beyond the covenant with Israel to the origins of that people.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Saruch, which was the son of Ragau, which was the son of Phalec, which was the son of Heber, which was the son of Sala,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Σαροὺχ τοῦ Ῥαγαῦ τοῦ Φάλεκ τοῦ Ἕβερ τοῦ Σαλὰ
verses.transliteration: tou Sarouch tou Rhagau tou Phalek tou Heber tou Sala
verses.study_note: 'Serug...Reu...Peleg...Eber...Shelah' — the post-Flood, pre-Abraham patriarchs of Genesis 10-11 (the Table of Nations and the genealogy of Shem). Eber gives his name to the 'Hebrews' (Heb. ʿIbri — cf. Genesis 10:21; 11:14-17). Peleg lived 'in his days was the earth divided' (Genesis 10:25) — possibly a reference to the dispersion at Babel. These names are from the Shem genealogy of Genesis 11:10-26, taking the lineage back to Noah's son Shem, the ancestor of the Semitic peoples. Luke's genealogy deliberately transcends the bounds of Israel's ethnic history to reach the universal origins of humanity.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Cainan, which was the son of Arphaxad, which was the son of Sem, which was the son of Noe, which was the son of Lamech,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Καϊνὰν τοῦ Ἀρφαξὰδ τοῦ Σὴμ τοῦ Νῶε τοῦ Λάμεχ
verses.transliteration: tou Kainan tou Arphaxad tou Sēm tou Nōe tou Lamech
verses.study_note: 'Cainan...Arphaxad...Shem...Noah...Lamech' — the line from Noah through Shem. 'Cainan' between Arphaxad and Shelah appears in the LXX of Genesis 10:24 and 11:12-13 but not in the MT. Luke follows the LXX tradition. 'Shem' (Sem) was the ancestor of the Semitic peoples, blessed by Noah (Genesis 9:26: 'Blessed be the LORD God of Shem'). 'Noah' — the survivor of the Flood, the new Adam after the judgment of the waters. 'Lamech' — Noah's father (Genesis 5:28-31), who named his son 'Noah' (comfort/rest) saying 'This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands.' Even in naming, the hope of redemption is embedded.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Mathusala, which was the son of Enoch, which was the son of Jared, which was the son of Maleleel, which was the son of Cainan,
verses.original_text: τοῦ Μαθουσάλα τοῦ Ἑνὼχ τοῦ Ἰάρεδ τοῦ Μαλελεὴλ τοῦ Καϊνὰν
verses.transliteration: tou Mathousala tou Henōch tou Iared tou Maleleēl tou Kainan
verses.study_note: 'Methuselah...Enoch...Jared...Mahalalel...Cainan' — the antediluvian patriarchs of Genesis 5, the line from Adam through Seth. 'Enoch' (Henōch) — uniquely, Enoch 'walked with God: and he was not; for God took him' (Genesis 5:24). Enoch's translation (without dying) is a preview of the resurrection and of the ultimate triumph of the godly over death. Jude 14-15 quotes Enoch's prophecy of the Lord's coming with ten thousands of his saints. Methuselah (Mathusala) lived 969 years — the longest-lived person in Scripture. Even in the extremity of human lifespan, death came (Genesis 5:27: 'and he died'). Into this world under the sentence of death comes the Son of God to defeat it.

verses.kjv_text: Which was the son of Enos, which was the son of Seth, which was the son of Adam, which was the son of God.
verses.original_text: τοῦ Ἐνὼς τοῦ Σὴθ τοῦ Ἀδὰμ τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: tou Enōs tou Sēth tou Adam tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Enos...Seth...Adam...the son of God' — the genealogy reaches its terminus and its summit in Adam, 'the son of God' (tou theou). Adam was created directly by God, the unique formation of divine breath and dust (Genesis 2:7), and thus 'of God' in a way no subsequent human being is. Luke's genealogy uniquely ends here (Matthew begins with Abraham), and the theological significance is profound: Jesus comes not merely as the Jewish Messiah but as the Second Adam (Romans 5:12-21; 1 Corinthians 15:45-49), the restorer of what the first Adam lost. By tracing to Adam, Luke declares that Jesus's salvation is for all humanity, not one nation. The genealogy ends where the Gospel begins: the Son of God has come to restore what the son of God lost.

meta.overview: Luke 4 narrates three pivotal events that define the nature and scope of Jesus's messianic mission. The Temptation in the Wilderness (vv.1-13) portrays Jesus, filled with the Holy Spirit, being led into the desert for forty days of satanic testing. Three temptations target appetite, authority, and divine protection; Jesus answers each with Deuteronomy, refusing to acquire the kingdom by any path other than obedience to the Father. Satan departs 'for a season' (v.13), planning a more opportune time (cf. 22:3). The Rejection at Nazareth (vv.14-30) provides the programmatic declaration of Jesus's mission: reading from Isaiah 61 in the synagogue, he announces 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor... to set at liberty them that are bruised' (vv.18-19), and then declares 'This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears' (v.21). When Jesus implies that Gentiles would receive what Israel rejected (the examples of Elijah and Elisha), the Nazareth congregation attempts to kill him (vv.28-30). This rejection of his hometown anticipates the wider rejection by Israel. The Galilean Ministry (vv.31-44) begins with power: exorcism in the Capernaum synagogue (vv.31-37), the healing of Simon's mother-in-law (vv.38-39), and a mass healing at sunset (vv.40-41). When the demonic cry of recognition disturbs the synagogue, Jesus silences it — consistently refusing to allow demons to proclaim his identity. The chapter closes with Jesus withdrawing to pray before declaring his universal mission: 'I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent' (v.43).
meta.theme: The Anointed One — Withstanding Temptation and Proclaiming the Jubilee of the Spirit
meta.keyWordMeaning: It is necessary, one must, it is divinely required — from the impersonal verb from deō (to bind). The word expresses divine compulsion, the inescapable necessity of God's redemptive plan. In Luke 4:43: 'I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent' — the dei of mission. Throughout Luke, dei marks the non-negotiable elements of Jesus's vocation: he must suffer (9:22; 17:25; 24:7), be crucified (24:26), rise again (24:26), and ascend (Acts 1:11). In chapter 4, the dei is implicit in the temptation (he must resist every shortcut to the kingdom) and explicit in the mission statement (he must go to other cities). Jesus lives under divine compulsion — not external coercion but the inner necessity of love and obedience to the Father's redemptive plan.
outline: The Temptation of Jesus (vv.1-13): Jesus being full of the Holy Ghost returned from Jordan and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness being forty days tempted of the devil. The devil said — If thou be the Son of God command this stone to be made bread. Jesus answered — It is written Man shall not live by bread alone. The devil took him up and showed him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time and said — All this power will I give thee if thou wilt worship me. Jesus answered — It is written Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God and him only shalt thou serve. The devil set him on a pinnacle of the temple and said — If thou be the Son of God cast thyself down for he shall give his angels charge over thee. Jesus answered — Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God. The devil departed from him for a season

The Rejection at Nazareth (vv.14-30): Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee. He came to Nazareth where he had been brought up and went into the synagogue on the sabbath day and stood up to read. There was delivered to him the book of Esaias. He found the place where it was written — The Spirit of the Lord is upon me because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor he hath sent me to heal the brokenhearted to preach deliverance to the captives and recovery of sight to the blind to set at liberty them that are bruised to preach the acceptable year of the Lord. He closed the book and said — This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears. They said — Is not this Joseph's son? He said — No prophet is accepted in his own country. He cited Elijah sent to a Gentile widow and Elisha healing Naaman the Syrian. All in the synagogue were filled with wrath and thrust him out and led him to a cliff to throw him down but he passing through the midst of them went his way

The Capernaum Ministry (vv.31-44): He came to Capernaum and taught on the sabbath. In the synagogue was a man with an unclean spirit who cried out — Let us alone what have we to do with thee Jesus of Nazareth art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art the Holy One of God. Jesus rebuked him — Hold thy peace and come out of him. And he came out. The people were all amazed. He arose out of the synagogue and entered Simon's house and his wife's mother was sick of a great fever. Jesus stood over her and rebuked the fever and it left her. When the sun was setting all they that had any sick with divers diseases brought them and he laid his hands on every one of them and healed them. Devils also came out of many crying — Thou art Christ the Son of God. He rebuking suffered them not to speak for they knew he was Christ. When it was day he departed and went into a desert place. The people sought him and said — Stay with us. He said — I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also for therefore am I sent. And he preached in the synagogues of Galilee

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus being full of the Holy Ghost returned from Jordan, and was led by the Spirit into the wilderness,
verses.original_text: Ἰησοῦς δὲ πλήρης Πνεύματος Ἁγίου ὑπέστρεψεν ἀπὸ τοῦ Ἰορδάνου καὶ ἤγετο ἐν τῷ Πνεύματι ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ
verses.transliteration: Iēsous de plērēs Pneumatos Hagiou hypestrepsen apo tou Iordanou kai ēgeto en tō Pneumati en tē erēmō
verses.study_note: 'Full of the Holy Ghost' (plērēs Pneumatos Hagiou — full of the Holy Spirit; the same Spirit that descended at the baptism now fills Jesus). Luke emphasises the Spirit's role throughout the temptation: Jesus is led by the Spirit (v.1), returns in the power of the Spirit (v.14), and reads the Spirit-anointed Isaiah passage in the synagogue (v.18). The Spirit does not protect Jesus from temptation but leads him into it — the testing is a necessary precondition for the public ministry. 'Was led' (ēgeto — was being led; imperfect passive, indicating the Spirit's continuous guidance throughout the wilderness period). The wilderness (erēmos) echoes Israel's forty years of testing in the desert — Jesus succeeds where Israel failed.

verses.kjv_text: Being forty days tempted of the devil. And in those days he did eat nothing: and when they were ended, he afterward hungered.
verses.original_text: ἡμέρας τεσσεράκοντα πειραζόμενος ὑπὸ τοῦ διαβόλου καὶ οὐκ ἔφαγεν οὐδὲν ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις ἐκείναις καὶ συντελεσθεισῶν αὐτῶν ἐπείνασεν
verses.transliteration: hēmeras tesserakonta peirazomenos hypo tou diabolou kai ouk ephagen ouden en tais hēmerais ekeinais kai syntelestheisōn autōn epeinasen
verses.study_note: 'Forty days' (hēmeras tesserakonta — forty days; cf. Moses: Exodus 24:18; 34:28 — forty days on Sinai without food; Elijah: 1 Kings 19:8 — forty days to Horeb). The forty days identify Jesus with the great mediators of Israel. 'Tempted' (peirazomenos — being tested, tried; peirazomai = to test, examine, tempt; the word can mean either genuine testing for the purpose of proof or enticement to sin; here both meanings apply: the devil seeks to lead to sin; God uses it to demonstrate the Son's faithfulness). 'He afterward hungered' (epeinasen — he was hungry; aorist: the acute hunger came at the end of the fast, when his human body reached its physical limits — the precise moment of maximum vulnerability for the first temptation).

verses.kjv_text: And the devil said unto him, If thou be the Son of God, command this stone that it be made bread.
verses.original_text: εἶπεν δὲ αὐτῷ ὁ διάβολος Εἰ υἱὸς εἶ τοῦ θεοῦ εἰπὲ τῷ λίθῳ τούτῳ ἵνα γένηται ἄρτος
verses.transliteration: eipen de autō ho diabolos Ei huios ei tou theou eipe tō lithō toutō hina genētai artos
verses.study_note: 'If thou be the Son of God' (Ei huios ei tou theou — if you are the Son of God; the 'if' is not a statement of doubt but a conditional: 'Since you are...then prove it by...'; or possibly a genuine test of whether Jesus will use his divine power for himself). The devil has just heard the Father's declaration 'Thou art my beloved Son' (3:22); his first temptation targets that identity. 'Command this stone to be made bread' — a miracle within Jesus's power, to meet a genuine need (hunger after forty days). The temptation is not to do something obviously evil but to do something good (feeding himself) by the wrong means (using his divine power for self-preservation rather than in obedience to the Father's leading). The issue is not whether he can but whether he should.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answered him, saying, It is written, That man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word of God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀπεκρίθη Ἰησοῦς πρὸς αὐτόν γέγραπται ὅτι Οὐκ ἐπ᾽ ἄρτῳ μόνῳ ζήσεται ὁ ἄνθρωπος
verses.transliteration: kai apekrithē Iēsous pros auton gegraptai hoti Ouk ep artō monō zēsetai ho anthrōpos
verses.study_note: 'It is written' (gegraptai — it stands written; perfect passive: permanently written, always applicable). Jesus quotes Deuteronomy 8:3 — God's explanation to Israel of why he let them hunger in the wilderness (so they would learn to trust his word). 'Man shall not live by bread alone' (Ouk ep artō monō zēsetai ho anthrōpos) — life in its fullest sense (zōē) is sustained not by physical nutrition but by every word (rhēmati) proceeding from God's mouth. Jesus refuses to act independently of the Father's word. Where Israel murmured at the lack of bread (Exodus 16), Jesus trusts the God who provides. He cites the lesson Israel was supposed to learn from the wilderness but failed to — and he himself lives it. All three of Jesus's answers come from Deuteronomy (6-8), the section dealing with Israel's wilderness testing.

verses.kjv_text: And the devil, taking him up into an high mountain, shewed unto him all the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἀναγαγὼν αὐτὸν ὁ διάβολος εἰς ὄρος ὑψηλὸν ἔδειξεν αὐτῷ πάσας τὰς βασιλείας τῆς οἰκουμένης ἐν στιγμῇ χρόνου
verses.transliteration: Kai anagagōn auton ho diabolos eis oros hypsēlon edeixsen autō pasas tas basileias tēs oikoumenēs en stigmē chronou
verses.study_note: 'All the kingdoms of the world in a moment of time' (pasas tas basileias tēs oikoumenēs en stigmē chronou — all the kingdoms of the inhabited world in a point/instant of time; stigmē = a point, an instant; the Greek root of our word 'stigma'). The vision is supernatural, a panoramic spiritual display. The second temptation is the greatest in scope: all political power and authority over the entire inhabited world. 'In a moment of time' implies a vision beyond natural sight — either a spiritual experience or a demonic projection. The kingdoms of the world are the Messiah's by right (Psalm 2:8: 'Ask of me, and I shall give thee the heathen for thine inheritance, and the uttermost parts of the earth for thy possession') — but the devil offers to shortcut the cross and give them now, by a different path.

verses.kjv_text: And the devil said unto him, All this power will I give thee, and the glory of them: for that is delivered unto me; and to whomsoever I will I give it.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὁ διάβολος Σοὶ δώσω τὴν ἐξουσίαν ταύτην ἅπασαν καὶ τὴν δόξαν αὐτῶν ὅτι ἐμοὶ παραδέδοται καὶ ᾧ ἂν θέλω δίδωμι αὐτήν
verses.transliteration: kai eipen autō ho diabolos Soi dōsō tēn exousian tautēn hapasan kai tēn doxan autōn hoti emoi paradedotai kai hō an thelō didōmi autēn
verses.study_note: 'Delivered unto me' (emoi paradedotai — it has been handed over to me; a claim based on the Fall — Satan acquired a degree of authority over the fallen world, cf. John 12:31: 'the prince of this world'; 2 Corinthians 4:4: 'the god of this age'). The claim is partially true (Satan does have real, temporary dominion over fallen humanity) but the offer is deceptive: (1) Jesus can receive what is rightfully his (Psalm 2) only through the Father, not through Satan; (2) acceptance would mean worshipping Satan — an absolute inversion of the divine order; (3) the glory offered is real but temporary; the glory obtained through the cross is eternal (Philippians 2:9-11). The temptation targets political ambition — the desire to establish the kingdom by the world's methods rather than God's.

verses.kjv_text: If thou therefore wilt worship me, all shall be thine.
verses.original_text: σὺ οὖν ἐὰν προσκυνήσῃς ἐνώπιον ἐμοῦ ἔσται σοῦ πᾶσα
verses.transliteration: sy oun ean proskynēsēs enōpion emou estai sou pasa
verses.study_note: 'If thou therefore wilt worship me' (ean proskynēsēs enōpion emou — if you will prostrate yourself before me; proskyneō = to bow down, prostrate in worship; the word used for worship throughout the NT). The condition is unambiguous and irreducible: to receive what the devil offers requires worshipping the devil. There is no subtlety here, no disguise — the temptation is naked and absolute. The offer encapsulates the entire history of human compromise with evil: gaining the world's goods, power, or pleasures by paying homage to what opposes God. Jesus refuses not because he does not want the kingdoms but because he will not worship anything other than the Father to obtain them. The cross is the alternative path to all authority (Matthew 28:18: 'All power is given unto me in heaven and in earth').

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answered and said unto him, Get thee behind me, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς αὐτῷ εἶπεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς Ὕπαγε Σατανᾶ γέγραπται Κύριον τὸν θεόν σου προσκυνήσεις καὶ αὐτῷ μόνῳ λατρεύσεις
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis autō eipen ho Iēsous Hypage Satana gegraptai Kyrion ton theon sou proskynēseis kai autō monō latreuseis
verses.study_note: 'Get thee behind me, Satan' (Hypage Satana — go away, Satan; hypage = depart; Luke does not include the 'behind me' of Matthew 4:10 in many manuscripts, though some do). Jesus's response is immediate and decisive: no negotiation, no consideration — absolute rejection. 'It is written' — again Deuteronomy (6:13): 'Thou shalt fear the LORD thy God, and serve him.' The commandment of exclusive worship is the foundation of the covenant. 'Him only shalt thou serve' (autō monō latreuseis — him alone you shall worship/serve; latreuō = to render sacred service, to worship; the word for Israel's whole religious service). The first commandment (Exodus 20:3: 'Thou shalt have no other gods before me') is upheld. Jesus does not merely refuse Satan; he articulates the positive principle: God alone is to be worshipped.

verses.kjv_text: And he brought him to Jerusalem, and set him on a pinnacle of the temple, and said unto him, If thou be the Son of God, cast thyself down from hence:
verses.original_text: Ἤγαγεν δὲ αὐτὸν εἰς Ἱερουσαλὴμ καὶ ἔστησεν αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὸ πτερύγιον τοῦ ἱεροῦ καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Εἰ υἱὸς εἶ τοῦ θεοῦ βάλε σεαυτὸν ἐντεῦθεν κάτω
verses.transliteration: Ēgagen de auton eis Hierousalēm kai estēsen auton epi to pterygion tou hierou kai eipen autō Ei huios ei tou theou bale seauton enteuthen katō
verses.study_note: 'The pinnacle of the temple' (pterygion tou hierou — the little wing/pinnacle of the temple; pteryx = wing; possibly the southeastern corner of the temple mount where it towered over the Kidron Valley, a drop of perhaps 450 feet; or the gatehouse at the temple's highest point). 'If thou be the Son of God' — the same conditional as the first temptation. The third temptation is the most subtle: Satan now quotes Scripture (Psalm 91:11-12) to support his suggestion. 'Cast thyself down' — a public miracle that would authenticate Jesus's divine identity spectacularly before the Jerusalem crowds. The temptation is to use God's promises as a blank cheque for self-exhibition — to test the Father's protective faithfulness for personal advantage rather than trusting it in genuine faith.

verses.kjv_text: For it is written, He shall give his angels charge over thee, to keep thee:
verses.original_text: γέγραπται γὰρ ὅτι Τοῖς ἀγγέλοις αὐτοῦ ἐντελεῖται περὶ σοῦ τοῦ διαφυλάξαι σε
verses.transliteration: gegraptai gar hoti Tois angelois autou entellsetai peri sou tou diaphylaxai se
verses.study_note: Satan quotes Psalm 91:11-12 — a psalm of divine protection for the one who trusts in God. The quotation is accurate but the application is perverse: the psalm promises protection for those who trust in God's providence; Satan invites Jesus to manufacture a crisis to prove the promise. 'He shall give his angels charge over thee' — the promise of angelic guardianship is real and will be fulfilled in genuine ways (Luke 22:43; Mark 1:13). But the promise cannot be invoked to test God by unnecessary self-endangerment. Satan's use of Scripture is a warning: the Bible can be quoted to support virtually any human agenda if isolated from its context and purpose. The answer to misused Scripture is not less Scripture but Scripture rightly understood.

verses.kjv_text: And in their hands they shall bear thee up, lest at any time thou dash thy foot against a stone.
verses.original_text: καὶ ὅτι Ἐπὶ χειρῶν ἀροῦσίν σε μήποτε προσκόψῃς πρὸς λίθον τὸν πόδα σου
verses.transliteration: kai hoti Epi cheirōn arousin se mēpote proskopsēs pros lithon ton poda sou
verses.study_note: Satan's citation of Psalm 91:12 continues. The promise — 'lest thou dash thy foot against a stone' — was given for the ordinary hazards of the path of obedience, not for voluntarily leaping from heights. The distinction is crucial: God promises to protect his servants in the course of their faithful service; he does not promise miraculous rescue from self-inflicted crises designed to test his reliability. This temptation is directed at presumption — the sin of demanding that God prove himself on our terms. It is essentially a demand for a sign — the same demand that Israel repeatedly made in the wilderness and that Jesus will later refuse (11:29: 'an evil generation seeketh after a sign').

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said unto him, It is said, Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς ὅτι Εἴρηται Οὐκ ἐκπειράσεις κύριον τὸν θεόν σου
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis eipen autō ho Iēsous hoti Eirētai Ouk ekpeiraseis kyrion ton theon sou
verses.study_note: Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 6:16: 'Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye tempted him in Massah.' The reference is to Exodus 17:1-7 where Israel demanded water and essentially demanded God prove his presence and care (Massah = 'testing'). 'Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God' — to 'tempt' (ekpeiraseis — from ek-peirazo, an intensified form: to put to the test thoroughly, to try the limits of) God is to presume upon his favour in a way that doubts his genuine faithfulness and demands proof. Jesus refuses to play by Satan's rules even when Scripture is quoted. He responds not by matching Satan's citation with a counter-quotation but by identifying the principle that governs all such promises: God is not to be tested.

verses.kjv_text: And when the devil had ended all the temptation, he departed from him for a season.
verses.original_text: Καὶ συντελέσας πάντα πειρασμὸν ὁ διάβολος ἀπέστη ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ ἄχρι καιροῦ
verses.transliteration: Kai syntelesas panta peirasmon ho diabolos apestē ap autou achri kairou
verses.study_note: 'When the devil had ended all the temptation' (syntelesas panta peirasmon — having completed every temptation; syntelesō = to bring to full completion; panta peirasmon = every possible temptation, the full battery). The three explicit temptations are representative; the testing was comprehensive. 'Departed from him for a season' (apestē ap autou achri kairou — departed from him until an opportune time; kairos = a significant, appointed moment). Luke's distinctive addition: 'for a season.' Satan does not give up; he waits for a better opportunity. The 'opportune time' comes at the Passion (22:3 — 'Satan entered into Judas'; 22:31 — 'Satan hath desired to have you, that he may sift you as wheat'; 22:53 — 'this is your hour, and the power of darkness'). The temptation resumes in Gethsemane and at the cross. Jesus's victory here is not the final triumph but the decisive victory of the opening battle.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus returned in the power of the Spirit into Galilee: and there went out a fame of him through all the region round about.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ὑπέστρεψεν ὁ Ἰησοῦς ἐν τῇ δυνάμει τοῦ Πνεύματος εἰς τὴν Γαλιλαίαν καὶ φήμη ἐξῆλθεν καθ᾽ ὅλης τῆς περιχώρου περὶ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: Kai hypestrepsen ho Iēsous en tē dynamei tou Pneumatos eis tēn Galilaian kai phēmē exēlthen kath holēs tēs perichōrou peri autou
verses.study_note: 'In the power of the Spirit' (en tē dynamei tou Pneumatos — in the power of the Spirit; dynamis = power, might, the source of our word 'dynamite'). The same Spirit that filled him (v.1) and led him into the wilderness now fills his Galilean ministry with supernatural power. 'Fame of him' (phēmē — a report, news; the word for the spreading of news about Jesus; cf. 5:15 where 'so much the more went there a fame abroad'). The public ministry begins with growing popular recognition, which will soon bring both disciples and opposition. Luke summarises the beginning of the Galilean ministry before narrating the paradigmatic episode of the Nazareth rejection, which sets the theological agenda for the whole ministry.

verses.kjv_text: And he taught in their synagogues, being glorified of all.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς ἐδίδασκεν ἐν ταῖς συναγωγαῖς αὐτῶν δοξαζόμενος ὑπὸ πάντων
verses.transliteration: kai autos edidasken en tais synagōgais autōn doxazomenos hypo pantōn
verses.study_note: 'Taught in their synagogues' (edidasken en tais synagōgais — was teaching in the synagogues; imperfect, the ongoing practice). The synagogue was the social and religious centre of every Jewish community — a gathering place for prayer, Scripture reading, and teaching on the Sabbath. Any qualified Jewish man could be invited to read and comment on the text. 'Being glorified of all' (doxazomenos hypo pantōn — being honoured/glorified by all; imperfect, continuous reception of honour). The initial Galilean response is entirely positive — a contrast that makes the Nazareth rejection even more shocking. The synagogue ministry was Jesus's primary teaching forum throughout the Galilean period.

verses.kjv_text: And he came to Nazareth, where he had been brought up: and, as his custom was, he went into the synagogue on the sabbath day, and stood up for to read.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἦλθεν εἰς τὴν Ναζαρά οὗ ἦν τεθραμμένος καὶ εἰσῆλθεν κατὰ τὸ εἰωθὸς αὐτῷ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ τῶν σαββάτων εἰς τὴν συναγωγήν καὶ ἀνέστη ἀναγνῶναι
verses.transliteration: Kai ēlthen eis tēn Nazara hou ēn tethrammenos kai eisēlthen kata to eiōthos autō en tē hēmera tōn sabbatōn eis tēn synagōgēn kai anestē anagnōnai
verses.study_note: 'Where he had been brought up' (hou ēn tethrammenos — where he had been nurtured/raised; trephō = to nourish, bring up). Nazareth was not just his birthplace but his home for approximately 28 years. 'As his custom was' (kata to eiōthos autō — according to his established habit; eiōthos = what is habitual, customary; the perfect participle indicates a settled, ongoing practice). Jesus's Sabbath synagogue attendance was not occasional but his regular practice — the custom of a lifetime of covenant faithfulness. 'Stood up for to read' (anestē anagnōnai — he arose to read; the custom was to stand for the reading of Scripture and sit for the exposition, v.20). The reader was typically invited by the synagogue ruler; Jesus's standing up may indicate he requested the reading or was invited.

verses.kjv_text: And there was delivered unto him the book of the prophet Esaias. And when he had opened the book, he found the place where it was written,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπεδόθη αὐτῷ βιβλίον τοῦ προφήτου Ἠσαΐου καὶ ἀναπτύξας τὸ βιβλίον εὗρεν τὸν τόπον οὗ ἦν γεγραμμένον
verses.transliteration: kai epedothē autō biblion tou prophētou Hēsaiou kai anaptysas to biblion heuren ton topon hou ēn gegrammenon
verses.study_note: 'There was delivered unto him the book of the prophet Esaias' (epedothē autō — was given over to him; the synagogue attendant handed the scroll to Jesus). 'He found the place' (heuren ton topon — he found the passage; possibly rolled to a specific section; the scroll of Isaiah is long, and finding a specific passage required skill and intention). The passage read (Isaiah 61:1-2) was divinely chosen, whether by providential assignment in the lectionary cycle or by Jesus's deliberate selection. It is the most programmatic Messianic text possible: the Servant anointed with the Spirit, commissioned to preach good news to the poor. Jesus does not choose a general text but the specific passage that defines his entire mission. The selection itself is an act of self-identification.

verses.kjv_text: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor; he hath sent me to heal the brokenhearted, to preach deliverance to the captives, and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty them that are bruised,
verses.original_text: Πνεῦμα κυρίου ἐπ᾽ ἐμέ οὗ εἵνεκεν ἔχρισέν με εὐαγγελίσασθαι πτωχοῖς ἀπέσταλκέν με κηρύξαι αἰχμαλώτοις ἄφεσιν καὶ τυφλοῖς ἀνάβλεψιν ἀποστεῖλαι τεθραυσμένους ἐν ἀφέσει
verses.transliteration: Pneuma kyriou ep eme hou heineken echrisen me euangelisasthai ptōchois apestalken me kēryxai aichmalōtois aphesin kai typhlois anablepsin aposteilai tethrausmenous en aphesei
verses.study_note: Isaiah 61:1-2 — the programmatic text of Jesus's mission. 'The Spirit of the Lord is upon me' — Jesus's ministry is Trinitarian: he is anointed (by the Father) with the Spirit at the baptism (3:22). 'Because he hath anointed me' (echrisen me — he anointed me; the verb from which 'Christ' = Anointed One derives). Jesus declares himself to be the Anointed Servant of Isaiah 61. 'Preach the gospel to the poor' (euangelisasthai ptōchois — to announce good news to the destitute; ptōchos = the completely destitute, not just financially poor but spiritually and socially marginalised). 'Deliverance to the captives' (aichmalōtois aphesin — release to prisoners of war). 'Recovering of sight to the blind' (typhlois anablepsin — seeing again to the blind; the miracles of healing will fulfil this literally). 'Set at liberty them that are bruised' (tethrausmenous en aphesei — the crushed, battered ones in release/freedom). The Jubilee language: the year of release, the year of the LORD's favour.

verses.kjv_text: To preach the acceptable year of the Lord.
verses.original_text: κηρύξαι ἐνιαυτὸν κυρίου δεκτόν
verses.transliteration: kēryxai eniauton kyriou dekton
verses.study_note: 'The acceptable year of the Lord' (eniauton kyriou dekton — the year of the Lord's favour/acceptance). Jesus stops reading in the middle of Isaiah 61:2 (the full verse continues 'and the day of vengeance of our God' — which he deliberately omits). The Jubilee (Leviticus 25) was the fiftieth year when debts were cancelled, slaves were freed, and land returned to original owners. Jesus announces the ultimate Jubilee — the year of God's grace and favour in which the spiritual realities symbolised by the Jubilee are actualised. The Jubilee language is all about release: from debt, slavery, poverty, and alienation. Jesus is the fulfilment of the Jubilee principle — his death cancels the debt of sin; his resurrection releases prisoners; his gospel restores the alienated to their inheritance.

verses.kjv_text: And he closed the book, and he gave it again to the minister, and sat down. And the eyes of all them that were in the synagogue were fastened on him.
verses.original_text: καὶ πτύξας τὸ βιβλίον ἀποδοὺς τῷ ὑπηρέτῃ ἐκάθισεν καὶ πάντων οἱ ὀφθαλμοὶ ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ ἦσαν ἀτενίζοντες αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: kai ptyxas to biblion apodous tō hypēretē ekathisen kai pantōn hoi ophthalmoi en tē synagōgē ēsan atenizaontes autō
verses.study_note: 'He closed the book' — the deliberate act of rolling up the scroll and handing it back. 'Sat down' (ekathisen — he sat down; the teacher's posture for exposition in the rabbinic tradition). 'The eyes of all them...were fastened on him' (panton hoi ophthalmoi ēsan atenizaontes — all eyes were fixed on him; atenizō = to fix the gaze intently, to stare; a Lukan word, cf. Acts 3:4; 6:15; 7:55; 10:4). The congregation is riveted in expectation. They have heard the reading; now they wait for the interpretation. The moment of tension between reading and exposition is extended by Luke to dramatic effect — every eye on the one who is about to make the most extraordinary claim in the history of the synagogue.

verses.kjv_text: And he began to say unto them, This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears.
verses.original_text: ἤρξατο δὲ λέγειν πρὸς αὐτοὺς ὅτι Σήμερον πεπλήρωται ἡ γραφὴ αὕτη ἐν τοῖς ὠσὶν ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: ērxato de legein pros autous hoti Sēmeron peplērōtai hē graphē hautē en tois ōsin hymōn
verses.study_note: 'This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears' (Sēmeron peplērōtai hē graphē hautē — today this scripture has been fulfilled; sēmeron = today, this very day; peplērōtai = perfect passive, has been and remains fulfilled). This is the most audacious synagogue sermon in history. Jesus does not say 'this scripture will be fulfilled when the Messiah comes' — he says it is fulfilled today, in your hearing, by me. The claim is simultaneously exegetical (the text refers to the Messiah), historical (the Messiah has now come), and personal (I am he). 'In your ears' (en tois ōsin hymōn — in your ears; not just 'to you' but 'as you are hearing it'). The fulfilment is happening in real time, in the act of his reading and speaking. This single sentence is the christological hinge of the entire Lukan narrative.

verses.kjv_text: And all bare him witness, and wondered at the gracious words which proceeded out of his mouth. And they said, Is not this Joseph's son?
verses.original_text: καὶ πάντες ἐμαρτύρουν αὐτῷ καὶ ἐθαύμαζον ἐπὶ τοῖς λόγοις τῆς χάριτος τοῖς ἐκπορευομένοις ἐκ τοῦ στόματος αὐτοῦ καὶ ἔλεγον Οὐχὶ υἱός ἐστιν Ἰωσὴφ οὗτος
verses.transliteration: kai pantes emartyroun autō kai ethaumazon epi tois logois tēs charitos tois ekporeuomenois ek tou stomatos autou kai elegon Ouchi huios estin Iōsēph houtos
verses.study_note: 'All bare him witness' (pantes emartyroun autō — all were testifying to him, speaking well of him; imperfect: a sustained initial reaction of positive response). 'The gracious words' (tois logois tēs charitos — the words of grace; logos tēs charitos = speech characterised by grace/favour; not just pleasant words but words full of divine grace). The congregation initially receives the teaching positively — they are drawn in by the beauty and authority of his speech. 'Is not this Joseph's son?' (Ouchi huios estin Iōsēph houtos — Is this not the son of Joseph?) — the question reveals the barrier that prevents full acceptance: they know his human family. The familiarity of thirty years of village life becomes the obstacle to recognising his divine identity. What should be an asset — knowing his home, his family — becomes the stumbling block.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Ye will surely say unto me this proverb, Physician, heal thyself: whatsoever we have heard done in Capernaum, do also here in thy country.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Πάντως ἐρεῖτέ μοι τὴν παραβολὴν ταύτην Ἰατρέ θεράπευσον σεαυτόν ὅσα ἠκούσαμεν γενόμενα εἰς τὴν Καφαρναοὺμ ποίησον καὶ ὧδε ἐν τῇ πατρίδι σου
verses.transliteration: kai eipen pros autous Pantōs ereite moi tēn parabolēn tautēn Iatre therapeuson seauton hosa ēkousamen genomena eis tēn Kapharnaoum poieson kai hōde en tē patridi sou
verses.study_note: 'Physician, heal thyself' (Iatre therapeuson seauton — Doctor, heal yourself; a common proverb in the ancient world). Jesus reads the subtext of their wonder: they want signs and miracles for their own benefit, as they have heard he performed in Capernaum. 'Do also here in thy country' — the demand for local miracles to authenticate his claim. The proverb implies: if you are truly capable, demonstrate it here where we can see and assess it. Jesus perceives the unbelief beneath the initial positive response. The demand for local miracles reflects the same spirit as the temptation to cast himself from the pinnacle: use your power to prove yourself on the terms others demand. Jesus refuses this consistently — miracles come in response to faith, not as a means of producing it on demand (cf. 11:29).

verses.kjv_text: And he said, Verily I say unto you, No prophet is accepted in his own country.
