verses.original_text: εἶπεν δέ Ἀμὴν λέγω ὑμῖν ὅτι οὐδεὶς προφήτης δεκτός ἐστιν ἐν τῇ πατρίδι αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: eipen de Amēn legō hymin hoti oudeis prophētēs dektos estin en tē patridi autou
verses.study_note: 'Verily I say unto you' (Amēn legō hymin — truly/amen I say to you). 'Amēn' as an introductory formula is unique to Jesus among all ancient Jewish teachers — rabbis said 'Amēn' to endorse what others had said, at the end of prayers; Jesus uses it to introduce his own authoritative teaching. 'No prophet is accepted in his own country' (oudeis prophētēs dektos estin en tē patridi autou — no prophet is acceptable/welcome in his homeland). The proverb, applied to himself, is a warning: the Nazareth congregation's familiarity with him will prevent them from receiving him as a prophet. Familiarity breeds contempt — or at least comfortable assumption. Those who knew Jesus as a boy cannot easily accept him as the Messiah. The same principle operates today: those closest to Jesus in social proximity are not necessarily those closest to him in faith.

verses.kjv_text: But I tell you of a truth, many widows were in Israel in the days of Elias, when the heaven was shut up three years and six months, when great famine was throughout all the land;
verses.original_text: ἐπ᾽ ἀληθείας δὲ λέγω ὑμῖν πολλαὶ χῆραι ἦσαν ἐν ταῖς ἡμέραις Ἠλίου ἐν τῷ Ἰσραήλ ὅτε ἐκλείσθη ὁ οὐρανὸς ἐπὶ ἔτη τρία καὶ μῆνας ἕξ ὡς ἐγένετο λιμὸς μέγας ἐπὶ πᾶσαν τὴν γῆν
verses.transliteration: ep alētheias de legō hymin pollai chērai ēsan en tais hēmerais Hēliou en tō Israēl hote ekleisthē ho ouranos epi etē tria kai mēnas hex hōs egeneto limos megas epi pasan tēn gēn
verses.study_note: 'Many widows were in Israel in the days of Elias' — the first of two OT precedents Jesus cites (Elijah and Elisha) to demonstrate that God's grace has historically broken the ethnic/national boundaries of Israel to reach Gentiles. The three-and-a-half year drought (1 Kings 17-18; James 5:17: 'it rained not on the earth by the space of three years and six months') caused widespread famine throughout Israel. 'Many widows' in Israel suffered — yet God did not send Elijah to any of them. The implication for the Nazareth congregation is painfully sharp: God is not obligated to bless Israel's hometown (or Israel itself) simply because of national privilege. He sends grace where it is received.

verses.kjv_text: But unto none of them was Elias sent, save unto Sarepta, a city of Sidon, unto a woman that was a widow.
verses.original_text: καὶ πρὸς οὐδεμίαν αὐτῶν ἐπέμφθη Ἠλίας εἰ μὴ εἰς Σάρεπτα τῆς Σιδωνίας πρὸς γυναῖκα χήραν
verses.transliteration: kai pros oudemian autōn epemphthē Hēlias ei mē eis Sarepta tēs Sidōnias pros gynaika chēran
verses.study_note: 'Sarepta...Sidon' — a Gentile city in Phoenicia (modern Lebanon), far north of Israel (1 Kings 17:8-24). The widow of Zarephath (Sarepta) was not a Jew but a Phoenician — a Gentile woman who received the blessing of Elijah's presence and miracle (the inexhaustible meal and oil, the resurrection of her son). God's sovereign grace bypassed the many widows of Israel to reach one Gentile widow. The pattern anticipates the whole Gentile mission of Luke-Acts: when Israel rejects the gospel, it goes to the Gentiles (Acts 13:46; 18:6; 28:28). Jesus is not simply making an historical observation; he is warning the Nazareth congregation that their rejection of him will result in the grace flowing to others.

verses.kjv_text: And many lepers were in Israel in the time of Eliseus the prophet; and none of them was cleansed, saving Naaman the Syrian.
verses.original_text: καὶ πολλοὶ λεπροὶ ἦσαν ἐν τῷ Ἰσραὴλ ἐπὶ Ἐλισαίου τοῦ προφήτου καὶ οὐδεὶς αὐτῶν ἐκαθαρίσθη εἰ μὴ Ναιμὰν ὁ Σύρος
verses.transliteration: kai polloi leproi ēsan en tō Israēl epi Elisaiou tou prophētou kai oudeis autōn ekatharisthē ei mē Naiman ho Syros
verses.study_note: 'Naaman the Syrian' (Naiman ho Syros — Naaman the Syrian; 2 Kings 5). Naaman was the commander of Syria's army — an enemy of Israel — who was cleansed of leprosy by Elisha. Many lepers in Israel went uncleansed; the one Gentile enemy commander received healing. The pattern is the same: God's grace bypassing the national Israel to reach an individual Gentile who came in humility and faith. Naaman's story has the additional element of resistance — he almost refused to obey Elisha's undignified instruction to wash in the Jordan seven times (2 Kings 5:11-13). The double example of the widow of Zarephath (woman) and Naaman (man) is comprehensive: both sexes, Phoenicia and Syria, north and east — the grace of God knows no national boundaries.

verses.kjv_text: And all they in the synagogue, when they heard these things, were filled with wrath,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπλήσθησαν πάντες θυμοῦ ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ ἀκούοντες ταῦτα
verses.transliteration: kai eplēsthēsan pantes thymou en tē synagōgē akouontes tauta
verses.study_note: 'Were filled with wrath' (eplēsthēsan pantes thymou — all were filled with rage; plēroō = to fill; thymos = passionate anger, rage; the strongest Greek word for anger). The shift from v.22 ('all bare him witness and wondered') to v.28 ('filled with wrath') is dramatic and instantaneous. What changed? Jesus's implication that God's grace was going to Gentiles while bypassing unbelieving Israel. The congregation could accept that he was a gifted teacher; they could not accept that their national privilege was not automatic, that their rejection of him would result in the blessing flowing to others. Ethnic-religious pride is the most tenacious human sin, and Jesus has directly attacked it. The same message produces wonder when it offers grace and rage when it removes privilege.

verses.kjv_text: And rose up, and thrust him out of the city, and led him unto the brow of the hill whereon their city was built, that they might cast him down headlong.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀναστάντες ἐξέβαλον αὐτὸν ἔξω τῆς πόλεως καὶ ἤγαγον αὐτὸν ἕως ὀφρύος τοῦ ὄρους ἐφ᾽ οὗ ἡ πόλις ᾠκοδόμητο αὐτῶν εἰς τὸ κατακρημνίσαι αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai anastantes exebalon auton exō tēs poleōs kai ēgagon auton heōs ophryos tou orous eph hou hē polis ōkodomēto autōn eis to katakrēmnisai auton
verses.study_note: 'Rose up, and thrust him out' (exebalon auton — they threw him out; ekballō = to cast out; the forceful, violent ejection). The synagogue congregation becomes a mob. 'Led him unto the brow of the hill' (eos ophryos tou orous — to the edge/brow of the mountain; ophrys = eyebrow, edge, brow). Nazareth sits on the edge of a hill with rocky escarpments. 'Cast him down headlong' (katakrēmnisai — to throw down a precipice; katakrēmnizomai = to cast down a cliff). This was both mob violence and possibly an attempt at extra-legal execution: throwing from a height was a form of death sentence sometimes applied outside formal legal processes. The Passion narrative begins in Nazareth: the first attempt on Jesus's life comes from his own hometown.

verses.kjv_text: But he passing through the midst of them went his way.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ διελθὼν διὰ μέσου αὐτῶν ἐπορεύετο
verses.transliteration: autos de dielthōn dia mesou autōn eporeueto
verses.study_note: 'Passing through the midst of them went his way' (dielthōn dia mesou autōn eporeueto — passing through the middle of them, he was going on his way). No explanation is given of how Jesus escaped — the text simply records that he passed through them. This has been interpreted as: a miraculous invisibility, a divine restraint on the mob, or simply a calm authority that checked their violence. Luke's understated narration is deliberate: it is not the moment for Jesus to die (cf. John 7:30; 8:20: 'his hour was not yet come'). The time and manner of his death are under divine control. He goes on his way — the way of the cross, on God's timetable, not in Nazareth at the hands of an angry synagogue congregation.

verses.kjv_text: And came down to Capernaum, a city of Galilee, and taught them on the sabbath days.
verses.original_text: Καὶ κατῆλθεν εἰς Καφαρναοὺμ πόλιν τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ ἦν διδάσκων αὐτοὺς ἐν τοῖς σάββασιν
verses.transliteration: Kai katēlthen eis Kapharnaoum polin tēs Galilaias kai ēn didaskōn autous en tois sabbasin
verses.study_note: 'Came down to Capernaum' (katēlthen eis Kapharnaoum — he went down to Capernaum; Nazareth is at a higher elevation than Capernaum on the shore of the Sea of Galilee). Capernaum became Jesus's operational headquarters for the Galilean ministry (Matthew 4:13: 'he...dwelt in Capernaum'). The shift from rejected Nazareth to receptive Capernaum illustrates the Elijah/Elisha principle just enunciated: grace goes where it is received. Capernaum is a fishing village on the northern shore of the Sea of Galilee; its synagogue (discovered archaeologically) was probably built near the time of Jesus or earlier. 'Was teaching on the sabbath days' (ēn didaskōn en tois sabbasin — was teaching on the sabbaths; imperfect: ongoing, habitual practice).

verses.kjv_text: And they were astonished at his doctrine: for his word was with power.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξεπλήσσοντο ἐπὶ τῇ διδαχῇ αὐτοῦ ὅτι ἐν ἐξουσίᾳ ἦν ὁ λόγος αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai exeplēssonto epi tē didachē autou hoti en exousia ēn ho logos autou
verses.study_note: 'Astonished at his doctrine' (exeplēssonto epi tē didachē autou — they were struck with astonishment at his teaching; exeplēssō = to strike out; the same root as ekplēssō in 2:47 for the temple teachers' astonishment at the twelve-year-old Jesus). 'His word was with power' (en exousia ēn ho logos autou — his word was with authority; exousia = authority, the right and power to act; distinct from dynamis [miraculous power] though related). The Capernaum congregation senses immediately what makes Jesus different from the scribes: he speaks with direct divine authority, not by citing other authorities ('Rabbi X says...'). His word has inherent authority because it is the word of God himself speaking through his Son.

verses.kjv_text: And in the synagogue there was a man, which had a spirit of an unclean devil, and cried out with a loud voice,
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐν τῇ συναγωγῇ ἦν ἄνθρωπος ἔχων πνεῦμα δαιμονίου ἀκαθάρτου καὶ ἀνέκραξεν φωνῇ μεγάλῃ
verses.transliteration: kai en tē synagōgē ēn anthrōpos echōn pneuma daimoniou akathartou kai anekraxen phōnē megalē
verses.study_note: 'A spirit of an unclean devil' (pneuma daimoniou akathartou — a spirit of an unclean demon; daimonion = demon, unclean spirit; akathartou = unclean, morally impure). The demon's manifestation in a synagogue — the place of worship and Torah study — is significant: the presence of Jesus triggers demonic reaction. The demons recognise Jesus immediately (v.34) even when humans do not. 'Cried out with a loud voice' (anekraxen phōnē megalē — shouted with a great voice) — the demon's agitation at the presence of the Holy One is visible and audible. The synagogue in Capernaum was Jesus's first major public confrontation with demonic power, establishing the pattern of the coming kingdom: where Jesus goes, demons must flee.

verses.kjv_text: Saying, Let us alone; what have we to do with thee, thou Jesus of Nazareth? art thou come to destroy us? I know thee who thou art; the Holy One of God.
verses.original_text: Ἔα τί ἡμῖν καὶ σοί Ἰησοῦ Ναζαρηνέ ἦλθες ἀπολέσαι ἡμᾶς οἶδά σε τίς εἶ ὁ Ἅγιος τοῦ θεοῦ
verses.transliteration: Ea ti hēmin kai soi Iēsou Nazarēne ēlthes apolesai hēmas oida se tis ei ho Hagios tou theou
verses.study_note: 'Let us alone' (Ea — an exclamation of protest: leave off! stop!; a word of alarm and resistance). 'What have we to do with thee?' (ti hēmin kai soi — what is there between us and you?; a Semitic idiom expressing total separation of interests). 'Art thou come to destroy us?' — the demon correctly perceives that the coming of the Holy One means its destruction. 'I know thee who thou art' (oida se tis ei — I know you who you are; oida = to know with certainty). 'The Holy One of God' (ho Hagios tou theou — the Holy One of God; the most exalted title; used in the OT for the LORD himself in Psalm 71:22; Isaiah 1:4; 30:11 as 'the Holy One of Israel'). The demonic confession is the most accurate christological statement in Capernaum's synagogue that day — yet Jesus refuses to accept it. Correct doctrine from the wrong source, in the wrong context, for the wrong purpose is not welcome.

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus rebuked him, saying, Hold thy peace, and come out of him. And when the devil had thrown him in the midst, he came out of him, and hurt him not.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπετίμησεν αὐτῷ ὁ Ἰησοῦς λέγων Φιμώθητι καὶ ἔξελθε ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ καὶ ῥίψαν αὐτὸν τὸ δαιμόνιον εἰς μέσον ἐξῆλθεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ μηδὲν βλάψαν αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: kai epetimēsen autō ho Iēsous legōn Phimōthēti kai exelthe ap autou kai rhipsan auton to daimonion eis meson exēlthen ap autou mēden blabpsan auton
verses.study_note: 'Rebuked' (epetimēsen — he rebuked/commanded with authority; epitimaō = to rebuke, censure, charge with authority). 'Hold thy peace' (Phimōthēti — be muzzled, be silenced; phimoō = to muzzle; used of silencing oxen in 1 Corinthians 9:9 and of Jesus silencing the storm in Mark 4:39). The two commands are absolute: Silence! Out! Both are obeyed instantly. 'He came out of him, and hurt him not' (exēlthen...mēden blabsan auton — came out...having harmed him nothing). The demon obeys — throwing the man down in its departure (a final act of spite or involuntary convulsion) — but cannot harm him. Jesus's authority over demonic power is total: the demon must leave, must be silent, and cannot cause lasting harm. The man is left whole.

verses.kjv_text: And they were all amazed, and spake among themselves, saying, What a word is this! for with authority and power he commandeth the unclean spirits, and they come out.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο θάμβος ἐπὶ πάντας καὶ συνελάλουν πρὸς ἀλλήλους λέγοντες Τίς ὁ λόγος οὗτος ὅτι ἐν ἐξουσίᾳ καὶ δυνάμει ἐπιτάσσει τοῖς ἀκαθάρτοις πνεύμασιν καὶ ἐξέρχονται
verses.transliteration: kai egeneto thambos epi pantas kai synelaloun pros allēlous legontes Tis ho logos houtos hoti en exousia kai dynamei epitassei tois akathartois pneumasin kai exerchontai
verses.study_note: 'Amazement' (thambos — astonishment, wonder, reverential awe; a stronger word than thaumazō). 'What a word is this!' (Tis ho logos houtos — what is this word?). The congregation focuses on the word — Jesus commands with a word and the demon obeys. 'With authority and power' (en exousia kai dynamei — in authority and power; exousia = the right/authority to command; dynamis = the power/ability to execute). Both together: the right to command and the ability to compel. Jesus has both; the scribes and Pharisees have neither over demons. 'They come out' (exerchontai — present tense: they keep coming out; the generalisation from this one case to the ongoing pattern). The exorcism authenticates his teaching: the word that commands demons is the word that teaches with authority — both are from the same source.

verses.kjv_text: And the fame of him went out into every place of the country round about.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐξεπορεύετο ἦχος περὶ αὐτοῦ εἰς πάντα τόπον τῆς περιχώρου
verses.transliteration: kai exeporeueto ēchos peri autou eis panta topon tēs perichōrou
verses.study_note: 'The fame of him went out' (exeporeueto ēchos peri autou — there went out a sound/report about him; ēchos = a sound, noise, echo; our word 'echo' comes from this Greek word). The news of what happened in the synagogue spread organically throughout the surrounding region. Luke's verb ēchos (echo, sound) is vivid: the news resonated and reverberated like sound waves through the area. This growing fame will bring the crowds that fill the next scenes (vv.40-41). The public ministry of Jesus generates its own momentum: healings and exorcisms produce reports that draw more people seeking healing, which creates opportunities for more teaching and more healing. The kingdom advances both through direct ministry and through the reports it generates.

verses.kjv_text: And he arose out of the synagogue, and entered into Simon's house. And Simon's wife's mother was taken with a great fever; and they besought him for her.
verses.original_text: Ἀναστὰς δὲ ἀπὸ τῆς συναγωγῆς εἰσῆλθεν εἰς τὴν οἰκίαν Σίμωνος πενθερὰ δὲ τοῦ Σίμωνος ἦν συνεχομένη πυρετῷ μεγάλῳ καὶ ἠρώτησαν αὐτὸν περὶ αὐτῆς
verses.transliteration: Anastas de apo tēs synagōgēs eisēlthen eis tēn oikian Simōnos penthera de tou Simōnos ēn synechomenē pyretō megalō kai ērōtēsan auton peri autēs
verses.study_note: 'Simon's house' — Peter (Simon) was from Bethsaida (John 1:44) but had a house in Capernaum — his wife's family home, perhaps. Peter is already known to Jesus (he will be formally called in Luke 5:1-11), and the house becomes a base of operations. 'A great fever' (pyretō megalō — a great/high fever; Luke alone specifies 'great,' showing his physician's interest in degree of illness). 'They besought him for her' (ērōtēsan auton peri autēs — they asked him concerning her; the family intercedes for the sick woman). This is a pattern in the Gospels: others ask on behalf of the sick or demon-possessed. Intercession for others is honoured by Jesus consistently (cf. 7:2-3; 8:41-42; Matthew 15:22-28; John 11:3).

verses.kjv_text: And he stood over her, and rebuked the fever; and it left her: and immediately she arose and ministered unto them.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐπιστὰς ἐπάνω αὐτῆς ἐπετίμησεν τῷ πυρετῷ καὶ ἀφῆκεν αὐτήν παραχρῆμα δὲ ἀναστᾶσα διηκόνει αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: kai epistas epanō autēs epetimēsen tō pyretō kai aphēken autēn parachrēma de anastasa diēkonei autois
verses.study_note: 'Stood over her, and rebuked the fever' (epetimēsen tō pyretō — he rebuked the fever). Luke uniquely uses the same verb 'rebuked' (epitimaō) for the fever that was used for the demon in v.35. Whether the fever was itself demonic or simply a natural disease treated with the same sovereign authority is unclear; Luke's use of the same vocabulary suggests Jesus exercises the same royal authority over physical illness that he exercises over demonic possession. 'Immediately she arose and ministered' (parachrēma de anastasa diēkonei autois — at once, having arisen, she was serving them; diakoneo = to serve at table, to minister). The completeness of the healing: not just fever gone but full strength immediately restored. Her response to healing is service — the pattern of genuine discipleship.

verses.kjv_text: Now when the sun was setting, all they that had any sick with divers diseases brought them unto him; and he laid his hands on every one of them, and healed them.
verses.original_text: Δύνοντος δὲ τοῦ ἡλίου ἅπαντες ὅσοι εἶχον ἀσθενοῦντας νόσοις ποικίλαις ἤγαγον αὐτοὺς πρὸς αὐτόν ὁ δὲ ἑνὶ ἑκάστῳ αὐτῶν τὰς χεῖρας ἐπιτιθεὶς ἐθεράπευεν αὐτούς
verses.transliteration: Dynontos de tou hēliou hapantes hosoi eichon asthenountas nosois poikilais ēgagon autous pros auton ho de heni hekastō autōn tas cheiras epititheis etherapeuen autous
verses.study_note: 'When the sun was setting' (Dynontos tou hēliou — at the setting of the sun; the Sabbath ended at sunset, at which point it was permissible to carry the sick without violating Sabbath restrictions on work and burden-carrying). The community brings their sick to Jesus the moment the Sabbath legally ends — they have waited all day, watching Jesus's synagogue ministry from a distance. 'All they that had any sick' (hapantes hosoi eichon asthenountas — all those who had sick people). The universal scope of the healing ministry: every sick person in Capernaum is brought. 'He laid his hands on every one of them' (heni hekastō autōn tas cheiras epititheis — placing his hands on each one of them individually). Not a mass-healing word but individual, personal touch for each — the pastoral care of the infinite for the individual. 'And healed them' — the comprehensive result.

verses.kjv_text: And devils also came out of many, crying out, and saying, Thou art Christ the Son of God. And he rebuking them suffered them not to speak: for they knew that he was Christ.
verses.original_text: ἐξήρχετο δὲ καὶ δαιμόνια ἀπὸ πολλῶν κράζοντα καὶ λέγοντα ὅτι Σὺ εἶ ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ ἐπιτιμῶν οὐκ εἴα αὐτὰ λαλεῖν ὅτι ᾔδεισαν τὸν Χριστὸν αὐτὸν εἶναι
verses.transliteration: exērcheto de kai daimonia apo pollōn krazonta kai legonta hoti Sy ei ho huios tou theou kai epitimōn ouk eia auta lalein hoti ēdeisan ton Christon auton einai
verses.study_note: 'Thou art Christ the Son of God' — the demons' confession at Capernaum is even more explicit than in the synagogue: 'the Son of God.' 'He rebuking them suffered them not to speak' (epitimōn ouk eia auta lalein — rebuking, he was not permitting them to speak; the messianic secret). Why does Jesus silence the demons? Multiple reasons: (1) demonic testimony, however accurate, is tainted — they serve the wrong master; (2) the correct understanding of 'Christ' and 'Son of God' requires the cross and resurrection — premature Messianic proclamation would create wrong expectations; (3) Jesus's identity is to be revealed through his works and ultimately through his death and resurrection, not through demonic proclamation. 'For they knew that he was Christ' (oti ēdeisan ton Christon auton einai — because they knew him to be the Christ/Messiah). The irony: the demons know what most humans do not yet see.

verses.kjv_text: And when it was day, he departed and went into a desert place: and the people sought him, and came unto him, and stayed him, that he should not depart from them.
verses.original_text: Γενομένης δὲ ἡμέρας ἐξελθὼν ἐπορεύθη εἰς ἔρημον τόπον καὶ οἱ ὄχλοι ἐπεζήτουν αὐτόν καὶ ἦλθον ἕως αὐτοῦ καὶ κατεῖχον αὐτὸν τοῦ μὴ πορεύεσθαι ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: Genomenēs de hēmeras exelthōn eporeuthē eis erēmon topon kai hoi ochloi epezētoun auton kai ēlthan heōs autou kai kateichon auton tou mē poreuesthai ap autōn
verses.study_note: 'A desert place' (erēmon topon — a solitary, deserted place) — Jesus's withdrawal for prayer and solitude (cf. 5:16; Mark 1:35: 'in the morning, rising up a great while before day, he went out, and departed into a solitary place, and there prayed'). Prayer marks every decisive moment of Jesus's life in Luke. After a night of healing and exorcism, the morning is for prayer. 'The people sought him' (epezētoun auton — were seeking him; imperfect: persistent seeking). 'Stayed him, that he should not depart from them' (kateichon auton tou mē poreuesthai — they were holding him back from going away; katechō = to hold down, restrain; the people want to keep Jesus as their local healer and teacher). Jesus resists this local ownership of his ministry — his mission is not to one place but to the world.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, I must preach the kingdom of God to other cities also: for therefore am I sent.
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς ὅτι καὶ ταῖς ἑτέραις πόλεσιν εὐαγγελίσασθαί με δεῖ τὴν βασιλείαν τοῦ θεοῦ ὅτι ἐπὶ τοῦτο ἀπεστάλην
verses.transliteration: ho de eipen pros autous hoti kai tais heterais polesin euangelisasthai me dei tēn basileian tou theou hoti epi touto apestalēn
verses.study_note: 'I must' (dei — it is divinely necessary; the word of divine compulsion). 'Preach the kingdom of God to other cities also' (tais heterais polesin euangelisasthai — to proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other cities also). Jesus's mission is not local but universal. 'For therefore am I sent' (oti epi touto apestalēn — because for this purpose I have been sent; apostellō = to send with authority and commission; the same verb as 'apostle'). Jesus defines himself in terms of his commission: he is the Sent One, the Apostle of the Father (Hebrews 3:1). His purpose is determined not by popular demand (Capernaum wants him to stay) but by divine commission. The 'kingdom of God' (basileia tou theou) — the central theme of Jesus's preaching in Luke — is the dynamic reign of God breaking in through Jesus's words and deeds.

verses.kjv_text: And he preached in the synagogues of Galilee.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἦν κηρύσσων εἰς τὰς συναγωγὰς τῆς Γαλιλαίας
verses.transliteration: kai ēn kēryssōn eis tas synagōgas tēs Galilaias
verses.study_note: 'He preached in the synagogues of Galilee' (ēn kēryssōn — was proclaiming; imperfect: ongoing, habitual). Luke's summary statement closes the chapter and anticipates the extended Galilean ministry that follows. Some manuscripts read 'Judea' instead of 'Galilee' — if so, it refers to the broader Jewish territory (Judea in its widest sense including Galilee). The synagogue network provided Jesus with a ready-made communication infrastructure: every Jewish village had one, operating on a regular weekly schedule, with an established practice of inviting visiting teachers to speak. Jesus uses this network systematically to proclaim the kingdom throughout Galilee. The circuit preaching of the itinerant prophet-teacher is the model for the apostolic mission that follows in Acts.

meta.overview: Luke 5 narrates a series of defining encounters that establish the nature of Jesus's ministry and the pattern of discipleship. The Miraculous Catch of Fish (vv.1-11) introduces Simon Peter, James, and John as disciples through an overpowering miracle: after a fruitless night of fishing, Jesus commands them to launch into the deep, and their nets nearly break with fish. Peter's response — 'Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord' (v.8) — captures the pattern of all genuine encounter with holiness: overwhelming conviction of unworthiness followed by divine commission: 'Fear not; from henceforth thou shalt catch men' (v.10). The Cleansing of the Leper (vv.12-16) demonstrates Jesus's willingness to touch the untouchable: 'he put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will: be thou clean' (v.13). Jesus's touch reverses the expected direction of contagion — instead of being defiled by the leper, he makes the leper clean. The Healing of the Paralytic (vv.17-26) introduces the first major conflict with the Pharisees and scribes: Jesus forgives the paralytic's sins (v.20), triggering the charge of blasphemy. His response — 'Which is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Rise up and walk?' (v.23) — demonstrates that his authority to heal physically is evidence of his authority to forgive spiritually. The Call of Levi (vv.27-32) shows Jesus eating with tax collectors and sinners, prompting the question about his association with the disreputable. His answer defines his entire ministry: 'I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance' (v.32). The chapter closes with two 'new cloth/new wine' parables (vv.33-39) addressing the incompatibility of the old and new: the kingdom of God cannot be contained within the forms of old religion.
meta.theme: The Call and Conversion — From Unworthiness to Commission, From Sinners to Saints
meta.keyWordMeaning: Release, forgiveness, remission — from aphiēmi (to send away, to let go, to release from obligation or penalty). Aphesis appears twice in the pivotal healing of the paralytic: Jesus sees the faith of the men who carry the paralytic and says 'Man, thy sins are forgiven thee' (apheōntai soi hai hamartiai sou — your sins have been released/sent away, v.20). The scribes and Pharisees charge blasphemy: only God can aphiēmi sins. Jesus responds by demonstrating his divine authority through the physical healing. The word captures the essence of the gospel: sins are not merely overlooked or minimised but actually sent away — their guilt and power released. The same word is used for the release of prisoners, the cancellation of debts, and the forgiveness of sins throughout Luke and Acts. The forgiveness Jesus offers is real, complete, and divinely authoritative — not the therapeutic reassurance of a sympathetic teacher but the judicial release of a divine King.
outline: The Miraculous Catch of Fish and the Call of Simon (vv.1-11): The people pressed upon Jesus to hear the word of God as he stood by the lake of Gennesaret. He saw two ships by the lake and entered into Simon's ship and taught the people. When he had left speaking he said — Launch out into the deep and let down your nets. Simon answered — Master we have toiled all the night and taken nothing nevertheless at thy word I will let down the net. When they had done this they enclosed a great multitude of fishes. They beckoned to their partners James and John in the other ship. When Simon Peter saw it he fell down at Jesus's knees saying — Depart from me for I am a sinful man O Lord. Jesus said — Fear not from henceforth thou shalt catch men. And when they had brought their ships to land they forsook all and followed him

The Cleansing of the Leper (vv.12-16): A man full of leprosy fell on his face and besought him saying — Lord if thou wilt thou canst make me clean. Jesus put forth his hand and touched him saying — I will be thou clean. The leprosy immediately departed. Jesus charged him to tell no man but to go to the priest. But so much the more went there a fame abroad of him and great multitudes came to hear and to be healed. He withdrew himself into the wilderness and prayed

The Healing of the Paralytic (vv.17-26): Pharisees and doctors of the law were sitting by who had come from every town of Galilee and Judaea and Jerusalem. Men brought a man lying on a bed and sought means to bring him before Jesus. They went upon the housetop and let him down through the tiling into the midst before Jesus. When Jesus saw their faith he said — Man thy sins are forgiven thee. The scribes and Pharisees reasoned — Who is this that speaketh blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God alone? Jesus said — Whether is it easier to say Thy sins be forgiven thee or to say Rise up and walk? But that ye may know the Son of man hath power on earth to forgive sins — he said — Rise take up thy couch and go to thine house. He arose immediately and they were all amazed and glorified God

The Call of Levi and the Question about Fasting (vv.27-39): Jesus saw a publican named Levi sitting at the receipt of custom and said — Follow me. He left all rose up and followed him. Levi made him a great feast and there was a great company of publicans. The Pharisees murmured — Why do ye eat and drink with publicans and sinners? Jesus answered — They that are whole need not a physician but they that are sick. I came not to call the righteous but sinners to repentance. The disciples of John fast often and make prayers likewise the Pharisees but thine eat and drink. Jesus said — Can ye make the children of the bridechamber fast while the bridegroom is with them? The days will come when the bridegroom shall be taken away then shall they fast. New cloth on an old garment makes a worse rent. New wine must be put in new bottles

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, that, as the people pressed upon him to hear the word of God, he stood by the lake of Gennesaret,
verses.original_text: Ἐγένετο δὲ ἐν τῷ τὸν ὄχλον ἐπικεῖσθαι αὐτῷ καὶ ἀκούειν τὸν λόγον τοῦ θεοῦ καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν ἑστὼς παρὰ τὴν λίμνην Γεννησαρέτ
verses.transliteration: Egeneto de en tō ton ochlon epikeisthai autō kai akouein ton logon tou theou kai autos ēn hestōs para tēn limnēn Gennēsaret
verses.study_note: 'The people pressed upon him' (ton ochlon epikeisthai autō — the crowd was pressing on him; epikeimai = to lie upon, press upon, throng). The demand for Jesus's teaching is overwhelming. 'To hear the word of God' (akouein ton logon tou theou — to hear the word of God) — Luke's identification of Jesus's teaching as 'the word of God' is significant: Jesus does not merely teach about God's word but speaks it. 'Lake of Gennesaret' (limnēn Gennēsareth — the lake of Gennesaret; the Sea of Galilee is properly a lake; also called Sea of Tiberias, John 6:1). The lake setting introduces the call narrative: the miraculous fish catch will demonstrate authority over the natural world, providing the compelling context for the disciples' call.

verses.kjv_text: And saw two ships standing by the lake: but the fishermen were gone out of them, and were washing their nets.
verses.original_text: καὶ εἶδεν δύο πλοῖα ἑστῶτα παρὰ τὴν λίμνην οἱ δὲ ἁλιεῖς ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν ἀποβάντες ἔπλυνον τὰ δίκτυα
verses.transliteration: kai eiden dyo ploia hestōta para tēn limnēn hoi de halieis ap autōn apobantes eplyunon ta diktya
verses.study_note: 'Two ships' (dyo ploia — two boats). One will prove to be Simon's (v.3). 'Washing their nets' (eplyunon ta diktya — were washing the nets; plynō = to wash; diktyon = a fishing net; plural). Washing nets after a fruitless night's fishing is a detail of professional frustration and faithful duty: even after catching nothing, the proper care of equipment continues. The fishermen have gone through the night, come up empty, and are now doing the unglamorous work of cleaning. Into this scene of professional disappointment and mundane labour, Jesus arrives with a word that will transform everything.

verses.kjv_text: And he entered into one of the ships, which was Simon's, and prayed him that he would thrust out a little from the land. And he sat down, and taught the people out of the ship.
verses.original_text: ἐμβὰς δὲ εἰς ἓν τῶν πλοίων ὃ ἦν Σίμωνος ἠρώτησεν αὐτὸν ἀπὸ τῆς γῆς ἐπαναγαγεῖν ὀλίγον καὶ καθίσας ἐδίδασκεν ἐκ τοῦ πλοίου τοὺς ὄχλους
verses.transliteration: embas de eis hen tōn ploiōn ho ēn Simōnos ērōtēsen auton apo tēs gēs epanagagein oligon kai kathisas edidasken ek tou ploiou tous ochlous
verses.study_note: 'He entered into one of the ships, which was Simon's' — Jesus commandeers Simon's boat as a floating pulpit without ceremony or formal introduction. Simon's compliance at this stage suggests a prior relationship (cf. 4:38 — Jesus had healed Peter's mother-in-law). 'Thrust out a little from the land' (eparanagagein oligon — push out a little; the lake surface provides natural amplification for the teacher's voice, and a small distance prevents the crowd from crowding further onto the boat). 'Sat down, and taught' (kathisas edidasken — sitting he was teaching; the teacher's seated posture). Jesus uses every available platform — synagogues, hillsides, temple courts, and now a fishing boat. The kingdom message finds expression through whatever is at hand.

verses.kjv_text: Now when he had left speaking, he said unto Simon, Launch out into the deep, and let down your nets for a draught.
verses.original_text: ὡς δὲ ἐπαύσατο λαλῶν εἶπεν πρὸς τὸν Σίμωνα Ἐπανάγαγε εἰς τὸ βάθος καὶ χαλάσατε τὰ δίκτυα ὑμῶν εἰς ἄγραν
verses.transliteration: hōs de epausato lalōn eipen pros ton Simōna Epanaggage eis to bathos kai chalasate ta diktya hymōn eis agran
verses.study_note: 'Launch out into the deep' (Epanagage eis to bathos — push out into the deep; bathos = depth). After teaching, Jesus gives a fishing instruction to a professional fisherman — a startling reversal of expertise. 'Let down your nets for a draught' (chalasate ta diktya hymōn eis agran — lower your nets for a catch; chalō = to lower, let down; agra = a catch, haul of fish). The instruction violates professional wisdom: (1) daytime fishing in deep water was not productive — the fish in the Sea of Galilee stayed deep in the day and came near the surface at night; (2) the nets had just been washed, meaning they were clean and ready for storage, not re-use. Obeying this command requires faith against experience, trust against expertise, submission against professional pride.

verses.kjv_text: And Simon answering said unto him, Master, we have toiled all the night, and have taken nothing: nevertheless at thy word I will let down the net.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς Σίμων εἶπεν Ἐπιστάτα δι᾽ ὅλης νυκτὸς κοπιάσαντες οὐδὲν ἐλάβομεν ἐπὶ δὲ τῷ ῥήματί σου χαλάσω τὸ δίκτυον
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis Simōn eipen Epistata di holēs nyktos kopiasantes ouden elabomen epi de tō rhēmati sou chalasō to diktyon
verses.study_note: 'Master' (Epistata — overseer, commander; a different word from Didaskalos [Teacher] or Kyrios [Lord]; epistates = one set over, a commander; used exclusively in Luke for those addressing Jesus [7:13; 8:24, 45; 9:33, 49; 17:13]). 'Toiled all the night and have taken nothing' — professional fishermen on their home lake, using the best conditions (nighttime) and methods, completely empty-handed. 'Nevertheless at thy word' (epi de tō rhēmati sou — but at your word; rhēma = word, command; epi = upon, on the basis of). Peter's response is the paradigm of faith: not logical conviction (the conditions argue against it) but trust in the word of Jesus. The 'nevertheless' is the hinge of faith — overriding experience, professional judgment, and natural reasoning with a simple trust in the word of Jesus.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had this done, they inclosed a great multitude of fishes: and their net brake.
verses.original_text: καὶ τοῦτο ποιήσαντες συνέκλεισαν πλῆθος ἰχθύων πολύ διερρήσσετο δὲ τὸ δίκτυον αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: kai touto poiēsantes synekleisan plēthos ichthyōn poly dierrēsseto de to diktyon autōn
verses.study_note: 'They inclosed a great multitude of fishes' (synekleisan plēthos ichthyōn poly — they enclosed a great multitude of fish; synkleiō = to shut together, encircle, enclose; the same word in Galatians 3:23 for the Law's enclosing/imprisoning function). 'Their net brake' (dierrēsseto to diktyon autōn — the net was breaking; imperfect: it was in the process of breaking, under the strain). The miracle is immediate and overwhelming: where hours of professional effort produced nothing, a single obedient lowering of the net at Jesus's word produces a catch that nearly destroys the net. The miracle is not subtle or ambiguous — it is physically and economically staggering. The contrast with the fruitless night is total: 'have taken nothing' (v.5) becomes 'a great multitude' (v.6) in an instant.

verses.kjv_text: And they beckoned unto their partners, which were in the other ship, that they should come and help them. And they came, and filled both the ships, so that they began to sink.
verses.original_text: καὶ κατένευσαν τοῖς μετόχοις τοῖς ἐν τῷ ἑτέρῳ πλοίῳ τοῦ ἐλθόντας συλλαβέσθαι αὐτοῖς καὶ ἦλθον καὶ ἔπλησαν ἀμφότερα τὰ πλοῖα ὥστε βυθίζεσθαι αὐτά
verses.transliteration: kai katineusan tois metochois tois en tō heterō ploiō tou elthontas syllabsthai autois kai ēlthan kai eplēsan amphotera ta ploia hōste bythizesthai auta
verses.study_note: 'Beckoned' (katineusan — signalled by nodding/gesturing; the situation was too loud or distant for calling out). 'Their partners' (metochois — partners, sharers, associates; metochos = one who shares in something together; the word used in Hebrews 1:9 for our sharing in Christ). 'Both the ships, so that they began to sink' (amphotera ta ploia hōste bythizesthai — both boats so that they were sinking; bythizō = to cause to sink, to submerge). The catch is beyond the capacity of both boats combined. The catch of fish is so abundant that it threatens to overwhelm the very vessels meant to contain it — an image of the abundance of grace that will overflow every human container in the mission ahead. The new wine will burst old wineskins (v.37-38).

verses.kjv_text: When Simon Peter saw it, he fell down at Jesus' knees, saying, Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord.
verses.original_text: ἰδὼν δὲ Σίμων Πέτρος προσέπεσεν τοῖς γόνασιν Ἰησοῦ λέγων Ἔξελθε ἀπ᾽ ἐμοῦ ὅτι ἀνὴρ ἁμαρτωλός εἰμι κύριε
verses.transliteration: idōn de Simōn Petros prosepesen tois gonasin Iēsou legōn Exelthe ap emou hoti anēr hamartōlos eimi kyrie
verses.study_note: 'When Simon Peter saw it' (idōn — having seen; the moment of recognition). 'Fell down at Jesus's knees' (prosepesen tois gonasin Iēsou — fell before the knees of Jesus; prospipto = to fall forward before; the posture of prostration before a recognised superior, divine or human). This is Peter's decisive moment of christological recognition: the miracle reveals not merely a great teacher but a divine figure before whom human unworthiness is exposed. 'Depart from me; for I am a sinful man, O Lord' (Exelthe ap emou hoti anēr hamartōlos eimi, kyrie — depart from me because I am a sinful man, Lord). The pattern mirrors Isaiah 6:5 ('Woe is me! for I am undone; because I am a man of unclean lips') and Job 42:5-6. The encounter with holiness does not produce pride or comfort but acute moral self-awareness. Peter does not ask for a teaching; he asks Jesus to leave — the instinctive response of sinfulness before purity.

verses.kjv_text: For he was astonished, and all that were with him, at the draught of the fishes which they had taken:
verses.original_text: θάμβος γὰρ περιέσχεν αὐτὸν καὶ πάντας τοὺς σὺν αὐτῷ ἐπὶ τῇ ἄγρᾳ τῶν ἰχθύων ᾗ συνέλαβον
verses.transliteration: thambos gar perieschen auton kai pantas tous syn autō epi tē agra tōn ichthyōn hē synelabon
verses.study_note: 'Astonishment' (thambos — awe, wonder; periechō = to surround, encompass; the awe encompassed or gripped him completely). The same word in 4:36 for the synagogue's reaction to the exorcism. The cause of Peter's prostration is explicitly the fish miracle, not some independent spiritual vision. The physical miracle is what reveals the divine — the extraordinary catch of fish from water that had yielded nothing is the undeniable sign of supernatural authority over nature. 'All that were with him' — the reaction is universal among the fishermen present. The miracle affects all who witness it equally; Peter's response is uniquely personal but his astonishment is shared.

verses.kjv_text: And so was also James, and John, the sons of Zebedee, which were partners with Simon. And Jesus said unto Simon, Fear not; from henceforth thou shalt catch men.
verses.original_text: ὁμοίως δὲ καὶ Ἰάκωβον καὶ Ἰωάννην υἱοὺς Ζεβεδαίου οἳ ἦσαν κοινωνοὶ τῷ Σίμωνι καὶ εἶπεν πρὸς τὸν Σίμωνα ὁ Ἰησοῦς Μὴ φοβοῦ ἀπὸ τοῦ νῦν ἀνθρώπους ἔσῃ ζωγρῶν
verses.transliteration: homoiōs de kai Iakōbon kai Iōannēn huious Zebedaiou hoi ēsan koinōnoi tō Simōni kai eipen pros ton Simōna ho Iēsous Mē phobou apo tou nyn anthrōpous esē zōgrōn
verses.study_note: 'James, and John, the sons of Zebedee, partners with Simon' (koinōnoi tō Simōni — partners/sharers with Simon; koinōnos = one who has common/shared participation). The same word-root as koinōnia (fellowship). 'Fear not' (Mē phobou — the angelic formula of divine encounter; cf. 1:13, 30; 2:10). Jesus addresses Peter's prostration not with rebuke or theological explanation but with commission: he does not need to depart from Peter — Peter needs to follow him. 'From henceforth thou shalt catch men' (apo tou nyn anthrōpous esē zōgrōn — from now you will be catching humans alive; zōgreō = to catch alive, to take captive alive). The fishing metaphor is transformed: as you have caught fish, so you will catch people — for life rather than death. The commercial skill becomes a spiritual vocation.

verses.kjv_text: And when they had brought their ships to land, they forsook all, and followed him.
verses.original_text: καὶ καταγαγόντες τὰ πλοῖα ἐπὶ τὴν γῆν ἀφέντες ἅπαντα ἠκολούθησαν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: kai katagagontes ta ploia epi tēn gēn aphentes hapanta ēkolouthēsan autō
verses.study_note: 'They forsook all, and followed him' (aphentes hapanta ēkolouthēsan autō — having left everything they followed him; akoloutheo = to follow, to be a disciple of). The response to the overwhelming miracle and the divine call is total and immediate. 'All' (hapanta — everything; emphatic form: absolutely everything). Not partial commitment or tentative discipleship — they leave the boats, the nets, the fish (the miraculous catch, their business, their livelihood), and their families. Luke's 'forsook all' (contrast Mark 1:20 where they simply left their father and the hired servants) is the most radical statement of the call's total claim. Discipleship in Luke costs everything: 'Whosoever he be of you that forsaketh not all that he hath, he cannot be my disciple' (14:33).

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass, when he was in a certain city, behold a man full of leprosy: who seeing Jesus fell on his face, and besought him, saying, Lord, if thou wilt, thou canst make me clean.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν τῷ εἶναι αὐτὸν ἐν μιᾷ τῶν πόλεων καὶ ἰδοὺ ἀνὴρ πλήρης λέπρας καὶ ἰδὼν τὸν Ἰησοῦν πεσὼν ἐπὶ πρόσωπον ἐδεήθη αὐτοῦ λέγων Κύριε ἐὰν θέλῃς δύνασαί με καθαρίσαι
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en tō einai auton en mia tōn poleōn kai idou anēr plērēs lepras kai idōn ton Iēsoun pesōn epi prosōpon edēthē autou legōn Kyrie ean thelēs dynasai me katharisai
verses.study_note: 'A man full of leprosy' (anēr plērēs lepras — a man full of leprosy; Luke alone specifies 'full' — an advanced case). Leprosy (lepra — a term covering various severe skin conditions in the ancient world) rendered a person ceremonially unclean and socially isolated (Leviticus 13:45-46: 'unclean, unclean...he shall dwell alone; without the camp shall his habitation be'). 'Fell on his face' (pesōn epi prosōpon — falling on his face; the extreme posture of supplication and reverence). 'If thou wilt, thou canst' (ean thelēs, dynasai — if you are willing, you are able). The man has no doubt about Jesus's power (dynasai = you are able); his only uncertainty is the will. This is genuine faith: acknowledging both the divine ability and the divine freedom to act as he chooses. He does not presume on grace but appeals to it.

verses.kjv_text: And he put forth his hand, and touched him, saying, I will: be thou clean. And immediately the leprosy departed from him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐκτείνας τὴν χεῖρα ἥψατο αὐτοῦ λέγων Θέλω καθαρίσθητι καὶ εὐθέως ἡ λέπρα ἀπῆλθεν ἀπ᾽ αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai ekteinas tēn cheira hepsato autou legōn Thelō katharisthēti kai eutheōs hē lepra apēlthen ap autou
verses.study_note: 'He put forth his hand, and touched him' (ekteinas tēn cheira hepsato autou — stretching out his hand he touched him; haptō = to touch, attach, make contact). The touch is theologically explosive: under Mosaic law, touching a leper made the healthy person unclean (Leviticus 5:3). Jesus reverses this: instead of contracting uncleanness, he imparts cleanness. The direction of contagion reverses. The Holy One does not become defiled by contact with defilement; instead, defilement yields to holiness. 'I will' (Thelō — I am willing; the direct answer to the leper's conditional 'if thou wilt'). No condition, no delay, no qualification — direct, immediate, unequivocal. 'Immediately the leprosy departed' (eutheōs hē lepra apēlthen — at once the leprosy went away). The word and the touch are instantaneous in their effect.

verses.kjv_text: And he charged him to tell no man: but go, and shew thyself to the priest, and offer for thy cleansing, according as Moses commanded, for a testimony unto them.
verses.original_text: καὶ αὐτὸς παρήγγειλεν αὐτῷ μηδενὶ εἰπεῖν ἀλλὰ ἀπελθὼν δεῖξον σεαυτὸν τῷ ἱερεῖ καὶ προσένεγκε περὶ τοῦ καθαρισμοῦ σου καθὼς προσέταξεν Μωϋσῆς εἰς μαρτύριον αὐτοῖς
verses.transliteration: kai autos parēngeilen autō mēdeni eipein alla apelthōn deixon seauton tō hierei kai prosenenke peri tou katharismou sou kathōs prosetaxen Mōysēs eis martyrion autois
verses.study_note: 'Tell no man' — the messianic secret (cf. 4:41). The reasons for silence include: (1) avoiding the wrong kind of Messianic expectation (purely political/miraculous); (2) allowing the religious authorities to evaluate the healing according to the proper protocol before Jesus's identity became publicly controversial; (3) protecting the man's acceptance back into society — his social reintegration requires the priestly certification before anything else. 'Shew thyself to the priest...as Moses commanded' (Leviticus 14:1-32 — a detailed cleansing ritual involving washing, offerings, and priestly inspection). 'For a testimony unto them' (eis martyrion autois — for a witness/testimony to them; the healed man's presentation to the priests is itself a proclamation that God has acted).

verses.kjv_text: But so much the more went there a fame abroad of him: and great multitudes came together to hear, and to be healed by him of their infirmities.
verses.original_text: διήρχετο δὲ μᾶλλον ὁ λόγος περὶ αὐτοῦ καὶ συνήρχοντο ὄχλοι πολλοὶ ἀκούειν καὶ θεραπεύεσθαι ἀπὸ τῶν ἀσθενειῶν αὐτῶν
verses.transliteration: diērcheto de mallon ho logos peri autou kai synērchonto ochloi polloi akouein kai therapeuasthai apo tōn astheniōn autōn
verses.study_note: 'So much the more went there a fame' (mallon ho logos — the word/report spread all the more). The command to silence paradoxically accelerates the spreading of the news. 'Great multitudes came together to hear, and to be healed' (synērchonto ochloi polloi akouein kai therapeuasthai — great crowds were coming together to hear and to be healed). The two verbs define the crowd's motivation: hearing and healing. Luke places hearing first — even the crowds seeking physical healing come for the word. The word and the works are inseparable in Jesus's ministry: the teaching creates the need for signs, and the signs confirm the teaching. 'Infirmities' (astheniōn — weaknesses, sicknesses; astheneia = want of strength, incapacity).

verses.kjv_text: And he withdrew himself into the wilderness, and prayed.
verses.original_text: αὐτὸς δὲ ἦν ὑποχωρῶν ἐν ταῖς ἐρήμοις καὶ προσευχόμενος
verses.transliteration: autos de ēn hypochōrōn en tais erēmois kai proseuchomenos
verses.study_note: 'He withdrew himself into the wilderness, and prayed' (ēn hypochōrōn en tais erēmois kai proseuchomenos — he was withdrawing into the desert places and praying; imperfect: a continuous, habitual pattern). Despite the overwhelming crowds and the press of human need, Jesus regularly withdraws for prayer. This is not retreat from ministry but the source of its power. The wilderness prayer (erēmois kai proseuchomenos) is one of Luke's characteristic notes (3:21; 6:12; 9:18, 28-29; 11:1; 22:41-44). The pattern is consistent: great public ministry is balanced by private prayer. The Lukan Jesus does not burn out through endless public demand; he regularly returns to the source. The ministry flows from the prayer, not the other way around.

verses.kjv_text: And it came to pass on a certain day, as he was teaching, that there were Pharisees and doctors of the law sitting by, which were come out of every town of Galilee, and Judaea, and Jerusalem: and the power of the Lord was present to heal them.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐγένετο ἐν μιᾷ τῶν ἡμερῶν καὶ αὐτὸς ἦν διδάσκων καὶ ἦσαν καθήμενοι Φαρισαῖοι καὶ νομοδιδάσκαλοι οἳ ἦσαν ἐληλυθότες ἐκ πάσης κώμης τῆς Γαλιλαίας καὶ Ἰουδαίας καὶ Ἱερουσαλήμ καὶ δύναμις κυρίου ἦν εἰς τὸ ἰᾶσθαι αὐτόν
verses.transliteration: Kai egeneto en mia tōn hēmerōn kai autos ēn didaskōn kai ēsan kathēmenoi Pharisaioi kai nomodidaskaloi hoi ēsan elēluthotes ek pasēs kōmēs tēs Galilaias kai Ioudaias kai Hierousalēm kai dynamis kyriou ēn eis to iasthai auton
verses.study_note: 'Pharisees and doctors of the law' (Pharisaioi kai nomodidaskaloi — Pharisees and teachers of the law; nomodidaskalos = law-teacher, a Rabbinic scholar; appears only here and Acts 5:34; 1 Timothy 1:7 in the NT). The observation committee has arrived: representatives from Jerusalem and throughout the Jewish world, come to assess Jesus. 'Sitting by' (kathēmenoi — seated; the posture of official observers). 'From every town' — a coordinated, widespread official investigation. 'The power of the Lord was present to heal' (dynamis kyriou ēn eis to iasthai — the Lord's power was there for healing). Luke's narrative note sets the scene for the miracle: the official observers are present precisely when the most controversial miracle-and-forgiveness occurs.

verses.kjv_text: And, behold, men brought in a bed a man which was taken with a palsy: and they sought means to bring him in, and to lay him before him.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδοὺ ἄνδρες φέροντες ἐπὶ κλίνης ἄνθρωπον ὃς ἦν παραλελυμένος καὶ ἐζήτουν αὐτὸν εἰσενεγκεῖν καὶ θεῖναι ἐνώπιον αὐτοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai idou andres pherontes epi klinēs anthrōpon hos ēn paralelymenos kai ezētoun auton eisenenkein kai theinai enōpion autou
verses.study_note: 'A man...taken with a palsy' (anthrōpon hos ēn paralelymenos — a man who was paralysed; paralyo = to loose beside, to cripple; the perfect passive participle: permanently paralysed). 'They sought means to bring him in' (ezētoun eisenenkein — were seeking how to carry him in; imperfect: they were earnestly seeking a way, actively trying). The paralytic himself cannot act; his friends act on his behalf. The crowd is so great that normal entry is impossible. Their persistence — seeking an alternative route — exemplifies the kind of faith that will not be turned back by obstacles. They will not take 'it's too crowded' as a final answer.

verses.kjv_text: And when they could not find by what way they might bring him in because of the multitude, they went upon the housetop, and let him down through the tiling with his couch into the midst before Jesus.
verses.original_text: καὶ μὴ εὑρόντες ποίας εἰσενέγκωσιν αὐτὸν διὰ τὸν ὄχλον ἀναβάντες ἐπὶ τὸ δῶμα διὰ τῶν κεράμων καθῆκαν αὐτὸν σὺν τῷ κλινιδίῳ εἰς τὸ μέσον ἔμπροσθεν τοῦ Ἰησοῦ
verses.transliteration: kai mē heurontes poias eisenegkōsin auton dia ton ochlon anabantes epi to dōma dia tōn keramōn kathēkan auton syn tō klinidiō eis to meson emprosthen tou Iēsou
verses.study_note: 'Went upon the housetop' (anabantes epi to dōma — going up to the roof; dōma = flat roof; Palestinian houses typically had flat roofs accessed by external stairs, used for drying grain, sleeping in summer, etc.). 'Through the tiling' (dia tōn keramōn — through the tiles; keramos = tile; Luke's version specifies tiled roofing, reflecting Hellenistic construction; Mark 2:4 says they 'uncovered the roof'). 'Let him down...into the midst' (kathēkan auton...eis to meson — they lowered him into the middle; kathiēmi = to let down). The friends lower the paralytic through the opened roof directly in front of Jesus, in full view of the official observers. The disruption is total, dramatic, and irreversible — there is no quiet approach here. Their faith is expressed not in words but in action, not in prayer but in physical effort and creative problem-solving.

verses.kjv_text: And when he saw their faith, he said unto him, Man, thy sins are forgiven thee.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἰδὼν τὴν πίστιν αὐτῶν εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ἄνθρωπε ἀφέωνταί σοι αἱ ἁμαρτίαι σου
verses.transliteration: kai idōn tēn pistin autōn eipen autō Anthrōpe apheōntai soi hai hamartiai sou
verses.study_note: 'When he saw their faith' (idōn tēn pistin autōn — seeing the faith of them; plural: the faith of the friends, not just the paralytic). Faith is made visible in action (lowering a man through a roof). Jesus responds not to the physical need but to the spiritual reality behind it. 'Man, thy sins are forgiven thee' (Anthrōpe apheōntai soi hai hamartiai sou — Man, your sins have been forgiven/sent away; apheōntai = perfect passive: the forgiving has happened and the result stands). Jesus addresses the deeper need first — not the paralysis but the sin. The Jewish mind (and the OT in Psalm 103:3) connected physical illness with sin; Jesus meets this expectation, but his forgiveness is not conditioned on the healing nor the healing on the forgiveness. Both are sovereign acts of the Son of Man.

verses.kjv_text: And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, Who is this which speaketh blasphemies? Who can forgive sins, but God alone?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἤρξαντο διαλογίζεσθαι οἱ γραμματεῖς καὶ οἱ Φαρισαῖοι λέγοντες Τίς ἐστιν οὗτος ὃς λαλεῖ βλασφημίας τίς δύναται ἁμαρτίας ἀφεῖναι εἰ μὴ μόνος ὁ θεός
verses.transliteration: kai ērxanto dialogizesthai hoi grammateis kai hoi Pharisaioi legontes Tis estin houtos hos lalei blasphēmias tis dynatai hamartias apheinai ei mē monos ho theos
verses.study_note: 'Who is this which speaketh blasphemies?' (Tis estin houtos hos lalei blasphēmias — who is this who speaks blasphemies?). The scribes' reasoning is theologically correct on its own terms: blasphemy (blasphēmia) is speaking in a way that claims divine prerogatives or dishonours God. 'Who can forgive sins, but God alone?' (tis dynatai hamartias apheinai ei mē monos ho theos — who is able to release/forgive sins except God alone?). This is orthodox Jewish theology: only God can forgive sins, because all sin is ultimately against God (Psalm 51:4: 'Against thee, thee only, have I sinned'). The scribes' logic is impeccable: if only God forgives sin, and Jesus claims to forgive sin, then either Jesus is God or Jesus is a blasphemer. There is no middle option — and Jesus will demonstrate which it is.

verses.kjv_text: But when Jesus perceived their thoughts, he answering said unto them, What reason ye in your hearts?
verses.original_text: ἐπιγνοὺς δὲ ὁ Ἰησοῦς τοὺς διαλογισμοὺς αὐτῶν ἀποκριθεὶς εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Τί διαλογίζεσθε ἐν ταῖς καρδίαις ὑμῶν
verses.transliteration: epignous de ho Iēsous tous dialogismous autōn apokritheis eipen pros autous Ti dialogizesthe en tais kardiais hymōn
verses.study_note: 'Jesus perceived their thoughts' (epignous tous dialogismous autōn — having fully known their inner deliberations; epiginōskō = to know fully, perceive accurately; dialogismos = inner reasoning, deliberation). The scribes and Pharisees have not spoken aloud — they are 'reasoning' (dialogizesthai) 'in their hearts' (en tais kardiais) — yet Jesus knows their thoughts. This supernatural knowledge of internal reasoning is a divine attribute (1 Kings 8:39; Psalm 139:2-4; Jeremiah 17:10). Jesus demonstrates the very omniscience that makes him capable of the forgiveness he has just pronounced: he who knows all sin can also forgive all sin. 'What reason ye in your hearts?' — the question is not ignorant enquiry but prophetic exposure.

verses.kjv_text: Whether is easier, to say, Thy sins be forgiven thee; or to say, Rise up and walk?
verses.original_text: τί ἐστιν εὐκοπώτερον εἰπεῖν Ἀφέωνταί σοι αἱ ἁμαρτίαι σου ἢ εἰπεῖν Ἔγειρε καὶ περιπάτει
verses.transliteration: ti estin eukopōteron eipein Apheōntai soi hai hamartiai sou ē eipein Egeire kai peripatei
verses.study_note: 'Which is easier, to say...Thy sins be forgiven...or...Rise up and walk?' (eukopōteron — easier; from eu + kopos = good + labour = not laborious, easy). The question is a masterpiece of argumentation. In one sense, 'thy sins are forgiven' is easier to say because it is unverifiable — no one can immediately check if forgiveness has occurred. 'Rise up and walk' is harder because it is immediately falsifiable. But in terms of the underlying authority, forgiving sins requires divine prerogative while healing paralysis requires divine power — both require God. Jesus's argument: I will prove the harder thing (verifiable healing) to demonstrate that I also have the authority for the easier-to-say-but-divinely-requiring thing (forgiveness). The visible miracle authenticates the invisible spiritual reality.

verses.kjv_text: But that ye may know that the Son of man hath power upon earth to forgive sins, (he said unto the sick of the palsy,) I say unto thee, Arise, and take up thy couch, and go into thine house.
verses.original_text: ἵνα δὲ εἰδῆτε ὅτι ὁ υἱὸς τοῦ ἀνθρώπου ἐξουσίαν ἔχει ἐπὶ τῆς γῆς ἀφιέναι ἁμαρτίας εἶπεν τῷ παραλελυμένῳ Σοὶ λέγω ἔγειρε καὶ ἄρας τὸ κλινίδιόν σου πορεύου εἰς τὸν οἶκόν σου
verses.transliteration: hina de eidēte hoti ho huios tou anthrōpou exousian echei epi tēs gēs aphienai hamartias eipen tō paralelumenō Soi legō egeire kai aras to klinidion sou poreuou eis ton oikon sou
verses.study_note: 'The Son of man hath power upon earth to forgive sins' (ho huios tou anthrōpou exousian echei epi tēs gēs aphienai hamartias — the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins). 'Son of Man' (huios tou anthrōpou) — Jesus's preferred self-designation, drawn from Daniel 7:13-14 ('one like the Son of man...there was given him dominion, and glory, and a kingdom'). The title is messianic and divine but allows more definitional flexibility than 'Messiah' or 'Christ.' 'On earth' — Jesus does not merely direct the paralytic to God for forgiveness; he himself grants it on earth, in time and space. The healing command ('I say unto thee, Arise') is the seal on the forgiveness — the visible proof of the invisible authority. The paralytic is commanded in three movements: arise, take up, go.

verses.kjv_text: And immediately he rose up before them, and took up that whereon he lay, and departed to his own house, glorifying God.
verses.original_text: καὶ παραχρῆμα ἀναστὰς ἐνώπιον αὐτῶν ἄρας ἐφ᾽ ὃ κατέκειτο ἀπῆλθεν εἰς τὸν οἶκον αὐτοῦ δοξάζων τὸν θεόν
verses.transliteration: kai parachrēma anastas enōpion autōn aras eph ho katekeito apēlthen eis ton oikon autou doxazōn ton theon
verses.study_note: 'Immediately he rose up before them' (parachrēma anastas enōpion autōn — at once, having arisen before them). The healing is instant, complete, and public. 'Before them' (enōpion autōn) — in full view of the scribes and Pharisees who came to observe and assess. 'Took up that whereon he lay' (aras eph ho katekeito — taking up what he was lying upon; the bed/mat he was carried in on, he now carries out). The reversal is total: arrived helpless on a mat carried by four men; departs carrying the mat, walking under his own power. 'Glorifying God' (doxazōn ton theon — praising/glorifying God). The man's response is correct: the miracle moves him to worship the God who has acted, not to elaborate theological argument about the healer's identity. His exit is an act of doxology.

verses.kjv_text: And they were all amazed, and they glorified God, and were filled with fear, saying, We have seen strange things to day.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἔκστασις ἔλαβεν ἅπαντας καὶ ἐδόξαζον τὸν θεόν καὶ ἐπλήσθησαν φόβου λέγοντες ὅτι Εἴδομεν παράδοξα σήμερον
verses.transliteration: kai ekstasis elaben hapantas kai edoxazon ton theon kai eplēsthēsan phobou legontes hoti Eidomen paradoxa sēmeron
verses.study_note: 'Amazement' (ekstasis — ecstasy, standing outside oneself; ek + stasis = standing out/outside; our word 'ecstasy'; the strongest Greek word for astonishment). 'Glorified God' (edoxazon ton theon — they kept glorifying God) — including, apparently, some of the observers. 'Filled with fear' (eplēsthēsan phobou — were filled with fear/awe; the reverential awe of the divine). 'Strange things' (paradoxa — paradoxical things, things contrary to expectation; para + doxa = beyond/against expectation; our word 'paradox'). What they have seen defies all categories: authority over sin (divine), authority over the body (miraculous), knowledge of thoughts (omniscient). The reaction is appropriate: this is a paradoxos day, when the categories of normal reality have been overturned.

verses.kjv_text: And after these things he went forth, and saw a publican, named Levi, sitting at the receipt of custom: and he said unto him, Follow me.
verses.original_text: Καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα ἐξῆλθεν καὶ ἐθεάσατο τελώνην ὀνόματι Λευὶν καθήμενον ἐπὶ τὸ τελώνιον καὶ εἶπεν αὐτῷ Ἀκολούθει μοι
verses.transliteration: Kai meta tauta exēlthen kai etheasato telōnēn onomati Leuin kathēmenon epi to telōnion kai eipen autō Akolouthei moi
verses.study_note: 'A publican, named Levi' (telōnēn onomati Leuin — a tax collector named Levi). Levi = Matthew (the Gospel of Matthew identifies himself as Matthew the tax collector, Matthew 10:3; 9:9). 'Sitting at the receipt of custom' (kathēmenon epi to telōnion — sitting at the tax office; telōnion = tax booth, toll office). Capernaum was on a major trade route; the toll office collected customs on goods passing through. A tax collector (telōnēs) sat at a specific, permanent post — he could not deny or hide his occupation. 'Follow me' (Akolouthei moi — keep following me; present imperative: begin following and continue). The briefest call in the Gospels — two words in the Greek. No explanation, no negotiation, no probationary period. The call is absolute, immediate, and unadorned.

verses.kjv_text: And he left all, rose up, and followed him.
verses.original_text: καὶ καταλιπὼν πάντα ἀναστὰς ἠκολούθησεν αὐτῷ
verses.transliteration: kai katalipōn panta anastas ēkolouthēsen autō
verses.study_note: 'Left all' (katalipōn panta — having abandoned all things). The response mirrors that of the fishermen in v.11 ('forsook all'). 'Rose up' (anastas — having arisen) — the same word used for resurrection; discipleship is a kind of rising from the old life. 'Followed him' (ēkolouthēsen autō — followed him; aorist: the definitive act of following). Levi's renunciation is perhaps more permanent than the fishermen's: fishermen could return to their boats (John 21:3 — Peter says 'I go a fishing' after the resurrection); a tax collector who has abandoned his post and his Roman commission cannot simply reclaim it. Yet Levi leaves 'all' without a recorded word of hesitation or negotiation. The call of Jesus creates its own response in those who hear it as the word of God.

verses.kjv_text: And Levi made him a great feast in his own house: and there was a great company of publicans and of others that sat down with them.
verses.original_text: Καὶ ἐποίησεν δοχὴν μεγάλην Λευὶς αὐτῷ ἐν τῇ οἰκίᾳ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἦν ὄχλος πολὺς τελωνῶν καὶ ἄλλων οἳ ἦσαν μετ᾽ αὐτῶν κατακείμενοι
verses.transliteration: Kai epoiēsen dochēn megalēn Leuis autō en tē oikia autou kai ēn ochlos polys telōnōn kai allōn hoi ēsan met autōn katakeimenoi
verses.study_note: 'A great feast' (dochēn megalēn — a great reception/banquet; dochē = reception, a formal dinner). Levi's first act as a disciple is to throw a party for Jesus — and to invite his professional network: 'a great company of publicans and of others.' The feast is Levi's evangelism: he brings Jesus into his world rather than extracting Jesus from the world. The 'others' (allōn) who sat with them include those the Pharisees will call 'sinners' (v.30) — the socially and religiously marginalised. Levi's banquet becomes a parable of the kingdom: the Messiah at table with the excluded, the despised, the commercially compromised. The table fellowship of Jesus is one of the most theologically significant aspects of his ministry in Luke.

verses.kjv_text: But their scribes and Pharisees murmured against his disciples, saying, Why do ye eat and drink with publicans and sinners?
verses.original_text: καὶ ἐγόγγυζον οἱ Φαρισαῖοι καὶ οἱ γραμματεῖς αὐτῶν πρὸς τοὺς μαθητὰς αὐτοῦ λέγοντες Διὰ τί μετὰ τῶν τελωνῶν καὶ ἁμαρτωλῶν ἐσθίετε καὶ πίνετε
verses.transliteration: kai egongyzon hoi Pharisaioi kai hoi grammateis autōn pros tous mathētas autou legontes Dia ti meta tōn telōnōn kai hamartwōlōn esthiete kai pinete
verses.study_note: 'Murmured' (egongyzon — grumbled, muttered; goggyzō = to grumble, mutter against; the word used of Israel's wilderness murmuring in the LXX, Exodus 16:7-8; Numbers 14:27-29). The Pharisees do not address Jesus directly (yet) but go to his disciples. Table fellowship in the ancient world was a statement of social solidarity and moral equivalence: eating with someone meant accepting them as equals, sharing their social identity. The Pharisees could not eat with tax collectors and sinners without compromising their purity; Jesus's eating with them is therefore a scandalous statement of inclusion. 'Sinners' (hamartōlōn — sinners, people who flagrantly violated the Law; the social category of the publicly disreputable).

verses.kjv_text: And Jesus answering said unto them, They that are whole need not a physician; but they that are sick.
verses.original_text: καὶ ἀποκριθεὶς ὁ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Οὐ χρείαν ἔχουσιν οἱ ὑγιαίνοντες ἰατροῦ ἀλλ᾽ οἱ κακῶς ἔχοντες
verses.transliteration: kai apokritheis ho Iēsous eipen pros autous Ou chreian echousin hoi hygiainontes iatrou all hoi kakōs echontes
verses.study_note: 'They that are whole need not a physician' (ou chreian echousin hoi hygiainontes iatrou — the healthy ones do not have need of a doctor). Jesus' self-designation as a physician (iatros) is rich: (1) it justifies his association with the 'sick' — doctors must go to the sick, not the healthy; (2) it defines his mission in terms of healing and restoration; (3) it challenges the Pharisees' implicit claim to health — they may be self-diagnosed as 'whole' but Jesus will shortly question whether any are truly healthy before God. 'They that are sick' (hoi kakōs echontes — those having it badly, those who are ill). The contrast between hoi hygiainontes (the healthy) and hoi kakōs echontes (the sick) is the fundamental distinction of the kingdom: those who recognise their need receive the Physician.

verses.kjv_text: I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance.
verses.original_text: οὐκ ἐλήλυθα καλέσαι δικαίους ἀλλὰ ἁμαρτωλοὺς εἰς μετάνοιαν
verses.transliteration: ouk elēlytha kalesai dikaious alla hamartōlous eis metanoian
verses.study_note: 'I came not to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance' (ouk elēlytha kalesai dikaious alla hamartōlous eis metanoian). The programmatic mission statement. 'I came' (elēlytha — perfect: I have come and remain; a statement of divine commission and conscious mission). 'The righteous' (dikaious — those who consider themselves righteous; the irony: the Pharisees who consider themselves righteous are precisely those who need the Physician most). 'Sinners' (hamartōlous — sinners, those who have missed the mark). 'To repentance' (eis metanoian — to repentance; the goal of the call). The call is not to confirm sinners in their sin but to call them to metanoia — the complete reorientation of life. Jesus does not despise the Pharisees' desire for righteousness; he questions their diagnosis of who has it and who needs it.

verses.kjv_text: And they said unto him, Why do the disciples of John fast often, and make prayers, and likewise the disciples of the Pharisees; but thine eat and drink?
verses.original_text: Οἱ δὲ εἶπαν πρὸς αὐτόν Διὰ τί οἱ μαθηταὶ Ἰωάννου νηστεύουσιν πυκνὰ καὶ δεήσεις ποιοῦνται ὁμοίως καὶ οἱ τῶν Φαρισαίων οἱ δὲ σοὶ ἐσθίουσιν καὶ πίνουσιν
verses.transliteration: Hoi de eipan pros auton Dia ti hoi mathētai Iōannou nēsteuousin pykna kai deēseis poiountai homoiōs kai hoi tōn Pharisaiōn hoi de soi esthiousin kai pinousin
verses.study_note: 'Why do the disciples of John fast often...but thine eat and drink?' — a second challenge: John's disciples fast (a mark of penitential piety) and the Pharisees fast (twice weekly, Monday and Thursday per tradition); but Jesus's disciples feast. The implicit charge: Jesus's disciples are insufficiently devout, insufficiently serious about sin. The question reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of what Jesus's coming means for the current moment in redemptive history. The answer Jesus gives introduces three parables about the incompatibility of the old and new.

verses.kjv_text: And he said unto them, Can ye make the children of the bridechamber fast, while the bridegroom is with them?
verses.original_text: ὁ δὲ Ἰησοῦς εἶπεν πρὸς αὐτούς Μὴ δύνασθε τοὺς υἱοὺς τοῦ νυμφῶνος ἐν ᾧ ὁ νυμφίος μετ᾽ αὐτῶν ἐστιν ποιῆσαι νηστεύειν
verses.transliteration: ho de Iēsous eipen pros autous Mē dynasthe tous huious tou nymphōnos en hō ho nymphios met autōn estin poiēsai nēsteuein
verses.study_note: 'Children of the bridechamber' (huious tou nymphōnos — sons of the bridal chamber; the wedding attendants/groomsmen who accompany the bridegroom; the ones who share in the joy of the wedding celebration). 'The bridegroom is with them' (ho nymphios met autōn estin — the bridegroom is with them). Jesus identifies himself as the bridegroom — a divine title (Isaiah 62:5: 'as the bridegroom rejoiceth over the bride, so shall thy God rejoice over thee'). The present time of Jesus's ministry is the wedding banquet — fasting is incompatible with wedding feasting. The kingdom of God has a festive character precisely because it is the presence of the divine Bridegroom. Fasting belongs to a time of waiting and mourning; Jesus's disciples are not waiting but celebrating the Bridegroom's arrival.

verses.kjv_text: But the days will come, when the bridegroom shall be taken away from them, and then shall they fast in those days.
verses.original_text: ἐλεύσονται δὲ ἡμέραι καὶ ὅταν ἀπαρθῇ ἀπ᾽ αὐτῶν ὁ νυμφίος τότε νηστεύσουσιν ἐν ἐκείναις ταῖς ἡμέραις
verses.transliteration: eleusontai de hēmerai kai hotan aparthē ap autōn ho nymphios tote nēsteusousin en ekeinais tais hēmerais
verses.study_note: 'The bridegroom shall be taken away' (aparthē ho nymphios — the bridegroom shall be taken/removed; apairō = to take away, lift away; a word that carries overtones of violent removal). This is the first veiled prediction of the Passion in Luke. Jesus foresees his death as the taking away of the bridegroom — and in its wake, the disciples will fast. The fasting of the post-resurrection period (Acts 13:2-3; 14:23; 2 Corinthians 11:27) is the appropriate expression of grief over the absent Bridegroom who has ascended and not yet returned. Christian fasting is eschatological: it expresses longing for the Bridegroom's return, the ultimate wedding banquet (Revelation 19:7-9).

verses.kjv_text: And he spake also a parable unto them; No man putteth a piece of a new garment upon an old; if otherwise, then both the new maketh a rent, and the piece that was taken out of the new agreeth not with the old.
verses.original_text: Ἔλεγεν δὲ καὶ παραβολὴν πρὸς αὐτοὺς ὅτι Οὐδεὶς ἐπίβλημα ἀπὸ ἱματίου καινοῦ σχίσας ἐπιβάλλει ἐπὶ ἱμάτιον παλαιόν εἰ δὲ μήγε καὶ τὸ καινὸν σχίσει καὶ τῷ παλαιῷ οὐ συμφωνήσει τὸ ἐπίβλημα τὸ ἀπὸ τοῦ καινοῦ
verses.transliteration: Elegen de kai parabolēn pros autous hoti Oudeis epiblēma apo himatiou kainou schisas epiballei epi himation palaion ei de mēge kai to kainon schisei kai tō palaiō ou symphōnēsei to epiblēma to apo tou kainou
verses.study_note: 'New garment upon an old' (epiblēma apo himatiou kainou epi himation palaion — a patch from a new garment on an old garment). The parable of the incompatible patches. 'New' (kainos — new in quality, fresh, unprecedented; distinct from neos [new in time]). The kingdom Jesus brings is kainos — qualitatively new, not merely a newer version of the old. Sewing a patch of new cloth onto old cloth creates two problems: (1) the new cloth tears (the patch is ruined); (2) the sizes don't match. The kingdom of God is not a patch on the old religious system; it is a qualitatively new order that cannot be fitted into the forms of the old without destroying both.

verses.kjv_text: And no man putteth new wine into old bottles; else the new wine will burst the bottles, and be spilled, and the bottles shall perish.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐδεὶς βάλλει οἶνον νέον εἰς ἀσκοὺς παλαιούς εἰ δὲ μήγε ῥήξει ὁ νέος οἶνος τοὺς ἀσκούς καὶ αὐτὸς ἐκχυθήσεται καὶ οἱ ἀσκοὶ ἀπολοῦνται
verses.transliteration: kai oudeis ballei oinon neon eis askous palaious ei de mēge rēxei ho neos oinos tous askous kai autos ekchythēsetai kai hoi askoi apolountai
verses.study_note: 'New wine into old bottles' (oinon neon eis askous palaious — new wine into old wineskins; askos = a wineskin, a leather bag made from animal skin). 'New wine' (neon — neos: new in time, fresh, recently made). Freshly pressed grape juice continues fermenting, expanding under the pressure of fermentation gases. Old wineskins have lost their elasticity through previous stretching; they cannot accommodate the expansion of new wine and will burst, losing both wine and wineskin. The kingdom of God (new wine) cannot be contained within the rigid, inflexible forms of the old religious system (old wineskins). Both would be destroyed in the attempt. Jesus brings something so new that it requires new forms, new structures, new patterns of life and worship.

verses.kjv_text: But new wine must be put into new bottles; and both are preserved.
verses.original_text: ἀλλὰ οἶνον νέον εἰς ἀσκοὺς καινοὺς βλητέον καὶ ἀμφότεροι συντηροῦνται
verses.transliteration: alla oinon neon eis askous kainous blēteon kai amphoteroi syntērountai
verses.study_note: 'New wine must be put into new bottles' (oinon neon eis askous kainous — new wine into new wineskins). Note the deliberate variation: the wine is neon (new in time, recently produced) while the wineskins are kainous (new in quality — fresh, elastic, able to expand). The new community of the kingdom requires new structures (the church, new patterns of prayer, new table fellowship) that can accommodate the dynamic, expanding work of the Spirit. 'Both are preserved' (kai amphoteroi syntērountai — both are kept together intact; syntēreō = to keep together, preserve). The right combination produces preservation of both: the new wine (the kingdom reality) and the new wineskins (the appropriate forms). Rigid adherence to old forms destroys both the old and the new.

verses.kjv_text: No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better.
verses.original_text: καὶ οὐδεὶς πιὼν παλαιὸν εὐθέως θέλει νέον λέγει γάρ Ὁ παλαιὸς χρηστός ἐστιν
verses.transliteration: kai oudeis piōn palaion eutheōs thelei neon legei gar Ho palaios chrēstos estin
verses.study_note: 'No man also having drunk old wine straightway desireth new: for he saith, The old is better' (ho palaios chrēstos estin — the old is good/pleasant; chrēstos = useful, pleasant, good). The third parable is the most psychologically astute: the preference for the familiar. Old wine tastes better because the palate is accustomed to it. The Pharisees and John's disciples prefer the old forms (fasting schedules, ritual patterns) because they have been shaped by them. The transition from old to new is psychologically difficult — the new is unfamiliar, demanding, and unsettling. This final parable explains the resistance Jesus has encountered: it is not malice in all cases but the natural human preference for the comfortable and familiar. The kingdom requires a willingness to taste new wine, to allow the familiar categories to be disrupted by the fresh work of God.

meta.overview: Luke 6 presents a pivotal collection of Jesus's teachings and actions that define both his authority and the radical ethic of the kingdom. The chapter opens with two Sabbath controversies (vv.1-11): plucking grain on the Sabbath (vv.1-5) and healing the withered hand in the synagogue (vv.6-11). In both cases, Jesus asserts authority over the Sabbath — 'The Son of man is Lord also of the sabbath' (v.5) — and insists that the Sabbath is fulfilled in doing good and saving life, not in rigid legal observance. After a night of prayer (v.12), Jesus chooses the Twelve Apostles (vv.13-16) — the foundational leadership of the new Israel. The Sermon on the Plain (vv.17-49) is the Lukan parallel to Matthew's Sermon on the Mount, addressed to the whole crowd (not just the disciples) and delivered on level ground. The Beatitudes (vv.20-23) pronounce blessing on the poor, the hungry, the weeping, and the persecuted — and corresponding woes on the rich, full, laughing, and praised. The radical ethic follows: love your enemies (vv.27-36), a section more extensive than its Matthean parallel. The Golden Rule (v.31), the command to lend without expecting return (v.35), and the call to be merciful as the Father is merciful (v.36) characterise the kingdom community. The section on judging (vv.37-42) warns against the hypocrisy of judging others while ignoring one's own faults. The chapter closes with two parables on the importance of doing the word: the good/corrupt tree (vv.43-45) and the two builders — one on the rock, one on the sand (vv.46-49). The sermon's last word is that hearing Jesus without doing his words is suicidal folly, while hearing and doing builds a life that can withstand any storm.
